Your GMB insights tab will tell you where people requested transport instructions from their 
Google Maps to find you. 
 
You can use this data to write a direction instructions section on your website. 
 
Not only will this be helpful from a user experience perspective, but it also gives you the chance 
to write local, relevant content all about the area you are trying to rank in, which will reinforce 
where you are situated from Google’s perspective. 
 
You can talk about the different roads people need to drive on, or the different public transport 
options available to them.  
 
Google understands that other websites in this geographical location are mentioning these 
areas/roads/monuments etc. and will understand that you must also be situated in the same 
region. 
 
By writing directions, we are building good local relevance, as well as enhancing the proximity 
Google thinks we operate in. 
 
We’ll be looking at Google Stacks in the GMB optimization chapter and I would be inclined to 
make a Google Stack for a storefront business. With service area businesses we have the 
benefit of creating small location pages to increase our proximity. These storefront businesses 
can’t pretend they have coffee shops all over a city when they don’t… So in any keyword 
difficulty (KD!) niche, I would be creating a Google Stack and extending my local proximity 
through Google Stacks, directions and so on. 
Service Area Businesses 
Service area businesses are those which customers will not travel to a particular location for a 
product or service. 
 
Instead, the service provider will travel to the customer.  
 
Think tradesmen like roofers, builders, carpenters and painters. 
 
When creating your GMB account, you should select to hide your address as customers won’t 
be coming to you. 
 


 
 
 
As you don’t have a physical location that customers visit like storefront businesses, selecting 
the areas you operate in is particularly important.  
 
To not limit ourselves and make sure we have the opportunity to rank in all of our desired local 
areas, we should list as many of them as possible. 
 
Most other GMB optimizations will remain the same as a storefront business. 
 
We should still build structured NAP citations to the address we use as it will help Google to 
understand where we are geographically, even if customers aren’t going to visit our physical 
location.  
 
Most of the main websites we build citations on (Yell, Yelp, Bing etc.) will allow us to hide our 
address. Then, on directory sites that our customers are unlikely to visit (e.g. aboutus.com), we 
can add a citation to build local relevance and help to rank. 
 
Understanding Proximity For Service Area Businesses 
We can list the service areas the business operates in. However, where we verify our GMB is 
still a huge factor. Google assumes your base is where you verified your GMB, despite selecting 
the areas you serve. 
 
I have a client who ranks in the top 3 positions organically for all his [city] [service] type 
keywords. However, he is nowhere to be seen in the map pack for the exact same searches. 
 
Why? 
 
Well, the GMB was verified outside of the city centre in an area which is 40 minutes away by 
car. 
 
So despite the GMB name, website, content, and all-round strategy being related to that city, 
even ranking in the top 3 organically, the GMB still does not rank in the map pack for those 
specific search terms. 
 
The simple reason is proximity. 
 
Google knows the business is situated 40 minutes away from the centre of the city, so it does 
not want to rank us there, as it rightly assumes we will be taking a 40 minute drive to serve 
customers in that radius. Meanwhile, other businesses are closer and perhaps more suited to 
serve local customers.  
 
 


 
 
From this site, other client sites, my lead generation assets and other online research - ranking 
your GMB far away from where you originally registered it is hard to overcome for service area 
businesses. 
 
Sure, we will have proximity benefits in and around the address we verified our GMB at, but we 
can’t simply verify a GMB an hour away from a busy city, slap our target location as that city and 
just expect to rank there. There are ways to extend our GMB’s proximity (explored later), but our 
proximity eventually becomes too weak the further away we get from our GMB verification 
address.  
 
For very high ticket local businesses (e.g. legal), it could be worth actually moving offices in 
order to verify a GMB in a more populated and wealthy area simply for the GMB benefits. 
 
Although this information seems pointless because some businesses will simply be registered in 
areas that are sparsely populated - it is vital information to know as a Local SEO. Perhaps your 
client has a close family member with a better address they could relocate their GMB to? 
 
Or, if you are making your own lead generation asset, you know how important it is to be 
mindful of where your GMB is first verified. 
 
Fortunately, the organic rankings won’t penalise us like a GMBs registered address will. Like my 
client example above, if we can make our website strong and specific enough to rank for a 
search term, we can do so, despite being 40 minutes away from the city centre. 
Multi-Location 
Multi-location SEO is as it sounds - ranking in multiple locations, often states or cities 
throughout a country.  
 
As usual, our GMBs and websites will rank differently when targeting multiple locations. 
 
Fortunately, we can stick to one website and add multiple location landing pages to a single 
domain so as to not start afresh in every single additional location we want to target. 
 
As you’re probably beginning to understand, the same cannot be said for our GMBs as 
proximity is so important. 
 
You can’t get your GMB in Kansas to also rank in Kentucky. This means we need a new 
GMB for each major location that we target.  
 
Each GMB is its own little lead generation tool. If it’s connected to a high powered, 
multi-location website too? All the better. 
 


 
 
 
Importantly, I am not referring to having two offices in the same small location (unless there 
is a vast amount of searches, you won’t gain much of a benefit)... I am referring to entering 
more markets with more GMBs and dominating more areas locally. We will cover the 
specifics on having multiple GMBs/location pages later on. 
Lead Generation Assets 
Lead generation assets are my favourite strategy in Local SEO.  
 
You create websites and GMBs that you own, rather than working on a client’s SEO. These 
websites and GMBs (your assets) generate leads and you monetize them. 
 
When doing client work, you are trading your service for money. This is fine and works well 
because you can get instant cash flow from a client.  
 
However, lead generation assets are excellent because you own them. You are creating a cash 
flow producing asset which has a resale value. 
 
A client can drop us with a moment's notice. But you own your lead generation assets for as 
long as you decide to.  
 
Client work and lead generation assets have their strengths and weaknesses… Let’s have a 
look at them. 
 
 
The Benefits of Client Work  
 
Instant Cash Flow -​ If we take on a client, they will pay us the day we start working. This 
means you have a cash flow producing business fast, without necessarily creating any value 
yet.  
 
If you want to quit your job or start making money instantly, getting a client to pay you is likely 
the fastest way to make that happen. 
 
Network: ​Having happy clients helps you to gain referrals, reviews, and build your network. 
Happy clients will gladly refer you to their friends. This means more instant cash flow, referrals, 
reviews, and so on.  
 
 
 
 
 
 


 
 
The Negatives of Client Work  
 
Can Drop You At Any Time -​ This means we need to diversify across multiple clients in order 
to have a stable cash flow.  
 
We Aren’t Building An Asset - ​When doing client work, we are trading our time/skills for 
money and not building a long term, lasting asset.  
 
Clients Can Be Difficult - ​This is life. People can be difficult to deal with and mess you 
around/waste your time.  
 
 
The Benefits of Lead Generation Assets 
 
You own the assets you create: ​Nobody can fire you or take them away from you (apart from 
Google, although that won’t happen if you don’t do anything crazy).  
 
You can sell the assets: ​You can sell your lead generation asset for 20-40x monthly net profit. 
If you are making $1,000 net profit a month, that’s an exit of $20,000-$40,000!  
 
They are diverse:​ There are lots of niches to enter and ways to monetize a lead generation 
asset to suit you and your lifestyle.  
 
You design everything:​ With client work, you are often working in imperfect conditions, making 
it tricky to get excellent results. However, you can design your lead generation asset to make it 
as profitable and lasting as possible. You are making the asset purely to get excellent results 
from Local SEO, so you can revolve every decision around that. You don’t have to wait for a 
client, you can just start today. 
 
 
The Negatives of Lead Generation Assets 
 
Not instant cash:​ Even if you create a great GMB that ranks overnight, you will still need to find 
someone to give the leads to and therefore monetize your work. However, client work isn’t 
necessarily instant cash either, as you often can’t click your fingers and summon a brand new 
paying client. Attracting clients often takes a bit of time too.  
 
 
Sometimes they don’t work:​ I’ve made multiple lead generation assets that I miscalculated on, 
leading to a profitless venture. However, if I was optimizing a client’s site, I would have been 
paid. Still, these ‘failures’ are also just learning curves to make profitable lead generation assets 
in the future. 
 
 


 
 
 
Monetizing Lead Generation Assets 
 
There are multiple ways to monetize lead generation assets. Let’s look at them.  
 
Rank and rent - ​This is my favorite strategy. You rank your assets, get them generating 
inquiries, then redirect inquiries to a business for a monthly fee. This strategy is relatively 
hands-free once the site is rented out to a business. However, you will still want to invest in your 
asset anyway through getting links, adding content, and other strategies we will be exploring. 
 
You can even make the rental process really smooth by rebranding your website to appear like 
it is directly associated with your client, while not affecting the NAP - so Google isn’t aware and 
we don’t get penalized. 
 
This is a consistent way of monetizing your lead generation assets. 
 
Pay per lead - ​Pay per lead works well. You can either take the inquiries yourself or have 
inquiries redirect to another business.  
 
You then charge a set fee for every inquiry that is generated. This could be $20 or it could be 
$200, it just depends on the niche. 
 
This is a fast way of being paid, especially if the business will pay you quickly after you deliver a 
lead.  
 
Pay per conversion - ​Some businesses only want to pay you when they make money from 
your lead. They don’t want to pay upfront.  
 
You can then take a % of each conversion they make, which could equate to more than the rank 
and rent or pay per lead model.  
 
The benefit is that you can land larger payments. If you get 10% for every project a client 
completes, that could be anywhere from $10-$1,000+ per converted project.  
 
This strategy requires patience and trust. You need to know your client will pay you for each 
conversion, as they could quite easily deceive you. You also have to wait for a project to 
convert, which takes time in lots of industries. I would avoid this strategy unless you know the 
business owner very well.  
 
 
 
 
 
 


 
 
Getting Clients From Your Lead Generation Websites 
 
A great benefit of the pay per lead/conversion strategy is that you can disperse your leads to 
different business owners.  
 
You take the inquiry (whether email, phone call, or instant message) and then pass this onto 
business owners in that industry. 
 
This allows you to see who wants leads, see who you think is trustworthy, and see who you 
want to build long term business relationships with. 
 
Calling somebody with a qualified lead for their business is completely different from calling 
them and pitching SEO services. Suddenly, they are trying to sell you over the phone! It’s a 
powerful position to be in. 
 
Giving out leads is also a great way to prove your skills and land client work. If you give a 
business owner multiple leads which he converts, you have simultaneously made them money 
and proved your SEO strategy works. He will gladly hand over some of that money for you to 
work on the SEO for his business too. 
 
Overall, I think it’s a good idea to diversify between the strategies. I mostly have rank and rent 
assets (low hassle) but also have some pay per conversion sites handy in case I want to find 
new clients and land a big % from a conversion.  
 
Lead Generation Mindset 
Many people get confused about the lead generation strategy.  
 
They say things like: 
 
“How could I possibly own a painting website when I’m not a painter?!”  
 
But just because you are not a skilled painter, it does not mean you cannot start a painting 
business, generate inquiries through your Local SEO skills and get contractors to fulfill the work 
for you. 
 
You do not have to do all things within a business to start one.  
 
You have as much right to own a painting business as the guy who has been a painter for five 
years. The difference is that you know how to manipulate Google to get painting customers to 
find you and they don’t!  
 
Ultimately, many people will not hire you to do their SEO.  
 


 
 
 
They don’t understand it, they don’t want to pay for future results and they don’t trust you. 
 
So, we can simply start our own businesses/lead generation assets and compete with them. 
 
It could be seen that we are “taking leads from painters” - but business is war. We have as 
much right to “their” leads (the leads aren’t theirs) as they do.  
 
So the mindset you have to have when creating lead generation assets is that you are simply 
starting a business. You do not have to be an expert, nor qualified in order to start a business 
there, or even make a lot of money from it. 
 
You should have no guilt about starting any business just because you are not delivering the 
work. You are creating the work, which is extremely valuable. 
 
After all, sales are the lifeline of any business. You are simply creating an excellent sales 
system for a type of local business that you want to own. That’s perfectly acceptable.  
Understanding Keywords in Local SEO 
 
Whether we are doing client work or our own lead generation assets, keyword research is still a 
major part of any Local SEO campaign.  
 
Without the right keyword strategy, we are shooting in the dark and won’t create a successful 
SEO venture. 
 
When doing local client SEO, you are often limited to a certain amount of services they provide, 
in the areas they operate in.  
 
This means we need strategies to identify the best keywords in any given area. 
 
And for lead generation assets, we need to know how to do keyword research so we can scour 
through different niches/locations and find a goldmine to take over. 
 
How to do local keyword research  
Local keyword research isn’t rocket science.  
 
Let’s look at the most popular type of local keywords for most niches. 
 
Then let’s uncover exactly how to find the best keywords in any given niche. 
 
 


 
 
SL Keywords 
SL = [Service location]. 
 
SL keywords are probably the most common types of keywords across all local niches. So it’s 
worth dedicating time to understand more about SL keywords and how they work... 
 
Example: Roofers Edinburgh. 
 
Roofers Edinburgh is simply: [service] [location]. 
 
Some local SEO blogs talk about ‘SiL’ keywords (service in location)... 
 
But with all of mine and my client’s traffic, a search term like ‘roofers Cincinnati’ (SL) is usually 
more popular than a search term like ‘roofers in Cincinnati’ (but the SiL variation is normally in 
the top 10 for most searched keywords)...  
 
So we’ll call these valuable search terms SLs instead of SiLs for now. 
 
SLs make sense. If you lived in Birmingham and wanted a painter, what would you search? 
Probably ‘painter Birmingham’. 
 
Customers will also make more specific SL searches, like ‘roof repair Cincinnati, specifying a 
particular service they want as well as their location. 
 
Another common search term is “[service] + near me” 
 
“Coffee shop near me”, “barbers near me”, “painters near me” and so on. Again, it makes sense, 
because customers are looking for a business in their local area that will give them a good, fast 
experience. 
 
Then another keyword variation I see across most niches is “local + [service]”, like local 
cleaners or local dentists. 
 
Another common search term among most niches is random variations of SL keywords.  
 
Think “Cincinnati roofer”, “roofing Cincinnati” and so on. 
 
You can disperse these SL keyword variations throughout your site, Google entities, anchor 
texts, etc. to rank for the different SL keywords customers inevitably use (Edinburgh roofers, 
roofing Edinburgh, roofer Edinburgh, Edinburgh roofing, etc.) 
 
 


 
 
Here’s a cool free tool that auto-generates popular variations of [service] [location] keywords so 
you don’t have to manually think of them! 
https://www.highervisibility.com/free-seo-tools/bulk-keyword-generator/ 
 
However, we will mostly focus our OnPage strategy on the variations of SL keywords that get 
the most traffic. Generally, in each niche, there will be a few SL variations that are more popular 
than others.  
 
But it’s worth including other SL keyword variations throughout our website, Google entities, and 
anchor texts to help us rank for the other variations that collect some clicks too. 
 
Dominating the most popular search term (e.g. roofers Cincinnati) will ensure we have good 
ranking across most SL keywords, but it’s just good practice to involve as many of these 
keywords which are generating valuable traffic as possible. 
 
SL Keywords Hold Intent  
Searching something like ‘attorney Manchester’ holds a lot of intent. This search doesn’t 
represent someone researching, studying, or surfing for entertainment.  
 
No, it assumes you want to find a legal professional in that location. 
 
What do legal professionals do in any given location?  
 
They charge people that go to them! And make lots of money at that.  
 
So although our local SL keywords look ‘basic’ because they aren’t as blatant as a search term 
like “buy a football” - they still have a lot of buyer intent behind them.  
 
Searching for a service in a location is very problem/solution-oriented, meaning there’s a lot of 
buyer intent behind the search. 
 
Research Tools  
Okay, so we already know our local service is likely going to have lots of SL, local and near me 
keywords, let’s validate this notion and find other keywords we want to target. 
 
Ahrefs  
Ahrefs is going to be one of your best friends conducting local keyword research. Although this 
is a paid tool, it is definitely worth it. You can get a trial for $7 and if you’re making your first lead 
generation asset or you have your first client, I highly recommend getting the trial and 
conducting some keyword research on here, then canceling if you’re really short on cash. Or, 
you can go for the SEMrush 30-day free trial, which has similar functions. 
 


 
 
 
There are other free alternatives which we will look at, like Google Keyword Planner, but Ahrefs 
gives us really detailed insights. 
 
Ahrefs Keyword Explorer -​ You can use Keywords Explorer to research your niche and find 
the keywords with the most traffic. There are other metrics too, like KD (keyword difficulty), 
which we will analyze soon!  
 
Search Suggestions:​ Next, you can use the search suggestions tool within Keywords Explorer 
to find more keywords. 
 
 
 
Next, type in services/products/locations, etc. related to your niche to find more and more 
keywords that might contain traffic. Do this until you exhaust everything you can think of. We are 
trying to find as many keywords as possible with visible search traffic. 
 
This is the basis of how we understand how much traffic there is for a niche in a given location. 
There are more factors to consider, but this is a solid starting point. Between our SL keywords, 
service keywords, suggestions, and whatever else we found - we should have a solid starting 
point for what keywords have search volume for the niche. 
 
It’s worth mentioning now that not every single local search gets picked up by Ahrefs or virtually 
any data tracker out there. Sometimes you will obscurely get little to no searches for something 
which surely gets typed into Google. 
This could be because there actually IS no search volume… Or, there might just be a small 
number of searches/the searches aren’t being recorded. I have multiple local clients where 
 


 
 
there is supposedly a tiny amount of search volume, but the client still gets plenty of phone 
calls. 
 
So, we still need to be able to account for these missing searches in order to identify which 
niches are worth entering. You don’t want to offer Local SEO to a client who can’t gain anything 
from it, nor do you want to create a lead generation asset in a niche like this. 
 
Starting a project to rank for 0 volume keywords seems pretty silly if we don’t have any other 
accompanying data. 
 
However, if we know there are some searches in that niche and location, perhaps it’s a project 
worth pursuing. 
 
Another consideration is these really low search volume areas will also have very little supply of 
competition and will also be very easy to rank in.  
 
Use A Large City 
This is how we tackle “0 keyword search volume”. 
 
We take a location with a larger population, where it’s easier to record keyword search volume 
and compare it to our location with this formula:  
 
(Population of target town / Population of the larger city) * search volume of the larger city. 
 
Example: Let’s get a rough estimate of search volume for “cleaner Oxford” using the knowledge 
that “cleaner London” gets roughly 1400 searches  
(650,000 / 8,100,000) * 1400 = ​120 
Not horribly far off - the actual search volume is 60. 
  
Obviously this is just an estimate, but it helps when a client is in a low keyword volume area to 
uncover how much search volume there could be there. Seeing 0 search volume can often be 
deceiving as there are valuable keywords to be getting in front of after all. 
 
When researching a niche, I would always use the larger city method. Again, I have multiple 
clients and lead generation assets where there was seemingly no traffic for the niche, but there 
actually is. 
 
Even keywords with a local search volume of 10+ per month (discovered using the London 
technique) are going to be valuable, especially if the niche is uncompetitive. 
 


 
 
 
So, definitely don’t think there is no opportunity in a niche just because it looks bad at first 
glance.  
 
How Do Searches Change For GMBs? 
These are the different kind of keyword types our GMB will appear for: 
 
[Keyword] 
If you simply type carpenter into Google right now, Google will show you the top carpenters 
who are most proximate to you. 
This goes for any local search term. Coffee shop, library, electrician, doctor, bar, etc. 
 
 
 


 
 
Google will generate a map pack. Relevant and prominent GMBs near us will appear. The 
power of the GMB!  
Clearly, this is another stream of traffic for us all together - and something Ahrefs can’t quantify. 
This is either because there aren’t enough searches, or it struggles to calculate all keyword 
volume, or simply because people are making these local searches and not clicking on a 
website.  
Imagine this scenario. Someone searches ​“doctor near me” ​from their phone, sees a good GMB 
listing, calls them straight from the map pack number, and never visits the website. Ahrefs then 
doesn’t calculate any website traffic.  
 
We only have to compare GMB insights to Ahrefs predicted traffic to see how Ahrefs and similar 
won’t pick up these localized searches.  
 
 
 
Here’s an image of a client’s GMB insights. As you can see, there are plenty of non-location 
searches. The black boxes are the location the client serves. So those SL keywords are most 
 


 
 
frequent, but there are still plenty of localized searches too. None of these are calculated by 
Ahrefs.  
 
This means an element of common sense should be used when deciding if a keyword will have 
any volume. Let’s imagine you are looking at a town with a 10,000 population. There are four 
nearby towns all with a 10,000 population too. Ahrefs and other keyword planners say there is 
no electrician related searches for any of the areas. However, I’d be certain there would be 
variations of ​electrician, electrician near me, local electrician, fix my lights, home electrician, 
commercial electrician, ​etc. 
 
Thankfully, when considering working with a client, we can get access to their GMB (if they even 
have one!) and get a feel for the impressions they’re getting against their ranking position to see 
if there is potential in their location.  
 
So although these localized keywords are harder to calculate/find out - they will be there if your 
niche is an essential service - just how there are localized bathroom/kitchen fitting searches in 
the example above. 
 
The higher the population is, the higher chance there are localized searches, especially for 
essential local businesses. 
 
I use a rough estimation strategy to calculate how many localized searches there will be in a 
niche. I generally add 20% to my overall total keyword volume to account for them. 
 
So let’s say between all of the keywords we’re looking at in a particular niche, we have a total 
search volume of 286. I would add a further 20% to account for the localized words, giving us a 
total volume of 343. The actual figure in my experience is more like 30%+, but it’s better to see if 
the niche works without being overly ambitious and optimistic. 
 
This is another kind of search we will want to optimize for: 
 
[​Keyword] + City + State Abbreviation 
It’s not uncommon for multiple locations around the world to have the same name, especially in 
the States. It helps to optimize your GMB so Google understands your actual proximate 
location. Google understanding where we actually are is going to make it easier for them to rank 
us for non-location keywords (‘​carpenter’, not ‘carpenter [location]’​) like we just looked at. 
 
Competitor analysis  
We want to see which keywords the best competitors are ranking for and the kind of traffic 
they’re gaining. 
 
 


 
 
Again, this is where Ahrefs or a similar tool is pretty necessary. 
 
We can assess whether we want to rank for keywords in this niche or location based on how the 
competitor websites are doing. If the top competitors in an area have really, really low website 
traffic (less than 50), they probably don’t get a whole lot of business through their website. 
 
Not only that, but looking at the competition’s keywords gives us more keyword ideas and long 
tail keywords we didn’t find in our initial keyword explorer exercise. 
 
Another trick we can use with Ahrefs is through the content gap tool. First go to site explorer, 
then to content gap. Paste in the main competitor URLs while leaving the “at least one of the 
targets should rank in the top 10” box checked and click “show keywords”. This shows us the 
common keywords among the top competitors for even more ideas.  
 
You can even do a content gap analysis on businesses in larger/more competitive areas and 
find long tail keywords that your competitors missed. If London residents wanted a ‘thatched 
roof repair’, you can bet some percentage of people in your local areas will want them too - 
even if there are ‘no searches’. Even if there are no searches, we just use the larger city 
calculation to see how much volume there could be in our smaller area. 
 
This allows us to see most of what our competitors are ranking for, as well as extra keywords 
they’ve missed. Great.  
 
This competitor analysis isn’t all-inclusive though, as we have to consider local GMB traffic we 
can’t totally calculate. But it’s a good indication of how the current competitors are doing within 
the niche and if they have enough traffic that it could be making them money.  
 
Remember, don’t be scared because we aren’t looking at 500+ volume keywords. 
 
More Ways To Measure Keyword Volume And Quality 
Let’s look at more ways to analyze local keyword volumes. 
We’re diving deep into this topic because keywords are the bloodline of what we are doing. 
Ranking for garbage keywords = garbage SEO campaign. We want to rank for lots of good 
keywords that generate us or a client good money.  
Google Ads -​ We can spend a couple of hundred dollars on Google Ads testing our niche to 
see what kind of clicks we get.  
After running a campaign with a broad match modifier, go to “Keywords > Search Terms” to see 
what customers actually typed into Google to find your ad. It’s usually some variation of the 
 


 
 
keywords you selected to run your broad ads for. But this allows us to see the exact phrase they 
used and give us some intel. 
Look at where people made their searches from: “Reports > Predefined reports (Dimensions) > 
Locations > User locations”.  
This would be a strategy you use on an existing site, or you could make a basic landing page 
and ads campaign to test a niche before fully committing to it. 
Google autocomplete - ​Google gives you suggestions for the keywords you type into the 
search engine. These are words related to your keyword they deem to be valuable and 
generate searches for people already. 
Google autocomplete is mostly used to find long tail keywords to rank for, or to add as H2s or 
just generally disperse throughout your content to make it relevant to the keywords you’re trying 
to rank for.  
This is the free alternative to Ahrefs’ Keyword Search Suggestion Report. The Search 
Suggestion Report scrapes Google autocomplete suggestions for you so you don’t have to do it 
manually. 
 
 
 
Test and improve - ​If your site is already live, look at your impressions for different keywords 
on Google Search Console and see if any relevant keywords are getting impressions. If they 
are, it could be worth trying to rank higher for those keywords. Remember to add your target 
country to the search criteria. I find that new sites are always picking up impressions and clicks 
from abroad, which are probably just spambots. 
 
You can also check your GMB insights tab. Your GMB might appear in the map pack for some 
keywords (therefore telling you there is traffic), but your website is nowhere to be seen in the 
organic listings for that keyword. If you get good traffic for a keyword in your GMB insights, 
target that keyword for your organic strategy too (and vice versa). 
 
 


 
 
Local Long Tail Keywords 
When compiling all of our potential keywords, it’s important to think about long tail keywords as 
well as the obvious SL keywords.  
Optimizing our website and GMB for local long tail keywords is going to be essential for our 
GMB strategy. 
 
Relevancy trumping proximity - ​We can rank in the map pack in areas all over our target 
location/state/city, etc. due to relevancy, despite not necessarily being the closest business to 
the searcher… Because we are so optimized and relevant to that long tail keyword, and 
perhaps our competition isn't mentioning those phrases/services or whatever the long tail 
keywords might be about. This allows us to have a wider radius throughout the city/locations we 
are targeting while not being as close as the competition to the person that made the Google 
search. 
 
Long tail keywords have considerably less competition - ​Long tail keywords are worth 
pursuing because we can find very uncontested local keywords to collect traffic from. The fewer 
websites we are competing against, the less high domain authority/relevant websites we have to 
beat to dominate that keyword.  
 
Although we will generally have less long tail keyword options as a global affiliate site, there will 
still be opportunities to dig out in most local niches.  
 
The competition mostly optimizes words SL keywords​ - This means there are more sites to 
overtake for SL keywords. However, a lot of local competition doesn’t optimize for long tail 
keywords, leaving the door wide open for you to scoop extra traffic each month.  
 
We can make use of extras as H2 -​ If you have a large list of long tail keywords you think 
could generate some valuable local traffic, we can disperse these keywords as H2s throughout 
pages that target other more promising long tail keywords. There’s a chance you can still rank 
by having a long tail keyword as an H2 with some accompanying text because the competition 
is so weak. 
 
Local Keyword Golden Ratio 
KGR stands for Keyword Golden Ratio. 
The KGR is a formula: 
KGR = allintitle:search term Result volume / local search volume (<250) 
If our answer is lower than 0.25, it is easy to rank for this keyword.  
 


 
 
We look at keywords with a max volume of 250 searches. Obviously, we want to go for a 
localized search term that our client or lead generation asset can profitably offer as a service. 
An Allintitle: search will simply generate every website using your keywords within a page title. 
The websites generated in the search are your competition for this keyword. The more there 
are, the harder it’s going to be to compete in the niche.  
The reason we look for page title names is simple. If the topic isn’t mentioned in the page name, 
the page can only be partially about that topic, at best. You won’t rank for local long tail 
keywords like ​“commercial duct cleaning Richmond” ​with a page title like ​“kitchen cleaner 
Virginia”​.  
Page titles matter as Google would rather rank a page that is all about the topic rather than a 
name unrelated that might have some information about it. 
The KGR is designed to show us where we can easily rank. 
But the other factor is speed. If a keyword falls within the <0.25 range, you can also bet that it 
will be fast to rank. As SEO is generally ‘slow’, we need to get as many fast wins as possible. 
The KGR was created from an affiliate site perspective where they are having to account for 
higher amounts of traffic for conversion. Remember, our local keywords have a lot more buyer 
intent behind them.  
Also, Ahrefs and other keyword trackers only create estimates of search traffic, as well as miss 
some %, so we can be a bit more lenient towards our number of searches.  
We can normally find a lot of keyword ideas by looking at keyword volumes in these large cities 
where there are niche searches. The example I gave before was “thatched roof repair”. I would 
use the large city formula to determine the estimated traffic for my smaller location, then see if 
the keyword fell within the KGR. 
I would even be looking at 0 volume keywords that have potential and simply analyze how many 
competitor pages there were, how powerful they are, and how quickly we might rank for the 
keyword.  
How Do We Rank For KGRs? 
Just write good content! 
Just kidding. This phrase doesn’t really tell us a lot about ranking for a keyword. It’s just an 
industry term that has become popular, but it doesn’t tell us what to do.  
To really outrank the competition, we have to do what they’re doing - but better. “Good content” 
will occur as a result of doing exactly that.  
To help Google understand what we are trying to do (rank for our target KGR keyword), we 
should be somewhat like the competition they currently rank in the top 5. 
 


 
 
Google already understands that these top 5 websites are the most relevant to that search term. 
So by being similar and improving on them in terms of: 
- Keyword density 
- Volume of content 
- H Tag structure 
- NAP citations 
- Data points relevant to your keyword/location 
- Internal link structure to the page 
- And more 
We will go into more detail on how to rank specific pages in the OnPage section, but it’s nice to 
touch on things multiple times to build familiarity with key concepts. 
If we can outperform our competitors on these low competition search terms, we stand in good 
stead to rank for them, and quickly.  
Even those of you looking to target hard niches with competitors who have high Domain Ratings 
(DR), you can dominate a long tail keyword if you create a strong page and include some of the 
other points mentioned above. Relevancy is a big factor and if you have a really specific 
strategy focused on dominating these long tail terms, you will be more relevant than even sites 
with 50+ DR (strong domain).
 
Although the majority of our traffic is likely to come from SL keywords, near me keywords and 
similar - it’s still worth adding KGR keywords to your strategy and planning pages for what look 
like profitable KGR keywords and long tail keyword opportunities. 
Formulating A Keyword Spreadsheet  
When you research a niche you want to tackle, be it a client or your own lead generation asset, 
you’ll want to compile all your target keywords into a good old fashioned spreadsheet. By 
compiling our keywords into one place, we can see our entire strategy from one place at all 
times! 
 
From here, we can decide which keywords should rank for which pages. 
 
Generally, we set up our homepage to rank for SL keywords, then service pages rank for other 
main SL/service keywords like roof repair + location, or kitchen cleaning + location. 
 
Then you can add page ideas based on other keywords you want to rank for, like long tail 
keywords.  
 


 
 
 
You can also estimate how many local searches you’ll get for keywords like ​vet near me ​or ​local 
vet​ ​or ​vets. 
 
Then you can write down ‘supporting’ page ideas that will link to your ranking pages. 
 
You’re essentially planning your keywords, site architecture, and content strategy all in one go, 
while simultaneously double checking this is the right client or lead generation asset for you. 
 
Don’t skip this stage! This is your blueprint for success. 
 
If you get really busy and can’t give attention to that project - you’ll have forgotten all your 
research when you come back to it.  
 
However, if you have a spreadsheet telling you which keywords you’re targeting, what the KD of 
the keywords are, which local keywords you’re optimizing for, what your potential total keyword 
volume is, which long tail keywords you still have to target, which nearby local areas you still 
want to create location pages for, what supporting content and blog articles you want to write - 
you will be instantly reminded of what you need to do! 
 
You can update your spreadsheet as you gain more understanding of your niche. Perhaps 
you’ve seen impressions on webmasters for some keywords with potential, or perhaps your 
GMB insights will show you that there are keywords worth pursuing on the organic side too. 
Do I Have Enough Keyword Volume To Start?! 
Sometimes you can look at the keywords you’ve amassed, you’ve analyzed the competition 
which looks beatable, and you think… Is this estimate of keywords I’ll get going to be worth it?! 
Can I get my client an increase in revenue? Is it worth me making a lead generation asset in this 
niche? 
In Local SEO, it can be particularly daunting promising a client results or starting your own site 
when you aren’t looking at thousands and thousands of search volumes between your 
keywords. 
Let’s remember these factors when assessing if we will amass enough traffic to make a 
profitable venture:  
0 search volume search terms​ - Lots of cities, towns, and villages have 0 search volume for 
certain keywords, simply because the data is not being recorded. So, we use the formula 
described already: 
(Population of target town / Population of the larger city) * search volume for the larger city. 
 


 
 
If there are searches for something in a larger city, the same demands will be in your location, 
even if it has a smaller population.  
Ranking in nearby villages, towns, and cities​ - This is a big one. We should not only rank for 
city or statewide search terms.  
You can be more granular, adding nearby location pages for areas within or near our primary 
city/state to pick up even more local clicks.  
Targeting these localized long tail keywords and location pages within or near our primary 
location will also broaden the reach of our GMB and allow us to rank in more map packs for 
more keywords and more locations.  
You can also expand your local sites to venture into other states/cities if your client travels that 
far or if you want your lead generation asset to operate in another area.  
All these little locations with 10,000+ people in them will have searches within them, but the 
search volume won’t necessarily be picked up by Ahrefs and other tracking software. 
Searches are intent-based - ​Affiliate SEOs make websites trying to rank for tons of keywords 
with endless keyword volumes. We are actually more fortunate in Local SEO in that most 
searches for a local business are trying to take action with that search. Lots of affiliate keywords 
are simply people surfing the web for fun, meaning there’s a lot less intent behind their 
keywords. 
Whether customers want opening times, to book an appointment or travel directions, each click 
we get is far more valuable than the average click an SEO gets. This means we need less 
expected traffic in order to make our clients or ourselves money. Seriously. I have lead 
generation assets where there are two main keywords that get volume, totaling about 180 clicks 
a month supposedly. However, I’m getting a valuable lead almost every day from the site.  
Making sure we accounted for all possible clicks - ​While we can’t account for every click we 
might get, don’t forget about searches that don’t include the location. Some people simply turn 
to Google and search for something like “mechanic” or “accountant”. On Ahrefs, we can see 
keywords the competitors in our niche are gaining traffic from. It’s common that these 
non-location search terms get a decent amount of clicks and people forget about them. Add 
these to your spreadsheet.  
My experience is that essential services will always be searched no matter where people are, as 
long as you are ranking high and covering multiple local locations. People need these services 
and a good percentage turn to Google.  
 
Let’s use an example of an accountant. You’re in a location with 60,000 people. If you manage 
to rank in top 3 organic spots for the best keywords, as well as multiple map packs throughout 
multiple locations - you will get inquiries, guaranteed. People need accountants, not everyone 
 


 
 
knows an accountant and some want to find their own. If you appear there when they search for 
one, you will get inquiries.  
 
It can become obscure when you venture into markets that are less essential. I recently had a 
client ask me to make a bowls pitch creation website. You call my client if you want a temporary 
bowls pitch set up... The search volume doesn’t look great and it’s certainly not an essential 
service. I would be more hesitant about setting that up anywhere in the world vs. an essential 
service in any random US city and sublocations relevant to that city. 
Analyzing Keyword Difficulty 
I already mentioned in the introduction how a lot of the Local SEO landscape is uncontested.  
The beauty of Local SEO is our ability to beat the weak competition and dominate a niche 
without much technical know-how, heavy link investing, or year-long waits. 
Also, our GMB will rank quickly for searches near to where it is verified, which always has the 
chance to land fast results.  
Then we can target service-specific keywords, long tail keywords, local-specific keywords, KGR 
keywords, etc., which might also generate fast leads. 
Let’s have a look at some different keyword difficulties and how long it would take to dominate 
some of these niches.  
It has to be overstated here these are simply estimates that can change a lot from niche to 
niche, and location to location.  
However, it’s always reassuring to at least have some kind of indication of what to expect.  
We’ll use the keyword difficulty (KD) metric on Ahrefs, which is somewhat similar to most 
keyword difficulty tools out there. It assumes that 100 is the hardest keyword to rank for and 0 
should be easy peasy. 
Easy ​- The main keywords we want to rank for have a KD of 0-15 
Any keywords that fall within a 0-15 KD will be pretty easy to rank for. Usually, within this 0-15 
KD range, your most searched SL keywords will have a higher KD than other variations, like 
service search terms (boiler repair + location instead of plumbers + location).  
So if your main SL keywords have a 5-15 KD, you can bet that loads of relatively similar volume 
keywords will be in the 0-10 range, which will be even easier to rank for. 
This is where I think Local SEO is crazy. 
There are thousands and thousands of niches with main KL keywords in a niche having a KD of 
5-10... Meaning even the long tail/other keywords in that niche are even lower!  
 


 
 
Businesses are pulling thousands and thousands out of the market with websites competing in 5 
KD keyword difficulty niches... For their most profitable search term! 
It’s not uncommon for local businesses to make 10-50% of their yearly sales through a website, 
either.  
Other than repeat business and referrals - most local businesses simply make sales through 
Google.  
So to reiterate, people are making thousands and thousands a month with their website/GMB by 
winning in niches where most of their keywords have KDs of 5… 
Easy niches are a gold mine for us. It means easily dominating any clients’ competition, a low 
barrier for entry for us as SEOs to help them (competition is weak/making so many mistakes) 
and presents a delightful opportunity to dominate local markets with our own lead generation 
assets. 
By the end of this book, there’s no doubt you will be able to smash easy KD niches in Local 
SEO for either client work or your own lead generation assets. It’s crazy to me the SEO 
competition online is still this weak considering how much local businesses make each year. 
Even your average tradesman will make 6 figures for any kind of construction work, but the KD 
in his niche for the hardest terms where he makes 50% of that is 6/100. Most have barely even 
filled out their GMBs… 
Anyway, you only need a basic strategy to dominate niches/locations within the easy range. It 
just requires you to get all the basics right, because the competition is usually slipping up in 
some way.  
- Mobile optimized, fast loading speed website 
- Fully optimized GMB 
- Good site architecture, internal linking, H tag structure, and on-page content 
- Consistent citations on local directories 
Simply doing the basics without investing in backlinks or building PBNs will see us dominate 
most of the keywords in a niche. I have dominated loads of local easy niches without 
building/buying links (just using local directories) or spending much money on the site.  
Heck, when I started, I was accidentally ranking top for local niches without a clue what I was 
doing.  
I would still add a weekly or biweekly blog article, which gives us the opportunity to add more 
internal linking, citations, and relevant content to our niche. 
 


 
 
This could be overkill, but it’s worth doing to make sure you are able to rank for all the largest 
keywords within that niche. Also, content writing isn’t particularly difficult, nor is it expensive if 
you have money to outsource.  
 
Expected Time To Rank 
Expect to get GMB leads quickly, especially if registered in a good location. Potentially rank for 
KGR keywords within a couple of weeks and even dominate the main SL keywords in a few 
months. My experience is we will be partially sandboxed with brand new domains for the first 
few months. However, using expired domains, we can rank quickly should you do all the basics 
right. For beginners, I would recommend using a fresh domain until you have built up more core 
skills.  
I’ve also seen other Local SEOs skip through the sandbox and rank really quickly, even for SL 
keywords. Still, this is a marathon and not a sprint. As long as we are doing the fundamentals 
correctly, we will easily rank in these easy KD niches without too much time.  
Also, working with a client who has an aged domain can be extremely fast getting onto some 
page 1s. This is especially true when they are on lots of page 2s or just missing out on the map 
pack. They are there with virtually no optimization, and you are coming in and delivering a full 
optimization, unlike the competition. Things like adding the main location you are trying to rank 
for within their GMB name can have huge effects overnight.  
As a beginner, I would recommend you go for easy KD niches to rank lead generation assets in. 
You can also take on clients in easy KD niches and add a huge amount of value. The reason 
lead generation assets are so easy to make in easy KD niches is because clients we can serve 
don’t have time or aren’t interested in working on their Local SEO. This is why the competition is 
so weak. Not many SEOs are operating in easy KD niches. This allows us to charge clients a 
monthly fee and add huge amounts of value by just employing the basics on their website and 
GMB. Optimize their GMB name, fill out the GMB, add a blog, encourage them to get reviews, 
fix their OnPage, speed up their site, add some pages, etc.  
Medium -​ When your niche has a lot of 15-30 KD keywords, we are stepping up a level. I would 
expect 15-30 KD keywords to be white-collar businesses, like dentists, vets, and lawyers. 
We will need to do everything mentioned in the easy KW difficulty range, but will need to collect 
good local links too. Our competition will have lots of quality links, as well as regular content 
updates, good internal link structure, and so on. 
We won’t be able to dominate them as easily as we can the 0-15 KD range - where they are 
making glaringly obvious mistakes in their strategy. 
 


 
 
This level of Local SEO gets harder because we are more likely to be competing with SEO 
agencies. Medium level businesses (dentists etc.) can afford to pay a monthly retainer for an 
SEO company, so the competition gets harder.  
 
Expected Time To Rank 
Dominating a niche like this from a fresh domain would take at least 6 months. Again, it’s going 
to depend on you, how much you invest into the site and the niche itself, but that is my estimate. 
Even 6-9 months might be more realistic.  
Again, we can use expired domains to try and speed up this process if you are comfortable with 
doing so. For any asset I want to make, I prefer a fresh domain to protect longevity - as that is 
where a lot of value is realized in lead generation assets. If you have a client who wants to start 
in this niche, you should also put them on a fresh domain as it would be a catastrophe to get 
delisted or penalized for using an aged domain. That’s a hard one to explain to a client, 
especially if they are not well versed in SEO or digital marketing! 
Thankfully, these niches will still have lots of 0-15 KD keywords we can rank for, as well as GMB 
leads we can pick up through proximity. There’s opportunities in every market to steal long tail 
keywords and to get your GMB ranking, so I wouldn’t rule it out altogether if you are relatively 
new to SEO. 
If working with a client who already has good DR, lots of content, etc., we might be able to 
quickly boost rankings just like we did with easy KD niches. We probably won’t see as drastic 
changes as in the easy KD niches, but we can still make fast improvements if our client has an 
aged domain and some easy problems to fix. 
Although each case will be different, I think beginners can get involved in medium KD niches 
too. I would not recommend starting afresh in these niches for a client or your own site. 
However, if you see glaring mistakes and obvious room for improvement, you could assist a 
client who is already doing OK in the SERPs and get a higher fee than a really small local 
business can pay you. 
Hard ​- When your niche has a lot of 30+ KD keywords, we are in the hard territory. I would 
expect businesses like legal, medical, digital marketing, etc. - but in expensive locations, like 
capital cities or popular states like New York or Los Angeles.  
For hard KD keywords, we will need to do at least everything mentioned in the medium KD 
keyword section. 
For our GMB, we will need to have more proximity, prominence, and relevance. For our website, 
we will need more high-quality links to ranking pages and build our domain rating (DR) to 
compete with these stronger websites, as well as boost up individual pages to outrank the 
competition. 
 


 
 
Now we are definitely competing with SEOs and out-competing everyone isn’t going to be easy. 
But it’s still possible. 
 
Expected Time To Rank 
From a fresh domain, I would anticipate it taking at least 12 months to rank highly in a really 
hard niche. Again, some SEOs might be able to do it faster, some might take longer, but the 12 
months milestone is to signify it won’t be a fast turn around. 
The competition will simply have too strong of a DR, good trust signals, solid fundamental  SEO 
best practices that we can’t easily capitalize on any mistakes (usually). 
As ever, we have the benefits of having KGR long tail keywords and our GMB to pull in fast 
leads, but dominating the SL keywords in hard niches will not be fast.  
However, this isn’t necessarily a bad thing. Clients within niches like this will have more money 
and if you create a lead generation asset in a valuable niche like this, there could be a lot of 
money made.  
Another consideration is these high-value niches (law in popular locations) have sub-niches you 
can rank for. 
For example, law is broken up into multiple divisions. While there are extremely high DR 
competitors in law, not all of the legal practices can cover all of the sub-niches sufficiently. 
Perhaps their RD (root domain) has a high DR, but a specific service page within has no links to 
it. 
This leaves a gap in the market for you to make a niche-specific website for a particular legal 
service. You can revolve your entire strategy around ranking for that legal service, while the big 
powerful legal websites are trying to rank for a lot of different services and search terms. 
We can use the keywords explorer tab on Ahrefs to assess the difficulty of these sub-niches. 
So make sure not to instantly discount niches like law etc. when the main overarching 
niche/keywords are in the hard KD range - there could still be an opportunity for you. 
As a beginner, I would mostly avoid hard niches for client work or your own sites. You may not 
have the technical experience/confidence, capital, or the staying power required to dominate 
one of the hard niches. It will come though!  
Unless you can find a sub-niche or distinctly know you can help a client out, easy-medium KW 
difficulty niches will likely result in more success for you. 
How To Outrank The Competition 
Let’s look at the main factors we will be focusing on to outrank all three KD keyword levels.  
 


 
 
Again, we will go into more detail about these topics in their respective chapters, but let’s touch 
on them now. 
 
Off-page SEO​ - For organic  
 
As we can see, off-page SEO is one of the most important factors in ranking for both our GMB 
and in the organic results. 
Although the exact percentage of each ranking factor can’t be truly quantified, this at least gives 
us a good idea of how these ranking factors might impact our strategy. The pie chart isn’t an 
exact formula, but it gives us an indication of how important parts of our ranking strategy are. I 
would rather give you some sort of ranking factor indicator than not give you some rough 
estimates. Ta=hat P=pie chart is from Moz and seems fairly reasonable. 
There are lots of off-page SEO measures we can take. 
One example would be gaining high-quality local links to boost our DR and local 
relevance/prominence. Improving your strategy with links is a superpower for the easy KD 
 


 
 
keyword niches. If the competition isn’t even doing the basics right, you can be certain they 
aren’t investing time in gaining strong organic local links. 
Other examples are things like Google reviews, citation building, social signals, and behavioral 
signals. 
Easy KD niches will have very little Offpage SEO. From links to citations, they will have very 
little there and very little ongoing improvements either. 
Medium KD niches are likely to have a good amount of Offpage SEO. As mentioned, medium 
KD niche businesses are much more likely to invest in SEO compared to easy KD niches. They 
should have reviews, local links, and citations across most directories. 
Hard KD niches will have a lot of Offpage SEO as we will be competing directly with high-level 
SEO strategies (usually). Not only will we need links, but we’ll also need to build trust signals 
from highly authoritative websites, amongst other tactics. 
OnPage SEO​ - OnPage SEO signals account for 26.03% of local organic keyword ranking and 
13.82% for GMB ranking. 
So, if our competition slips up on their OnPage SEO optimization, we stand to gain a large 
advantage over them through a significant ranking factor. 
We touched on some of the basics already, but let’s have a look at some of the other ranking 
factors our competition might not have optimized, that we can. 
If any of these major factors aren’t optimized, there’s likely a large edge to be gained through 
your OnPage SEO in communicating which keywords you are relevant to.  
Does their OnPage have good: 
- Logical page architecture, with different pages clearly trying to rank for the high volume 
keywords  
- Strong, logical site interlinking structure to power up ranking pages 
- Clear longtail keyword strategy through additional pages and blog articles 
- A good user experience, meaning customers don’t want to bounce straight off the site 
- A fast loading website that Google and users prefer 
- Relevant, well-written content, while considering keyword density and other factors  
- A considered anchor text approach to power up their pages 
These are just some OnPage factors that our competition will slip up on somewhere.  
Easy KD niches will all have errors in their OnPage. 
 


 
 
Medium KD niches will have fewer errors in their OnPage, but will still have them. 
Hard KD niches will generally have a lot less fundamental errors and will even be updating and 
optimizing their OnPage - especially for the main pages they are trying to rank. 
In order to beat any of these difficulty levels, we simply need to do what they’re doing already, 
but better.  
Understanding Keywords For Local Lead Generation Assets 
As we already discussed, local lead generation assets are excellent because we decide 
whether we want to create an asset-based on how good the niche/location and keywords are. 
We can keep looking at different niches and their keywords until we decide on the industry we 
want to pick. Helping a client normally means working in imperfect conditions, potentially 
meaning mistakes to unwind and an undesirable location/niche. Creating your own asset 
eliminates that problem. 
 
Ideally, we want high-quality traffic (with buyer intent) in easy to rank niches (fast results, 
low-level competition). 
 
Yes, these large competitive keywords are going to be very profitable, but they’re also going to 
be extremely difficult to rank in, making for a time-consuming venture.  
 
On the flip side, we don’t want to go for keywords that don’t generate much value. The leads 
won’t be worth a lot and we might not get an ROI against our time invested. 
 
Valuing Local Keyword Traffic  
It’s important to understand the value of local traffic.  
Not all traffic is created equal. 
For example, a dentistry lead is going to be worth more than a car repair lead, generally. 
However, there are multiple factors to consider when picking a niche and determining the value 
of the traffic we generate.  
What’s the conversion rate? - ​This will differ from industry to industry and company to 
company, but be aware of how well your leads convert within a niche. Is it high or low? 
Do businesses want these leads? -​ I’ve made lead generation assets where the leads were 
hard to monetize. The industry already has an abundance of work and they face other 
problems, like efficiency, having the right team, and so on. A good example of this was a 
protection dog website. We rank really well and generate tons of inquiries, but so do all of the 
other protection dog firms. There aren’t many of them so demand is a lot higher than supply.  
 


 
 
Can you pass on leads in this industry? -​ Some local service businesses are more suited to 
the lead generation model than others. I personally like service area businesses that don’t 
require much originality. Businesses that are viewed as selling commodities. A commercial 
cleaning lead, for example, would be a prime example. Companies don’t really care who cleans 
their premises, as long as they do a good job and affordably. However, people are very 
sensitive about things like bridal hairdressers for example. They want to know the person doing 
their hair is excellent, perhaps wanting to know them personally before committing to anything. 
Therefore, making a faceless lead generation asset in this niche probably wouldn’t work that 
well. Whereas the commercial cleaning site would be fine. 
For your first assets, I definitely recommend setting up a basic service area lead generation 
model, like a trade service. 
In any case, you CAN get away with starting just about any local lead generation asset if you 
simply rent out the top organic position and rebrand the site to be like your partner who you give 
the leads to. 
Still, this can become contentious and tricky when dealing with a local business with a physical 
location as you can’t share their address.  
You could rank organically and rent that side out, but you wouldn’t be able to use a GMB as you 
can’t use the address that business already has. 
So if you wanted to rent out a website sitting top for dentist keywords, you could do that, and 
rebrand the site to appear like your business partner’s brand. 
However, if you ranked your GMB, it would have a different physical location as the one people 
need to visit to get their dentistry service.  
To get the most out of our asset, we want to combine the power of the GMB and the organic 
listings. That’s why I like generic service businesses that travel to the client. We can set the 
location on the GMB to serve a radius rather than having a physical location. Then when they 
call our generic GMB or website, they don’t care if they are suddenly dealing with a new 
company (or however you choose to monetize the lead) because they just want their problem 
solved as fast as possible. 
How much traffic are we likely to get? - ​It’s important to consider how many keywords you 
think you will rank for, how long it will take, and how much traffic you stand to gain.  
If you’re in a low value lead niche like taxi services, you will need a lot of traffic to gain a lot of 
leads, then some leads will turn into sales for your customer. Remember, although we are 
making a lead generation website, it’s still with the intention to make sales, not just to create 
leads.  
If you’re competing in a difficult niche like legal, you might need less traffic simply as each lead 
has the potential to be worth a lot more.  
 


 
 
 
 
How To Find Local Lead Generation Goldmine Niches 
There are lots of factors to consider when finding goldmine niches for local lead generation 
assets. Let’s look at them. 
 
KD level -​ Deciding on a KD level for the majority of your keywords within a niche is going to 
depend on your proficiency levels.  
 
For advanced local SEOs, a goldmine still might have a lot of high KD keywords. Despite KD 
being high, other factors may be in their favor making the niche a goldmine.  
 
However, for most, goldmine niches will have a lot of easy-medium KD keywords. They are 
simply easier and faster to rank for, meaning we have a clearer path to a cash-generating asset.  
 
I have sites in 0-15 KD niches making me over $500 a month with very little ongoing work. This 
is another thing to think about. Just because something is not widely contested on Google, it 
does not necessarily mean these keywords aren’t goldmines. There are lots of goldmines to be 
found in 0-15 KD niches for local lead generation assets. KDs this low also signifies the 
competition likely isn’t updating their strategy, meaning we don’t have to either once we top the 
SERPs.  
 
Many of my lead generation assets are on autopilot. I topped the SERPs, the competition isn't 
doing any SEO so I simply stay there with no effort. Still, you could be adding to your lead 
generation assets all the time, adding more areas, writing more blog articles and gaining more 
strong local links - but many can be left alone if you are pursuing more sites or don’t have time 
to work on them. 
 
Traffic​ ​- ​This is totally niche dependent but I want to give some rough metrics anyway. In an 
easy-medium KD difficulty range, if the top sites are getting ~100 organic visitors according to 
Ahrefs, there are lots of leads to be gained by dominating that niche. I have a lead generation 
asset that supposedly gets 80 organic clicks a month, but gets a high-value inquiry every few 
days, with the service costing anywhere from £1,000 to £10,000+ (with some small repair jobs 
thrown in there too). So, again, don’t be too scared by seeing these small amounts of clicks. 
The intent is there behind the searches, Ahrefs and similar won’t calculate all clicks. You can 
also expand into other areas as well as dominate more search terms within that niche to get 
more leads for your asset.  
 
The amount of clicks we want to see on our keyword analysis is competitors with 50-100+ traffic 
on Ahrefs, generally. This should lead to a successful lead generation asset if we can dominate 
them all for the best keywords (easy in an easy niche), meaning we should pick up a good 
 


 
 
amount of inquiries each month. My site supposedly having traffic of 80 visitors a month is a 
prime example of this. 
 
 
Converting the leads​ ​- ​Do people want the leads you generate and will they convert into 
money for your client? You need to know you are generating valuable leads before you pick 
your niche. Speak to your market. See if they are happy to take on more projects or want more 
customers. If no one wants your leads, you are not in a goldmine niche. 
 
Essential services​ ​-​ I already touched on this, but you can be certain that most essential 
services in any location will be used. This means there are valuable leads to be gained in 
virtually every location. From plumbing, to carpentry, to accounting - people in any location need 
these services. An added bonus is these niches often have very few SEOs in them, so the 
average KD for your keywords are in the easy 0-15 range. 
 
Niche essential services - ​Further to essential services are niche essential services. Niche 
essential services are simply sub-niches within essential services. 
 
Instead of just creating a builder lead generation asset, you could make one focused on 
extensions or loft conversions for example. 
 
It’s easier to dominate our keywords because our whole strategy revolves around only a handful 
of services... Whereas the competition is splitting themselves between lots of different services, 
meaning their relevancy is diversified between several topics. (Quick thought - yes you can 
dominate ‘builder’ type keywords AND sub-niches within builder type keywords, but the 
overarching term like ‘builder’ will generally be harder to rank for. We likely just need to build 
more DR, trust signals and topical authority in order to dominate niches and their sub-niches, 
something which isn’t impossible in easy niches) 
 
There’s still a high demand for these niche essential services, but with less supply, as there are 
more generalists (like builders) as opposed to specialists, like loft conversion specialists.  
 
Deciding how niche you want to go is up to you, your research, and the area you want to create 
your lead generation asset.  
 
Remember that context matters though. Sometimes it makes more sense to create the essential 
service site and simply rank sub-niches as service pages rather than creating a whole strategy 
around one sub-niche. Do your research and make an informed decision.  
 
Poor GMB optimization - ​If you find really poorly optimized GMBs in a niche, you might have a 
goldmine GMB opportunity. 
 
 


 
 
As GMBs are so heavily influenced by things like proximity and relevance, we can quickly rank 
above our competitors with a well placed registered address and a highly relevant GMB name 
and some basic optimizations. 
 
Although it’s against Google’s T&C’s to keyword stuff our GMB name, you can get away with it a 
lot of the time. To legitimize a keyword-stuffed GMB that is going well, you could make a 
website alongside it and perhaps even register a business in that name to make sure it doesn’t 
get delisted.  
 
You want to be looking for: 
 
- None of the keywords we want to rank for being used in the GMB name by our competitors. 
Law would be a good example of this, most companies are generalists and don’t only focus on 
one type of law.  
 
- Other GMBs ranking there by accident, rather than their business being revolved around the 
keywords they’re appearing for in the map pack. If you create a GMB purely to rank for these 
keywords, you will be viewed as the authority by Google and likely rank quickly.  
 
- GMBs not being central to the location we are trying to rank for. I wouldn’t expect to see much 
of this though. 
 
A GMB that can rank quickly makes your niche choice look a lot more attractive.  
 
If you can ethically (in Google’s eyes) set up GMBs in a central city location, you can get fast 
results because of your excellent proximity. If you also get your target keywords in your name, 
you have a chance to get really fast results through GMBs. 
 
For example, you could create a printing company in the middle of a city called ​The [city] 
printers. 
 
We get the main keyword and location keyword in our GMB name instantly, making us much 
more relevant than a lot of the competition without even starting on any other optimizations. We 
have a central city location to give us even more ranking power. Being central also increases 
our likelihood of being in a densely populated area, especially other business owners who might 
want printing services too.  
 
On the topic of central GMBs, I’ve been reading how it’s not that important to have them. I would 
disagree as we mostly want to rank for SL keywords. By being really proximate to our target 
location, we can rank in the map pack much easier. Being in the map pack and organic listing 
when someone types in an SL keyword is a lethal combination. Not to mention being central 
puts us in a densely populated area like I mentioned above, meaning we should be close to a lot 
of potential customers.  
 


 
 
 
Network - ​Knowing someone you can sell leads to instantly makes a niche a lot better. It 
normally takes a bit of time to create a good relationship with a business owner even when you 
are giving them leads. So, having someone in place to take them on can reduce the time 
between you and your lead generation asset making cash. 
 
However, a business owner to give the leads to is just an added bonus. It’s not necessary. It 
would be better to start an excellent goldmine niche lead generation asset and find someone 
after you start getting leads over starting a site simply because you know someone in that 
industry.  
 
Beware. When you mention the lead generation method to people, they will try and pitch you on 
making one for their industry.  
 
Remember, we make these lead generation websites because they can be excellent lasting 
assets, not because our friend wants more leads. 
 
Make sure to prioritize having an excellent niche over having a partner in place to help you 
monetize the leads. If the leads are valuable, you will always be able to monetize them. 
Remember that. 
 
The more I’ve been sharing this strategy with friends and other SEOs, the more I hear them 
getting enticed by a friend in a random location to start a random lead generation asset. I hear 
the idea and the whole concept is based around how their friend is a great guy and it’s a great 
industry!  
 
So what? Focus on the essentials listed in this chapter. If we create a lead generation asset in 
an easy KD niche, with high converting leads that are wanted by local businesses - we can’t go 
wrong. Making a lead generation asset for your buddy’s sake (at no cost) without it having the 
other necessary conditions of an excellent lead generation asset is going to lead to wasted time 
and wasted money. 
 
For a beginner lead generation asset, I would be looking at easy KD niches. I would also try to 
incorporate as many of the other goldmine factors as possible, like having a partner in place (or 
knowing the area you make the asset in) to take on the leads or rent the site out for a monthly 
fee.  
 
Go for easy KD niches because the competition are weak and not even SEOs. We can likely 
rank within a few months for SL keywords, hopefully faster for our KGR long tail keywords, and 
power up our GMB for fast results too.  
 
Even if your lead generation assets take more than a few months to rank, that’s okay because 
they could be at the top of Google for years. The value is often realized over the long term. Also, 
 


 
 
you don’t need to spend endless hours on each asset. You can be guiding multiple lead 
generation assets to the top of Google at the same time. You don’t have to create one and sit 
there for 4 months before starting another. Set one up, optimize it correctly, figure out the 
ongoing work you have, schedule that in, then focus on the next asset! 
 
To pick your niche, simply keep doing keyword research for different niches and locations until 
you have found the best one possible. Doing all this research will give you confidence in your 
niche, as well as giving you options for your next lead generation project. 
 
 
Your First Lead Generation Asset 
While your first site might not go to plan, as your skills and knowledge progress, you will start to 
create sites that are closer and closer to a goldmine niche.  
 
Not everyone has massive successes with their first site.  
 
However, you should definitely aim to have a successful first venture. If you follow everything 
laid out in this book, there’s no reason you won’t. It’s NOT farfetched to get a good rank and 
rent, pay per lead/conversion site on your first attempt, especially in easy niches. 
 
It’s worth going through the entire creation process for your first lead generation asset. For 
every future site, you understand the process and can outsource effectively. Or if you don’t want 
to input everything, you should at least be following what happens closely. 
 
Again, although at first you will be raring to go, it’s worth doing a lot of research on different 
locations and niches to find a good opportunity. 
 
As you become more confident and comfortable with the process, you can try more audacious 
lead generation assets. 
 
This could mean: 
 
- Trying foreign SEO, which has a weaker average competition, but the added language hurdle. 
 
- Competing in higher difficulty niches (higher reward), but speeding up the process through 
your Local SEO proficiency (proficiency = being able to manipulate the main website and GMB 
ranking factors as fast as possible, while not being penalized by Google and wasting any 
time/money investment. It’s worth noting that plenty of hard niches take time to rank in no matter 
how good you are).  
 
- Setting up a localized PBN to create extremely powerful, localized links in a city/location to 
power up all your lead generation assets. 
 


 
 
 
- Outsourcing the entire process or joint venturing with a partner to remove your time from the 
equation. When you first start, it might be unclear exactly how to make a successful lead 
generation asset (not because you don’t know, but simply because you’ve never experienced 
it), as well as not having someone to joint venture with. As you create your own lead generation 
assets, you will become confident enough to outsource the process, as well as meet people 
suitable to joint venture with.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 


 
 
Optimizing A Local Site’s OnPage  
 
OnPage SEO is just another opportunity for us to dominate the competition. By getting all the 
crucial OnPage elements fine-tuned, we stand a good chance to compete in any keyword 
difficulty from an OnPage perspective. 
 
We need to communicate to Google how our OnPage strategy is superior to our competitors in 
every way. 
 
Thankfully in Local SEO, we aren’t competing with sites who have hundreds of pages. This 
means we don’t need endless amounts of content. 
 
However, we do want to make each page we make as strong and optimized as possible. 
 
We’ll start from the top by looking at your website itself, the site architecture, etc. - then we’ll 
move onto creating and populating your content pages and how you should link everything 
together. 
 
We’ll explore optimizing for singular location sites and multi-location sites too. 
 
This chapter should be read from start to finish before implementing any steps. When it comes 
to OnPage, you need a holistic understanding of all the key elements before you can start 
making well-informed decisions for new sites or existing sites. 
 
I even created a dummy website showing you how to create locally optimized ranking pages 
which will feature throughout! 
Website Speed 
Website loading speed is an important ranking factor. Not to mention an important conversion 
factor. 
 
You should aim to have your website loading within 2-3 seconds if possible, but the faster the 
better. 
 
There are numerous guides online about cutting down your site speed. 
 
Two large contributors to your page speed are your code (WordPress theme) and your host. I 
like Hostgator and Siteground as hosting sites. Hostgator is really heap, while Siteground has 
pretty good support. 
 
 


 
 
I would recommend getting Elementor for your WordPress theme if creating your own site. Or if 
you want a totally free option, I like Generate Press - it’s very lightweight. Using the default 
WordPress editor can be painful and more time consuming than it needs to be. I would add the 
Generate Blocks plugin (same developer) with your theme to help you make landing page style 
pages.  
 
Although there isn’t a whole load of bloat on the example website given throughout this chapter, 
you can still see how fast it loads (using Generate Press & Generate Blocks). Check it out when
