you’re ready, it’s a template for best OnPage practises!  
 
https://localkingdom.net/ 
 
Mobile optimization is an important consideration for Local SEO. I find that most of my inquiries 
come through mobile phone calls, despite desktop traffic normally being in the 25-50% range. 
Either way, you would always expect mobile searches to be on the rise with everyone having 
smartphones these days. If you create your site on Generate Press, your site will look good on 
mobile, unless you create a huge header or just generally try and overhaul the format, which 
there isn’t much need to do so.  
 
 
 
 
If you’re working on a client site, run it through gtmetrix.com to see which parts of the site are 
loading slowly. Usually, images are a culprit on client sites. 
 
 
 
 


 
 
Site Architecture 
Site architecture (how you layout your pages) is really important. We need a clear and logical 
strategy.  
 
 
 
 
The architecture above is the general page structure we want for our websites. Let’s make sure 
we understand the purpose of each kind of page. Some pages are meant to rank, some are 
meant to support those ranking pages. We will look at internal linking later on in the chapter. 
 
 
Homepage -​ The homepage is generally going to be optimized for SL keywords according to 
where the business is first situated.  
 
www.example.com 
 
 
Location pages -​ Location pages act like homepages (we rank them for SL keywords), but 
have a slightly different URL structure. 
 
 
www.example.com/location  
 
 
Service pages -​ We rank service pages for service search terms with keyword volume, like 
“roof repairs Manchester”. 
 
www.example.com/roof-repairs 
 


 
 
 
 
Supporting service pages -​ We use service pages, sometimes even without keyword volume, 
to build more topical relevance and link to our other ranking service pages. Ideally, there will be 
searches, but in small locations, there might be a negligible amount. 
 
www.example.com/slate-tile-repair 
 
 
Blog articles -​ We write articles and link them to ranking pages to pass internal link juice. 
Occasionally we might want to rank a blog article. 
 
www.example.com/blog/best-tradesman-materials 
 
 
About -​ Just a generic page to give customers more info about the business. 
 
www.example.com/about 
 
 
Contact -​ A page for customers to contact us from. 
 
www.example.com/contact 
 
Canonicalization 
This is a really important part of OnPage SEO. Google doesn’t like you ranking for two very 
similar keywords with two (or more) separate pages, because they don’t want you to take up all 
the SERPs space. Having lots of similar pages will diminish ranking potential on any of these 
pages. We avoid canonicalization in our site architecture by clearly differentiating our page 
names. 
 
By avoiding canonicalization, we clearly communicate to Google how our pages should be 
understood and prioritized for the keywords we are targeting.  
 
Here are some example search terms that would all be under the same ranking page (the 
homepage in our example): 
 
“Roofing manchester” 
“Roofers manchester” 
“Roofing company manchester” 
 
 


 
 
Basically most of our SL keywords that are very similar.  
 
This means we don’t need to create additional pages in order to rank for these keywords. We 
don’t need an extra page like: 
 
roofersexample.com/roofers-manchester 
 
This would be a clear example of canonicalization. 
 
Both our homepage and our new​ /roofers-manchester​ page would be competing to rank for the 
same keywords. 
 
Let’s look at more examples. 
 
You wouldn’t want to name your About Us page anything like: 
 
example.com/about-our-manchester-roofing-company 
 
Keep it clean, keep it away from canonicalization and just call it About Us! 
 
Avoid this: 
 
example.com/roofing-services/flat-roofing 
 
Have this: 
 
example.com/flat-roofing 
 
Or 
 
example.com/flat-roofing-location 
 
example.com//flat-roofing/location 
 
For supporting content, try not to over canonicalize too.  
 
Just be mindful of your URLs and use interchangeable terms when you plan to use supporting 
content to point to a ranking page. 
 
So instead of having  
example.com/roof-repairs 
 


 
 
example.com/slate-roof-repairs 
 
Do this instead... 
example.com/roof-repairs 
example.com/slate-tile-repair 
 
You can get away with having a few crossover words in your URLs, but it’s something we want 
to avoid, especially as it’s relatively easy to avoid with a bit of word juggling.  
 
Just be aware you are trying to keep page names as separate as possible in order to help 
Google differentiate between how it should rank your pages. 
 
If you have built your site already and it is canonicalized to hell, start using 301 redirects on the 
pages that are canonicalizing your ranking pages. Redirect the canonicalizing page to the page 
you want all the relevancy pushed to - AKA your ranking pages. 
Types of Pages 
Let’s explore the different types of pages in more detail in order to fully understand why we have 
each of them in relation to our keyword strategy. 
 
Ranking pages - ​Ranking pages are our homepage, service pages, and location pages.  
 
Homepage - ​We rank our homepage for SL keywords. It’s our most powerful page and it’s the 
most logical page that would rank for SL keywords. 
 
If you have a client in Florida called bobsbarbers.com, our homepage should rank for “barber 
Florida” or “Florida barber” type keywords.  
 
Service pages - ​Service pages should rank for specific services you or your client provides. 
This means we make page names to match these keywords with keyword search volume. An 
example would be bobsbarbers.com/haircut. 
 
Supporting pages - ​Supporting pages are created to build topical relevance and send internal 
link juice throughout our site. We can boost these pages up and point them towards our home, 
services, or location pages. These are going to be our blog articles and service pages 
without/with less keyword volume. Ideally, these pages will rank and generate traffic in their own 
right, but if not, they can at least pass niche/location relevance to other pages. 
 
Locations - ​If Bob the barber added a new location to his business in Las Vegas, that page 
would be called bobsbarbers.com/barbers-las-vegas, his location page would rank for SL 
keywords in Las Vegas. Although we are using the word barbers twice in our URL, we are not 
canonicalized. The reason? Google realizes our homepage is designed to rank for “barber 
 


 
 
Florida” type keywords, whereas this new page is meant to rank for “barber Las Vegas” 
keywords. 
 
Homepage and services seem relatively obvious in the grand scheme of things, however, more 
thought has to be put towards location pages. 
Understanding Location Pages 
Location pages are important. They are ranking pages that focus on ranking in a specific 
location. This could mean a small area within your target state/city/location, or it could mean the 
next state/city/town near to you. It’s easier to manipulate Google for service area businesses as 
we can create pages to rank for different areas we don’t have a physical address at. Yet, saying 
you have a bakery in a location you don’t actually have one will only cause problems and not 
work. 
 
The location page is the page that will rank for SL keywords - and kind of act as your homepage 
for each additional area you target. As location pages can get us SL keyword clicks, they are an 
important part of our Local SEO strategy.  
 
If I had a client who wanted to serve a single state or city, I would still add additional location 
pages for areas within their overall service radius. For example, in our Manchester example, 
there are prominent nearby areas like Stockport. We would create a page like this to cover 
Stockport: 
 
bestroofingcompany.com/stockport 
 
We could also create pages for Salford, Ashton-Under-Lyne, Pendlebury, Stretford, Failsworth, 
etc.  
 
 


 
 
 
 
The reasons we do this are twofold. 
 
Firstly, local searchers in Stockport might specify Stockport in their search instead of 
Manchester, meaning we can pick up extra clicks from Stockport.  
 
The other reason is that it will add local relevancy to our organic and GMB approach. It will help 
our GMB to rank in that area (as long as it is not hours away and we have good authority), and 
generally writing about local points of interest is going to make our GMB and website more 
locally relevant as a whole from Google’s perspective. 
 
When a client has one state or city they want to target, it’s pretty easy to make these pages. 
There’s only going to be so many of them before you run out of areas. It’s definitely worth doing 
as part of your ongoing strategy.  
 
Another strategy we can use with location pages is creating them en masse. We’ll look at how 
to do that when we come to write our location page. 
 
The reason we do it? We can extend our GMBs reach by ranking in more local map packs 
(again, there is a ‘cap’ to this - eventually our GMB will run out of reach). The other reason is 
making an unlimited amount of location pages to rank in the organic listings.  
 
By creating loads of location pages, we can cover loads of new locations to pick up loads of SL 
keywords, as well as service clicks too: 
 
example.com/leeds 
example.com/manchester/ 
example.com/southampton/ 
 


 
 
 
Or 
 
example.com/roofers-leeds 
example.com/roofers-manchester 
example.com/roofers-southampton 
 
Now, without a high DR or local relevance, it will be hard to just walk into a brand new city and 
dominate any area with just a new location page. However, you should have success in adding 
more and more small local areas where there is less competition, especially if they are near 
your GMB. 
 
Here are a few different ways you might end up using location pages: 
 
A single state/city client site - ​If my client was set on sticking with their state or city and didn’t 
want to expand, I would create location pages for the areas within his service radius. I’d choose 
any with a population over 10,000. Again, this is more relevant to service area businesses.  
 
A multi-location client site - ​If you have a physical address client with multi-locations, you 
simply optimize each GMB location as best as you can. For service area businesses, I would 
optimize around the area where each GMB is, then add additional location pages nearby where 
it looks to be a good ranking opportunity (could be a nearby village with 20,000 people in it who 
might search “roofer [town]”). 
 
Your own lead generation asset - ​If you’re making your own lead generation asset, you can 
afford to be a bit more ​gung ho​ and see how far you can take adding extra location pages. I 
would still have some lead generation assets that were ‘safe’ and less spammy, but I would also 
be pushing the boundaries with others by adding loads of locations to pick up as many SL 
keyword clicks as possible!  
Writing Ranking Pages 
Let’s see how we create a ranking page, then we’ll look at location pages specifically.  
 
These are some general rules you should be thinking about when creating your content.  
 
We are trying to include words and phrases that are going to make your ranking pages relevant 
for their desired search terms. 
 
Check out this link to see a dummy page laid out with best practices: 
 
 


 
 
https://localkingdom.net/keyword/location/ 
 
First sentence -​ Use your targeted keywords in the first sentence of your ranking page. 
 
LSI keywords - ​Include LSI keywords in and around your targeted keywords to show 
niche/location relevancy. 
 
Avoid canonicalization - ​Ensure you are not canonicalizing other ranking pages by using too 
many LSI’s that are specific to another ranking page. 
 
Use specific data points - ​Google your page topic and see what appears. You might Google 
something like roof repairs. Check the top results and take specific data points from the top 
results. We know that Google understands these pages to have the best information on roof 
repairs, so if we can include numbers, dates, locations, etc. that these pages use, Google is 
more likely to think we are relevant to the keywords we are targeting. I haven’t proved this is a 
massive needle mover but I heard the concept a while ago and have implemented it ever since. 
It seems logical and I doubt it would hurt us even if it’s not a major needle mover. 
 
Ideally, you will outsource the content writing if making a new site. If you’re taking on a new 
client you should budget for content creation and if you’re making your own lead generation 
asset, it would be better to focus on other tasks if you have the money to outsource. If times are 
really tough, simply find inspiration online to write your pages. 
 
Quality - ​Ideally, if we can have quality content that people want to read, we should go for that. 
Overall, content becomes less important the more your business is viewed as a commodity. Not 
many people are going to read pages and pages about commercial cleaning, but they might for 
vets, beauty clinics, or hotels. 
Working Out Word Count & Keyword Density  
Let’s start with our trusty competitor analysis to see what kind of wording approach Google 
prefers for the SERPs we’re targeting. There is no one size fits all for OnPage SEO. In some 
niches, Google wants thousands and thousands of words per page to rank it. In other niches, 
only a small amount would be expected. That’s why we look to our competitors to gain an 
understanding of what Google might want for our target SERPs. 
 
In some easy niches, however, the competition might have garbage OnPage and therefore we 
revert to local OnPage best practices, which we’ll be looking at. There are endless local niches 
where the hardest keywords are KD 5 and I would expect their OnPage to be pretty bad. 
 
In medium - hard niches where the KD of keywords is generally 15+, modeling our competition 
is going to be more important than easier niches. 
 
 


 
 
Remodeling Competitors 
After choosing your niche and identifying the keywords you will target, let’s select a few sites 
within our niche to remodel our efforts against. 
 
If you’re working on a client site, perhaps they are already optimized for the best keywords in 
their location, or perhaps you need to rearrange their OnPage strategy to focus on the best 
keywords they can go for. Do that, decide on how the site should be organized, then let’s get 
back to finding our best competitors. 
 
If you have Ahrefs, go ahead and select the competitors on the first page for your keywords who 
have a low DA compared to the competition. The reason we are doing this is that we are 
assuming they are ranking well due to their OnPage optimizations, rather than their high DR. 
 
For a free alternative, use Moz, set up a free account, and look for the competitors with a low 
Domain Authority (similar to DR). 
 
We want to disregard the following competitors: 
 
- Pay less attention to exact match domains to your keyword (like someone called 
carpentrybaltimore.com) as they could be getting a relevancy push from their domain. While this 
is less of a factor than it used to be, from what I see in the local SERPs, it does make a 
difference. These exact match businesses also have exact match citations, social accounts, 
GMB names, etc. not just their domain pushing them to be more relevant to the search term 
than most competitors.  
 
- Exact match GMB names (see the screenshot of Manchester Roofing Services). It’s likely they 
are ranking well because of this rather than any OnPage wizardry. Disregard these as 
competitors to remodel your ranking page approach from.  
 


 
 
 
 
 
 
After you’ve got a few sites to remodel, let’s figure out the word count and keyword density we 
need for each ranking page by using Seoquake. Generate an info report on the competitor 
ranking pages we are remodeling.  
 
I like to be roughly in the middle of the averages for our keyword density - and 10%-20% above 
the average word count.  
 
If I was in an easy niche, I would probably just get a feel for the amount of content the 
competition is using, then just go with best practices. analyzing every single ranking page and 
turning this into a 1+ hour exercise isn’t really necessary when taking on weak competition. For 
medium - hard niches, I would pay more attention to wordcount and keyword density. 
 
I picked these three websites out for our roofing example: 
https://www.renownroofingmanchester.co.uk/​ (highest organic and does not massively beat 
competitors from a DA perspective)  
 
 


 
 
http://walkerroofingltd.co.uk/​ (low authority, not in the map pack, but must have good OnPage 
relevancy to rank organically. Even though it’s the lowest of the three examples, there’s also no 
use of Manchester or roofing in the name, which makes me think it will have good OnPage) 
 
https://www.manchesterandashton-roofing.co.uk/​ (ranks high organically and in the map pack, 
probably has good OnPage)

Now we want to remodel their general approach as this is what Google is promoting in the 
SERPs from an OnPage perspective. These are some best practices to bear in mind:  
 
- We don’t want a singular keyword over 3.5% - 4% keyword density (e.g. roofing) as you may 
be over-optimized from day one. 
 
- We don’t want a keyword like “manchester roofing” having over 2.8% keyword density. SL 
keywords are less natural than singular keywords like “roofing” - therefore we should have a 
lower keyword density or we could be over-optimized and get penalized.  
 
- Have over 650 words on a ranking page. These sites fit within this rule, so we’ll be writing 
more than 650 anyway. It can be easy to think you only need 200 words on a page, but as we’re 
talking about our main ranking pages here (home page, main service pages), it makes sense to 
beef them up with good niche and local content. 
 
After this analysis, if we were targeting this niche, I would be writing ranking pages to have a 
3.8% keyword density for “roofing” and 1.2% for “Manchester”. I would shoot for 800-1000 
words to make sure we have the competition outmatched. I’ve found the sweet spot to be 
around 1000 words on local ranking pages. However, if you’re in a competitive niche and 
everyone has over 1000 words, go for 20% more than the average word count. In easier niches, 
I think you always want at least 1000 words on your homepage and main service ranking pages, 
but less significant ranking pages can get away with 400-500 words. 
 
Best practice - ​If you find a really uncompetitive niche where the highest ranking pages have 
the world’s worst SEO (<200 words and >4% keyword density), just use these best practices 
instead. 
 
- Wordcount: 1000 words for main ranking pages (home, popular services, location pages) and 
400-500 words in really easy niches where the competition isn’t writing a lot of words at all. 
 
- Keyword density: 1.8% - 2.4% is a nice sweet spot for using the keywords we want to rank for.  
 
I’ve added a quick example below for a new site I’ve been working on. Within the first 7 days of 
these ranking pages indexing, I received 6 clicks and ranked high in the SERPs for a web 
design [location] search term. I mirrored two websites that were ranking all over the country. Of 
course, there are some other factors at play, but this is mostly without any optimizations, 
 


 
 
citations, links, etc. - simply throwing the site up with some good OnPage using the tactics 
spoken about above for my main ranking homepage and some ranking service pages. 
 
 
 
Writing Location Pages 
While our location pages are ranking pages and everything above applies to location pages - 
there are some additional factors to consider with location pages specifically.  
 
First things first, let’s see how we can create duplicate location pages en masse. 
 
Use Page Generator Pro to create loads of duplicate style location pages. It’s a great plugin that 
I’ve been using: ​https://www.wpzinc.com/plugins/page-generator-pro/ 
 
By the way, I found these two tutorials really useful when setting up that plugin: 
 
 


 
 
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_JnUcB4y8Qg 
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LqQgK_KsD8s 
 
When creating these extra location pages with the plugin, we will likely want to set our URLs to 
be like this: 
 
roofingexample.com/roof-repair/location 
 
However, if I wasn’t making loads of pages with the plugin I would be happy sticking with the 
other URL structure we were looking at before: 
 
roofingexample.com/roof-repair-london 
 
Both work. 
 
Although we can get away with a degree of duplicate content, Google doesn’t exactly love us for 
it. Certain websites ranking nationally are able to have 100% duplicate content on their location 
pages simply due to having really high DR. 
 
In my experience, this is how Google would rank 3 websites if all things remained the same 
other than what % of the content is duplicated: 
 
- Completely unique content would rank highest 
- 30% unique & 70% duplicate would come a close second 
- 100% duplicate would of course rank third 
 
With this in mind, decide what % of duplicate content you want to use for your extra location 
pages.  
 
Single location clients - ​I would learn towards writing main location pages (those near to them 
or where they actually have GMBs) as completely unique.  
 
Then for locations a bit further from their base or with smaller populations, I would consider the 
mix of unique and duplicate.  
 
For really small areas (15,000 and less), you could consider the 100% duplicate approach, but 
some clients might not be comfortable with that approach. Decide what is best for your situation. 
 
Multi-location clients -​ If my client had physical addresses in different areas, I would treat each 
area they have an address in like a single location client site described above. The location 
page would be centered around where their address (and therefore their GMB) is located. Then 
I would weigh up which additional locations in the area should have location pages and how 
much unique/duplicate content should be assigned to each page.  
 


 
 
 
I would follow the general theme of how I used some duplicate content pages for a single 
location, but consider using even more if I had loads of areas to create location pages for. 
 
 
Lead generation assets - ​When it comes to your own lead generation asset, you can afford to 
be more reckless as you aren’t risking a client site or your reputation. 
 
You can go crazy with the plugin and be audacious with the number of locations you target and 
the amount of duplicate content you use.  
 
Another consideration is if any duplicate pages are doing well, you can update these with 
unique content for longevity and to boost them even more (same goes for any clients who are 
happy to use this approach). 
 
The nice part about the plugin is that it acts as a flood and drain approach. We cast a wide net, 
see where we can pick up clicks, then optimize pages we want to improve and retract location 
pages we don’t want/if we think it’s too spammy. 
 
By the way, an easy way to find more local location pages is by using Google Maps to find your 
state/city/location, zooming out, then seeing which areas remain on the map. These areas tend 
to have more searches than those which disappear.  
 
Location pages are a real strength of Local SEO. Everyone hears “local” and assumes we are 
limited in some way. Remember, “local” is mostly just the perspective of the customer who 
wants a local business. Despite being in local, we can still think big and cover huge parts of a 
country. 
Structuring A Location Page 
As each location page will be targeting SL keywords, the way we distribute our word count and 
keyword density is the same as described for ranking pages described above. analyze your 
competitors (ideally those also using a multi-location strategy - look outside of your target area if 
you have to), then you can decide on how much of this content you want to be unique, a 
70%-30% duplicate split or completely duplicate.  
 
Write your location page content like it’s a homepage. Remember, we’re ranking for SL 
keywords - so people want to know a little bit about everything to do with your service. What 
services you offer, why they should choose you etc. 
 
If going down any kind of duplicate approach, make sure your images aren’t optimized or 
catered towards a specific location. Just make them niche relevant.  
 
 


 
 
I embed a location map in every location page. If this new location page is within my GMB 
service radius, I use the same map embed as I do for my home page. If this location page is 
outside of my GMB radius, I add a map embed of that specific location. For example, we’re 
roofers in Manchester but have added a Leeds location page. Assuming we have no GMB in 
Leeds, I would add a map embed of Leeds like so: 
 
 
 
 
Swap out the location word used before with your new location word throughout the page. 
 
If creating unique or partially unique landing pages, you can include specific data points that are 
relevant to that location. 
 
Here’s an example of a location page with 30% unique content and 70% duplicate content.  
 
https://localkingdom.net/keyword/another-location/
