Heading Structure 
For headings (H tags), I like to follow best practices as it can get messy following competitors. 
Also, if you are not in medium - hard niches, your competitors are likely to have a completely 
random heading structure, so modeling those doesn’t do a lot for us. 
 
I see many SEOs saying that H tags don’t mean anything in SEO, but we have seen jumps in 
our ranking position when experimenting with OnPage factors, like H tag structure. 
 
My “best practice” header tag structure came from listening to those in SEO who spend a lot of 
time manipulating OnPage Local SEO. I copied what they were saying and tested on new sites 
to see what works best.  
 
This structure is what I use: 
 
H1 -​ Include your niche keyword - but not your location 
 
H2 - ​Include your niche keyword and location (1-2 of these) 
 
H3 - ​Include your keyword and location (2 of these) 
 
H4-H6 - ​Google especially doesn’t seem to pay much attention to these. I would still include 
something relevant in these H tags though, perhaps relevant keywords, a question that is 
frequently asked, and so on.  
 
Note -​ Do not include your main niche keyword (roofing in this case) in H1-H3 header tags on 
any other pages than your specified ranking page.  
 
For example, don’t include the keywords “roofing” or “manchester” in your H1-H3 on your about 
us page. Avoid canonicalizing in any way possible. This is just an extra step to avoid that 
canonicalization and keep our ranking pages more geared towards the words we actually want 
to rank for. 
 
The example URL provided shows you best practices for your heading structures: 
https://localkingdom.net/keyword/location/ 
 
 
 
 
 
 


 
 
Blog Articles 
Blog articles are a great way to move your ranking pages up the SERPs. 
 
For virtually all levels of KD, I like a biweekly blog article. You could write more in a medium - 
hard difficult niche. 
 
Here’s an example of a blog article template: 
 
https://localkingdom.net/blog-article-title-do-not-use-ranking-page-keywords/ 
 
As you can see, there are multiple benefits to writing blog articles: 
 
Local signals - ​We can embed our Google Maps listing, at the bottom of the article. This helps 
to build local relevance. We can also add an NAP citation and other links, like our web2.0s and 
main directories. 
 
Internally link to ranking pages - ​This is one of the strongest features of the blog writing 
strategy. I am really aggressive with internal anchors to ranking pages from blog articles. If I 
want to boost a specific page, I will use heavily optimized keyword anchor texts to link to that 
page from the article. For example, we could link to our manchester roofing example with 
anchors like “roofing manchester, manchester roofing, roofers in manchester, manchester 
roofing company” etc. - More on internal linking later in the chapter. 
 
Add more all-round relevance -​ By writing about sub-topics related to our ranking pages then 
linking to them, we are building up more contextual and local relevance. Also, Google enjoys 
fresh, ‘quality’, relevant content. 
 
Articles can rank themselves - ​We can target our KGR keywords and long tail keywords with 
blog articles. Sometimes these can rank by themselves, leading to more local traffic, or research 
type traffic from around the world. Local traffic stands the chance to convert and research traffic 
boosts our authority in the niche as we have more local traffic than our competitors.  
 
More niche/local relevance -​ Boost relevancy by externally linking out to authorities in our 
niche or local area (you should do this towards the end of the article as shown in the example) 
 
Actually help local customers -​ Actually write valuable content that local people will find 
useful. This will lead to more leads, conversions, and hopefully even links.  
 
Check out what I managed to do by using blog articles. We were able to funnel all of our 
relevance to our homepage for a national search term in the UK. It’s worth noting we built 0 links 
 


 
 
to this site but simply used blog articles and aggressive internal linking and anchor texts. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 


 
 
Supporting Content 
So we have written our ranking pages either by following the word count trend of our 
competitors or simply using best practices. Now we have a home page, service pages revolving 
around service keywords that pick up monthly searches and hopefully some extra location 
pages. 
 
Now we need to write supporting content. Yes, blog articles are a form of supporting content, 
but let’s think of supporting content as our extra service pages and any pages we don’t add to 
our blog. 
 
We should create pages relevant to our local business for a few reasons: 
 
- Add local relevance to our site 
- Add niche relevance to our site  
- Potentially pick up long tail traffic  
- Help customers through their buying journey by giving them useful information 
 
Niche Supporting Content  
Niche supporting content simply means to write about the industry or type of business you or 
your client has. A perfect example of niche content in the roofing space would be something like 
a “slate repair” page. It’s a good page for a few reasons: 
 
- Google will understand that it is a relevant topic in the roofing space, therefore making our 
website more relevant to roofing as a whole. 
 
- We could create multiple repair style pages like this slate repair page and link to our “roof 
repair” page, which is likely to get the most traffic. By linking relevant content to our main roof 
repair page, we are building the topical relevance of that page, as well as boosting it with 
relevant anchors.  
 
- A customer might get onto our site, suspect they have a slate problem, click that page and 
realize, yes, that is the problem and yes, we can help them out. So it helps the customer 
experience too. 
 
We don’t want to go overboard and add hundreds of supporting pages to our website. I would 
first add all the ranking pages we want to generate traffic to, then think which supporting niche 
pages make sense for our site.  
 
There are a few ways we can do this: 
 
 


 
 
- Ask your client.​ They might like a particular style of roofing that is profitable to them. Maybe 
the service gets very few searches, but it would add good niche relevance and they can tell you 
all about the topic, making it an easy page to write.  
 
- Competitor research.​ Look and steal any supporting pages your competition consistently 
has. If all the top 5 competitors in your niche had a ‘chimney repair page’, I would also create 
this even if my client didn’t want to provide a chimney repair service. Make sure to not be 
missing supporting pages that all our competitors seem to have.  
 
- Recommended searches.​ Use Google’s Keyword Planner tool. This is free. Or you can use 
Ahrefs keyword explorer. Type in your main SL keywords and service keywords to get 
supporting page ideas. Notice how there are keywords with 0 volume. Some of these pages can 
still be worth making as Google obviously thinks they are relevant to your main ranking 
keywords. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 


 
 
- Use “searched related to” - ​Google gives us even more ideas of keywords and terms they 
think are closely related to your ranking keywords.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
When writing niche relevant supporting pages, I would see how much the competition has 
written. If they have very few words, I would shoot for 300-500 words. If they have a lot of 
words, I would go for 20% over the average of my competitors. My keyword density would be in 
the 1.8% - 2.4% range.  
 
Here’s a single city rank and rent site below. Again, this was another site in an easy niche 
where 90% of the strategy is good OnPage, supporting content and blog articles. This asset is 
rented out for $400 a month and it’s barely been touched in well over a year.  
 


 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Local Supporting Content  
Local supporting content is a good way to dominate our competition. Most of our competitors 
will only have niche supporting content and hopefully not even be adding blog articles. 
 
By creating locally supporting content, we add more local relevance to our website and even our 
GMB.  
 
While I think niche supporting content should make up for 80/90%+ (niche dependent) of our 
supporting content strategy, I like to use locally focused content too. This content generally 
won’t drive traffic to our site, but we can always multi-purpose it, perhaps by making it useful 
enough that other local businesses would share it, getting us valuable local links. Still, even 
though it won’t drive us traffic for specific keywords, it should help us become more relevant to 
pretty much every type of keyword we are trying to rank for. 
 
We can also use local supporting content to give us more relevance when we are lacking it. 
Perhaps you’ve added more location pages to your website and can’t get a GMB in your new 
locations. Writing supporting content about the area you’ve entered will help you become more 
locally relevant and help your chances of ranking in that area. We would send an internal link 
from our new local supporting content page to our location we are trying to rank.  
 
 
 


 
 
Types of local content -​ Ideally you’ll make your local content useful, linkable, and relevant to 
your niche. Sure, you could write about the top 5 things to do in your location, but that’s quite 
generic. Here are some more ideas. 
 
- A plumber could write about the top 10 bathroom showrooms in the city. This would be really 
locally relevant as Google would understand the addresses, business names, etc. are all within 
your target location. It could also be useful for other people in the area and help you build 
relations with the businesses you linked to. 
 
- A hotel could talk about the top attractions in the location (a bit generic). 
 
- A restaurant could talk about the best places to get different ingredients from different shops 
around the location.  
 
You get the idea.  
Internal Linking 
 
Here’s a multi-location website and how you might expect to link your pages together. The same 
concepts apply to a site where you only want to target a small radius and have location pages 
for the areas in and around your service radius. 
 
It’s worth noting these aren’t ironclad interlinking rules but used to demonstrate a rough 
interlinking strategy. This is the style I go for and it works well.  
 
 


 
 
Generally, we want to be linking our main service pages to our homepage as they are really 
relevant. Then we can link our supporting pages either to ranking service pages or to our 
homepage. Whichever is more relevant.  
 
We can use blog articles to send an internal link to any page, whichever makes the most sense. 
That could be home, main service, support service, another blog article and location page, etc. 
 
Ideally, we won’t be sending loads of links away from our homepage as we want to be pushing 
internal link juice towards this page rather than away from it. However, this isn’t an ironclad rule 
either. You’ll see plenty of top tier websites with links going away from their homepage. So if it 
makes sense and you want to link away from your homepage, I don’t think it will desecrate your 
strategy.  
Anchor Text 
Internal anchor text is used to tell Google what that page is about. An internal link with an exact 
keyword match or partial keyword match anchor is going to help rank the page we link to. 
 
As I mentioned in the blog article section, we can be very aggressive with internal anchors. We 
can get away with a lot of internal anchor stuffing that our competition hopefully won’t be taking 
advantage of. In easy niches they usually don’t, medium niches have more and hard niches 
tend to do well for things like this. 
 
Typically aggressive anchors are exact keyword match anchors, like “roofing London” or “roofing 
manchester”.  
 
Then we can also filter in some less aggressive partial keyword match anchors, like “trusted 
roofers in manchester” or “roofers”. 
 
General rules: 
 
10-15 ranking anchors -​ Don’t have more than 10-15 targeting one ranking page targeting your 
niche keyword. Also, aim for 70% aggressive anchors and 30% non-aggressive anchors). For 
example, if your homepage is targeted towards “roofers Manchester”, I would have 6 or 7 
relatively exact match variations, like: 
 
“Manchester roofers“ x2 
“Roofers Manchester“ x2 
“Manchester roofer“ x2 
 
Then I would have partial keyword match variations like: 
 
“Trusted roofer in manchester“ 
 


 
 
‘“Roofer“ 
“Local Manchester roofers“ 
“Best roofing company” 
“Trusted roofer”  
 
If you ever suspect penalization for your internal anchors, you can test and reduce your 
anchors’ quantity and see if it makes a difference. Also, be mindful of your links from other 
entities and what anchors are being used. 
 
 
1 link per page - ​When creating support content, be that blog articles or niche/local support 
pages, go for one link per page. This will increase the power of your internal link compared to if 
you tried linking to four different pages at once.  
 
I’ve had really good results in easy niches (KD was in the 10-15 range for main SL keywords) 
without much linking/GMB activity. All I did was use good OnPage laid out in this chapter and 
really aggressive internal linking. See the example below. This site is monetized through pay 
per conversion. Each month a couple of conversion payments come in, ranging anywhere from 
$100 - $1500. 
 
This just goes to show the power of OnPage optimization, content, and internal linking. It’s 
basically enough by itself to dominate the competition in easy niches. Still, I would never want to 
rely on one strategy to rank, but it shows how lacking a lot of local competition if we don’t even 
need to get links or do anything special to dominate the SERPs. 
 
 
 
 


 
 
External Linking 
I generally keep external linking to supporting pages and blog articles. Sure, you can include 
them on ranking pages, but only where it makes sense. 
 
External linking isn’t a particularly powerful tool in our linking strategy as we are linking away 
from our website, but it should be involved to a degree.  
 
By linking to authorities in our niche or local businesses in our target locations, it helps to 
communicate to Google which niche we serve and which areas we operate in.  
 
A good rule to remember for external linking is that the highest link on our page is the strongest 
link on the page. Generally, we want our strongest links to be our internal links as we want to 
keep our ranking juice within our site rather than giving it to anyone else.  
 
Another measure we can take is making sure any external page is opened in a new tab. By 
opening external links in a new tab, it means Google robots (that scan our website) won’t be 
completely pushed off our site. To open a link in a new tab, WordPress should give you an 
option, or you can use code like so: 
 
<a href=”url” target=”_blank”>external link</a> 
 
Another good rule is, if using an external link, don’t use any of your main keywords in the 
anchor. Save those keywords for your internal linking to ranking pages. 
 
As you can see from the dummy blog page, the internal link is right at the top of the page, while 
the external is right at the bottom. 
 
https://localkingdom.net/blog-article-title-do-not-use-ranking-page-keywords/ 
 
Indexing Your Pages 
I use two techniques to index pages. You can use the URL Inspection tool found within Google 
Webmasters to see if a page has indexed. If it isn’t, you can submit a page to be indexed by 
Google. Normally this tool is enough. I go for 7 pages a day, then leave a two day gap before 
submitting any more pages. Even when I’m using 100% duplicate content, this strategy works if 
I submit 7 at a time and leave a two day gap before submitting more. I’ve managed to index 
hundreds of duplicate pages on the same domain with this strategy. 
 
If this still isn’t working, you can use Matt Diggity’s video sitemap trick. Read more about it here: 
https://diggitymarketing.com/quick-indexing-with-this-simple-trick/ 
 


 
 
 
I would start with the URL inspection tool though. 
 
If both of those strategies somehow failed, you can use something like ​https://speed-links.net/​. 
 
If you’re just testing or don’t mind if your site is penalized, speed links could be a good way of 
indexing loads of duplicate location pages all at once. I would avoid this kind of strategy for a 
client site or your own site that you’ve put a lot of time into.  
 
Optimizing Images 
I’ve somewhat given up on overly optimizing images. The ROI never seemed to be there 
considering how long it takes to do. I’ve tried everything from geotagging, meta descriptions, 
and everything in between. Nothing ever really seemed to have much of an effect. 
 
Now I just name the file name of the image as the keyword that page is targeting a maximum of 
two times. Then for any other images, I use an LSI keyword to add general relevance. 
 
For medium - hard KD niches, I would consider optimizing images, probably even paying for a 
service if my budget allowed it. For easy niches, I definitely wouldn’t bother. 
 
JSON Schema 
JSON is a way of highlighting content to Google that you deem very important. I haven’t seen 
the biggest ranking gains with JSON so I use it precautionarily. Using it incorrectly can hurt your 
strategy for sure though. For example, if you had your NAP in JSON on every page despite 
being a multi-location website. You wouldn’t want to tell Google you are based in Indianapolis 
for a page that is trying to rank in Louisville.  
 
For JSON, I don’t include it on pages that don’t visibly show NAP. If you haven’t written your 
NAP somewhere on the page, don’t include JSON data. 
 
Also, I wouldn’t include NAP JSON data on location pages that are far away from your NAP 
area. This will dilute Google’s understanding of where you are. I will only include JSON data if 
the location page is within a 10-mile radius of my NAP citation. 
 
Sitemap  
Creating a sitemap is pretty straight forward. Download the Yoast plugin, generate your 
sitemap, and submit it to Webmasters.  
 
You can add an HTML link to your footer as it’s a best practice, but I don’t think it’s a huge 
needle mover. Still, it could be worth doing as it’s low effort and might help. 
 


 
 
Local Link Building 
 
If you aren’t new to SEO, you know that links are one of the most important ranking factors out 
there. 
 
Why? 
 
Well, links are one of the hardest metrics to manipulate. 
 
If Moz (or some other authoritative website) links to your website, you could not have done 
anything to make them do so, other than write good content or give them a reason to.  
 
It is not within your power to get a link from Moz without Moz deciding to do so. You can’t ‘take’ 
a link from them. 
 
A link is essentially a vote from another website, endorsing your website or a particular page. 
 
Having high-quality links to your website boosts your DR, making it more authoritative and 
therefore more likely to rank for the keywords you target.  
 
Just look at the Moz pie chart again. Link building is the number one factor influencing organic 
ranking and the second most important factor impacting map pack ranking. 
 
 
 
So of course, link building is going to be an integral part of our local SEO strategy, as it is in 
virtually all SEO verticals. 
 
Although we are attacking our competitors with a holistic strategy - linking is certainly going to 
be one of the main needle movers. 
 
This section will be broken down into several sections. 
 
Let’s learn how we use links in Local SEO for existing sites, new sites and sites targeting 
different levels of KD keywords. Then we can look at different strategies to build our links later in 
the chapter.  
 


 
 
Preparing For Link Building 
If you are working on a pre-existing site, you will want to make sure it is ready to have links built 
to it. Link building is necessary for most SEO strategies, but you should solve any underlying 
issues the website faces before starting your link strategy. Building new links to a site that isn’t 
ready for them might render your efforts useless. This will either be a waste of time (if we do 
organic outreach to get links) or money (if we purchase links).  
 
Let’s always at least get these things in order before creating a link strategy: 
 
1​. ​Indexing​ ​- ​First ensure the site is indexed and healthy. Make sure the site has a good 
amount of relevant content across multiple pages on the website. Simply check out your 
main competitors (search ‘site:[their website]’) and assess how many pages and content 
they have across their site. If your content is very thin compared to theirs, it’s worth adding 
fresh content to the site to get it in a similar range as the competition if not more. Also, 
make sure you don’t have keyword cannibalization going on, which lots of pre-existing sites 
do. 
 
2. Penalties​ ​- ​If the site you're working on is riddled with penalties, trying to boost it up with 
links will likely not improve your standings. Deal with any penalty issues before investing in 
links. 
 
3. Existing links - ​You will need to analyze your existing links to decipher whether to add 
more now or not. A previous SEO or employee may have sent a spam link farm to your 
website, meaning you need to disavow any bad links that may be sandboxing or penalizing 
you. Go onto your Google Webmasters in order to assess the links your website has. The 
disavow tool can also be found here.  
 
4. Anchor text -​ If the site has already abused keyword anchor text (like spamming service 
+ location) throughout their whole Google entity, we might want to reduce this. Especially if 
the same anchor text is being repeated. This is a common mistake that spammed 
sites/entities have. We would probably still have a positive impact if we add links to a 
spammy site, but it’s worth reducing the amount of ‘exact match’ anchor texts throughout 
the entire entity before building links. 
 
To reduce internal linking keyword anchor spam, simply mix up the anchor text used. Avoid 
using the same anchor text over and over again, but keep the anchor relevant to the page 
you are linking to and the keyword that page is trying to rank for. 
 
 


 
 
For external links pointing to your site, you can do the same, but also throw in branded 
anchor text for variation. Use Ahrefs to see where your site is getting links from and assess 
the anchor text used. 
 
Get the fundamentals correct using the OnPage chapter, then we can look at building links. 
Competitor Analysis 
Now that our site is ready for link building, we want to do a competitor analysis to find out 
exactly what we need to do in order to outrank them in the SERPs. 
 
Remember, we want to analyze and improve on what the best competitors in the SERPs are 
doing. These competitors are the best in Google’s eyes (before we overtake them), so we want 
to model and improve on their approach, not do something totally random that Google doesn’t 
appreciate. 
 
Let’s say our main competitors have an average of 40 links. We wouldn’t start chucking 20 links 
a month from day one. Sure, the strategy might work, but it also could be strange to Google how 
your site is operating and would probably just be overkill... 
 
The four main factors we want to consider are: 
 
- How many links we want to build 
- The quality of the links we need to build 
- What the anchor text should be 
- The power of our homepage 
 
I use Ahrefs to analyze competitor link profiles. Ahrefs has a free version where you can see 
your competitor’s backlinks but it won’t give you as much detail as the paid version. Still, the 
free version will give you a lot of information (quantity of links, if they’re Do/Nofollow, etc.) and is 
a good resource for a competitor link analysis. If not, use the SEMrush free trial. 
 
 
How many links do I need? 
Although we are analyzing our competitor’s quality of link too, knowing how many they have and 
how many they are gaining each month is a metric worth considering. The reason is that if we 
have 5 high-quality Dofollow links and our competitor has 75 average quality Dofollow links, 
their domain authority might be a lot stronger than ours, simply as their backlink profile is a lot 
denser. 
 
Let’s assume we are working with a fresh domain meaning this strategy would work for a brand 
new client site or your own lead generation asset.  
 
 


 
 
If working with an aged client site, simply perform the optimizations above, then alter the 
number of links you need to acquire based on how many links the site has when you start 
managing it.  
 
Let’s say our 5 key competitors have an average of 100 links, they have been established for 1 
year and they gain 5 links a month (using basic numbers to illustrate the point).  
 
In order to match their link quantity in 1 year, we need to add roughly 13 links a month.  
 
1 year is a bit too long to get even with our competitor’s links.  
 
In harder niches, you might resound to building links across a year to become competitive 
because the competition just has too many links for you to build.  
 
3 months or less could be too fast and unnatural, while a year or over is a bit too long.  
 
Aiming to be even in a 6-month window would be better. 
 
Remember, linking is one of the most important ranking factors, so we don’t want to elongate 
dominating our competition for years. At the same time, we don’t want to blitz our site with tons 
of links in the first month. 
 
Back to the example.  
 
Let’s say our competition has 100 links. 90 of these are citation style links that generate a 
Nofollow link. A Nofollow link does not directly pass ranking juice to the site it is linking to. 
However, you will see in the citations section of this chapter that these links are still important 
for Local SEO. 
 
It’s a good idea to understand the types of links our competitors have. If they had 90 Dofollow 
links and we emulated their link quantity with 90 Nofollow links, we will likely be disappointed.  
 
As ever, emulation first. 
 
Now let’s say the other 10 remaining links they have are Dofollow and therefore passing link 
juice through to our competitor site. 
 
So in 6 months, we want at least 90 citation style Nofollow links and 10 Dofollow links. 
 
We can start the site by adding 25 high quality, local/niche relevant citations. This leaves 
roughly 60 citations to become even with our competitors. That means creating around 10-15 
citation style Nofollow links a month.  
 
 


 
 
We can also kickstart the new campaign with a couple of Dofollow links in the first month, then 
drip 1-2 Dofollow links a month.  
 
Within 6 months, we are on an even playing field with our competitors, or even beating them in 
terms of quantity of links.  
Quality of Links 
Arguably a more important metric is the quality of links we acquire. 
 
Some Local SEOs swear by quality and that we only need a handful of top quality links to 
dominate a niche.  
 
While this could be true, I still prefer my holistic approach of all major ranking factors, while 
emulating the highest-ranked competitors’ link approach, which leaves less to chance. 
 
If we have at least as many links as the competition, as well as having higher quality links, it’s 
hard for us to not be beating them in this department. 
 
Thankfully a lot of easy difficulty niches will have highly unsophisticated link profiles. For easy 
niches, you might really only need a few high-quality links to be outperforming everyone in your 
niche. Because the competition isn’t actively doing anything to improve their SEO, this also 
means you won’t need much of an ongoing link strategy, either. This is why easy niches are 
great. Client work becomes easy (but still valuable) and lead generation assets can be more 
passive compared to harder niches that require ongoing optimization. 
 
But what makes a quality local link?  
 
Well, even though we are working with Local SEO here, authority (or DR) is a major 
consideration. Getting a link from Moz is going to be way more significant than a link from a 
local blogger. 
 
Even Nofollow links from highly authoritative sites (think Wikipedia) will give our brand a lot of 
trust in the eyes of Google. 
 
So as ever, the DR of the site linking to us is still a major consideration.  
 
The next most important factors are if the link is niche or geographically relevant to our site. 
 
Remember, not only are we trying to get links from high DR sites (generally quite tricky), but we 
also want to build prominence, relevance, and proximity. Niche and locally relevant links directly 
communicate to Google that we are all three of those things. 
 
 


 
 
We also want to avoid spam links, where a website has relatively high DR, but they sell dofollow 
links to 100s of people a day, thus each link passing less and less juice, making the link not 
particularly valuable.  
 
Rare links from authoritative, local websites in your niche are really high-quality links. 
 
Another factor is how strong our competitor backlinks are. Yes, we can see the DR of their links 
and how niche/locally relevant they are - but how many links are going to the page that is linking 
to our competitor’s page?  
 
This is called tiered linking. You can boost your links by linking to the page that is linking to your 
website. I would expect tiered linking to be a strategy used in most hard niches, the occasional 
medium difficulty niche, and virtually never in easy niches. But certainly, you should have a look 
at this as we want to be aware of all factors at play. 
 
So now we gauge the quality of our competitor’s linking. Get a good feel for how good their links 
are and know that your strategy is to outperform their links mostly through quality, but also with 
regards to quantity. For a recap, these are the factors we are assessing and wanting to 
outperform them on: 
 
- How authoritative their links are 
- How niche/location relevant their links are 
- If they are manufacturing tiered linking (usually hard niches only)  
 
The next factor we can analyze is anchor text. Anchor text is simply the text used within a link. 
Anchor text is powerful because it communicates to Google what the entity we are linking to is 
about.  
 
We don’t need to worry too much about anchor text distribution for easy - medium KD keyword 
niches, but it’s something I would definitely consider in harder niches.  
 
In Local SEO we are usually dealing with a lot fewer links, so something like anchor text 
distribution becomes less relevant than many affiliate SEO sites that have hundreds if not 
thousands of links. 
 
Even still, I would at least look to see if there is a correlation worth considering for medium-hard 
KD keyword niches. 
 
We can use ​https://app.anchorprofiler.com/​ to see how often our competitors are using 
keywords, related words, LSI words, branded terms in their linking anchor text. We can follow a 
similar percentage distribution from all of our linking efforts.  
 
 


 
 
Again, you won’t need to do this in easy niches, perhaps in medium niches but definitely in hard 
niches. In general, a relatively even distribution between the different types of anchor text 
should see us do just fine as we generally aren’t competing against thousands of links. 
Assessing anchor text is getting really granular and I wouldn’t worry about this if you aren’t 
targeting really strong competition.  
 
 
Homepage power 
I already mentioned this above: “The reason is that if we have 5 high-quality Dofollow links and 
our competitor has 75 average quality Dofollow links, their domain authority might be a lot 
stronger than ours, simply as their backlink profile is a lot denser.” 
 
Let’s explore the issue in a little more detail.  
  
Something to understand about linking in SEO is that we can either link to our homepage, or a 
specific page we want to rank, or even pages that are pointing at pages we want to rank. 
 
If our competitors have a high DR, we will likely need to raise our DR in order to compete with 
them. Although there’s no exact science behind how close your DR needs to be to a competitor, 
a good general rule of thumb is that we don’t want to be 10-20+ behind in DR, especially if we 
are directly competing for multiple keywords in a location or niche. 
 
For affiliate SEO, DR can be less of an issue as you can compete with huge sites with 60/70+ 
DRs, but these websites aren’t as relevant to the keywords you’re targeting. This means it’s not 
uncommon to outperform 60/70+ DR websites because of how broad all the different topics are. 
However, in local, there are only so many searches within a geographical location that a website 
will be targeting. This means we can’t be too outgunned on the DR front and still outrank our 
competitors…  
 
A good way of raising our DR is sending our initial links to the homepage. 
 
This will help the overall DR of the site increase so we can compete with our higher DR 
competitors. 
 
A benefit of doing this is a decent percentage of our SL keywords are likely to rank through our 
homepage anyway, especially in the primary area our website operates from. 
 
An added benefit of boosting our homepage is adding any new pages will gain ranking benefits 
because of our improved DR. A website with high DR adding a new page will rank better than a 
website with 0 DR. 
 
 


 
 
We can also balance out our anchor profile by making these initial links to our homepage have 
mostly branded anchors. When we purchase or gain links in the coming months/future, we can 
optimize the anchor text towards our target keywords, thus helping us rank for those keywords. 
 
Although we don’t need to worry too much about anchor text distribution in the easy niches, I 
would still avoid spamming SL keywords and other variations for all of our anchor text. So 
building branded anchor text links at the start of the campaign will not only see us overtake 
competitor DR, we will have a nicely balanced link profile, improve any new page ranking and 
also boost up a page that will be ranking for profitable keywords anyway. 
 
 
Linking to individual pages 
In local, our ranking pages are generally going to be our homepage and location pages which 
target SL keywords (whether for one location or multiple locations) - or our service pages and 
sometimes blog pages. 
 
When looking at our target keywords, we should assess how powerful competitor individual 
pages are, not just our competitor’s overall DR.  
 
Although building DR is a better long term investment (as all new pages associated with that 
domain will perform better), it’s important to also consider individual page authority.  
 
Analyze specific pages your competitor ranks for the keywords you want to dominate.  
 
Thankfully again, most easy-medium niches won’t have many links to individual ranking pages. 
They will mostly have links only to their homepage.  
 
The reason? They aren’t investing in their SEO, and no one else online has a reason to link to 
any of their specific service pages because they’re generic - just like everyone else’s generic 
service/location pages. 
 
This makes it easy for us to power up specific location pages or service pages we are trying to 
rank above the competition.  
 
For the harder niches, you’ll need to analyze the link profile of specific pages you are trying to 
outrank and outperform them from a quality/quantity perspective. 
 
 
Before looking at how to build local links, let’s summarize this section. In order to outperform 
and dominate our competitor's link strategy, we need to understand: 
 
- DR of main competitors  
 


 
 
- Quality and quantity of links required 
- The timescale we should be building these links across  
- The quantity and quality of links going to specific ranking pages 
 
Now that we understand these factors, let’s get some links!  
Building Local Links 
There are endless ways of building links in Local SEO. The strategies you focus on are going to 
depend on you, your resources, your skills, the client you’re working with or if you’re working on 
a lead generation asset. Use the strategies that make the most sense for you and your 
individual circumstances.  
 
Now that we know the quantity and quality of links we are targeting, let’s keep everything 
organized by creating a spreadsheet with our link schedule. 
 
In this spreadsheet, we can create a clear path/record of our link building process. 
 
Within the spreadsheet, you can add how many links you need, where you’re building the link, 
the cost of the link, type of link, what the anchor text will be, and so on. You can also leave 
space for any additional notes that might be relevant to your link building strategy.  
 
By planning our link schedule in advance, it helps to keep us organized and leaves a clear 
record of where we are in the link building process for our campaign. 
 
We will start with Dofollow links below. Dofollow links are generally harder to acquire as we 
need to be linked to organically, or pay someone to link to our website. 
 
Then we will dive into Nofollow links and citations, generally built on directories and social 
media accounts which we can control. 
 
Both are very important, but let’s tackle the ‘harder’ links first… 
 
Dofollow Links 
Let’s go through different strategies to gain valuable Dofollow links... 
 
Steal competitor links - ​Start by analyzing your top competitor backlink profiles again. 
 
If we manage to get our top competitor’s Dofollow links as well as our own high-quality local 
links, we’ll be in a sweet position. 
 
See which websites are linking to them and try to understand, if possible, how they got that link. 
 
 


 
 
Do they have links from local bloggers, newspapers, authorities, event organizers, groups, and 
so on? 
 
Start digging into these lead sources and see if you can replicate the reason your competitor got 
that link. 
 
There could be some good low hanging lead fruit in the community that is only an email, phone 
call or in-person visit away. 
 
I find this is just an easy way to start any local lead building campaign.  
 
Before we try strategies listed later which involve expenses or manual labor, let’s try and find 
some easy leads first.  
 
Local Gold Mine Area 
In Local SEO, it helps to be… Local! It helps to have your very own Gold Mine Areas. You’re a 
person, right?  
 
Well, you know people or businesses where you live or a location you’ve spent loads of time in.  
 
Family. Friends. Clients. Groups. Organizations. Event holders. Businesses. Clubs. 
Associations. Loads of people you know locally have websites that would grant us a brilliant, 
local backlink.  
 
… And all these people YOU know… Know other people too!  
 
Yes, we can leverage our real-world experience in our Gold Mine Area to get local, prominent, 
relevant, and rare links. 
 
Getting high-quality Dofollow links can be a chore. Links are one of the most important ranking 
factors because they’re hard to manipulate. 
 
So if we can get a high-quality link just by calling up a friend, that beats paying $100 for it or a 
whole load of outreach. 
 
We want to make everything as easy as possible - while still taking the necessary action to 
dominate the competition.  
 
Generally, these local links will be valuable because they’re locally relevant and probably aren’t 
being distributed to other websites (a lot of links people offer online have thousands of links 
going out from the site, thus dissipating the strength of each link).  
 
 


 
 
Some people will simply give you a link because they know it doesn’t cost them anything and it 
will help you… 
 
Whereas others might need a bit more persuading.  
 
As ever when trying to get your way, simply show them your proposition is valuable.  
 
Offer them something you’re happy to offer in return for a link. This could be a link back, some 
work on their website, or perhaps some other thing you can offer them through the personal 
connection you’ve already built. 
 
You should also leverage the ability to meet people face to face. Build real connections with 
local bloggers, journalists, and industry experts that could be an endless source of valuable 
leads for years.  
 
Use this approach for client projects but also your own lead generation websites.  
 
It can’t be overstated how valuable it is to build up a real network of website owners where you 
have loads of clients or lead generation assets. It will make your life 100x easier in this industry. 
Also, some connections will have websites in different areas to help you with other projects, as 
well as more national sites that can give you a valuable link wherever one of your local sites are. 
 
Client gold mine area - ​Help your clients get quality local leads through your network and the 
people you know. More importantly, get them to think about their local goldmine area.  
 
Many local businessmen have lived in that location for years and years. They know endless 
other local businesses, they have suppliers, partners, friends, customers, people that owe them 
a favor, and so on. Make them rack their brains about all the possible link building opportunities 
they could get.  
 
Sure, you might have to do some of the outreach for them or manage the process, but 
leveraging their actions as a local business owner is going to be easier than imagining you have 
to build links from scratch. 
  
I really find that a client’s close business friends, partners, and suppliers are easy sources of 
links because they want your client to do well, and adding a link to their site costs them no 
money and little time. 
 
You should Google their brand to see if they already have mentions on the internet where you 
can request a link added. Be creative and think about your client’s footprint in the locale already 
and how you can leverage it to get links!  
 
 


 
 
Lead generation gold mine area - ​The same theory applies to your lead generation assets. 
Leverage your local community to pick up valuable, relevant, rare local links.  
 
A word of warning on local linking. If you made 5 lead generation websites on the same server, 
don’t link them to each other.  
 
Google recognizes any inter-server linking between your sites, so it will yield no benefit at best 
and likely hurt your ranking.  
 
Of course, you could put your lead generation assets within the same niche/location on different 
servers and link them together… 
 
But I prefer not to link my sites together. Diversification is the name of the game when we’re 
doing anything remotely ‘black hat’ like making loads of lead generation assets. 
 
If Google did ‘crackdown’ on us (unlikely if your lead generation asset is made to look like any 
normal site), we don’t want everything being penalized at once.  
 
Having lots of sites at your disposal in one area is a superpower. Be it through friends, your 
connections or your own clients and lead generation assets, amassing websites that can power 
up your ventures will only help you make more money.  
 
I have this exact set up in a city in England. Addresses for GMBs, clients in the same city, 
several similar niche lead generation assets, friends with websites, family with websites, good 
relations, and people I can call on for links.  
 
This isn’t to brag, but simply to point out a benefit you can create for yourself in this industry.  
This whole section should serve as a reminder that having as many clients and lead generation 
assets within similar states and cities is a powerful tool for our local link building arsenal. 
 
Write Linkable Content  
Writing excellent content is a great way to gain links from other websites. You can either choose 
to write something locally relevant or niche relevant. 
 
As part of your Local SEO strategy will include blog writing anyway, it makes sense to curate at 
least a few high-level articles that stand a chance to be shared in your community or niche. 
 
I generally prefer locally relevant content because there are potential local buyers who read the 
content and can become familiar with the brand. 
 
 
 
 


 
 
Researching the topic - ​An easy way to find locally relevant topics is by using Reddit. 
 
Simply type in your state, city, or target location into Reddit and a whole load of people will be 
talking about local topics of interest. 
 
If I go onto the reddit.com/r/Oxford Reddit page right now, I can see there are several people 
asking about different areas in Oxford. 
 
Perhaps you could create an article like: “What It’s Like To Live In Every Area Of Oxford- Ask 
The Locals”  
 
This would be a great topic for a real estate business.  
 
More people are talking about when certain parts around the city open up after COVID. 
 
An ongoing article that detailed when different businesses, locations, points of interest, gyms, 
etc. are opening after the COVID fiasco would be good. 
 
Ideally, you will write something that relates to your business in some way. However, as your 
client is from the area, that grants you enough authority to speak about the area.  
 
Having a client in your Gold Mine Area will help here because you might already have ideas of 
content the community needs and would share. 
 
Writing the article - ​Content is a necessary tool within our box of SEO tricks.  
 
Not everyone is a writer, but what you write doesn’t have to be word class either.  
 
It just needs to be locally/niche relevant and valuable. 
 
If you or your client do not want to write the article, you will need to hire someone to do it for 
you. 
 
The amount you spend is going to depend on what you want to create. 
 
You can hire high-quality writers with strong testimonials to write you an article for $100-$200. 
 
That price scares most beginners, which means you might have to write the article.  
 
Remember, the article only needs to be relevant and valuable in order for people to share it in 
the community.  
 
 
 


 
 
Sharing the content - ​The first place to post your article is the location/niche specific subreddit 
you got your idea from in the first place. 
 
Don’t be aggressive because nobody likes self-promotion in a public forum like Reddit.  
 
See what people say about your article and make any necessary amendments from their 
feedback. 
 
Now you can share your article in more places - here are some ideas: 
 
- Find people that link to similar pieces of content as yours, then reach out to them. 
- Reach out to local bloggers, influencers, and PR. 
- Use social media (Facebook groups, Twitter, LinkedIn, etc.) to share the article. 
- Reach out to other authorities on similar topics who aren’t necessarily local 
 
A quick note. I don’t like this strategy for lead generation assets as people will be confused by 
the business model. Avoid unnecessary press when operating lead generation assets.  
 
HARO 
HARO stands for help a reporter out (helpareporter.com) 
 
The premise of HARO is they connect you with journalists who need extra input for their content 
from credible sources. 
 
Starting at $19/per month, HARO sends you article topics for you to comment on or write about.  
 
If you submit high-quality work before the deadline, you can get a mention (unstructured 
citation) and link to your website. 
 
HARO is one of those strategies that CAN be great.  
 
It’s especially useful when you have a client who is a passionate writer or has a passionate 
writer on their team. 
 
It can be tricky when your client doesn’t have any writers, nor do they want to write anything. It’s 
hard to be a professional writer for all your clients across all their niches. 
 
However, you could use it to write about digital marketing and Local SEO for your own 
agency…  
 
Being aware of HARO and if it suits your strategy is worthwhile. Again, this could be a great 
source of links for the client with a content writer or who loves writing. 
 


 
 
 
If you’re a keen writer yourself, it could be a great source of leads for your own agency. 
 
HARO is also useful if your client is not in your Gold Mine Area and you have no local 
connections. 
 
In an ideal world we would build up more personable relationships, but HARO is a good 
alternative if we aren’t able to do that, nor is our client willing to do that.  
 
HARO can be a safe strategy for a lead generation asset. Most of the time we just get a quick 
mention with our link, so we don’t need to worry about a huge influx of suspecting traffic.  
 
Guest posting 
Probably the most popular link building strategy of all time, guest posting, is very relevant to 
Local SEO too. 
 
A guest post usually involves you writing a blog article for another website, then getting a link 
back to your site for your efforts.  
 
Guest posts are a simple way to get Dofollow backlinks for your campaign. 
 
We can either organically reach out to bloggers and website owners, or you can pay for a guest 
post from a link vendor. 
 
Guest posting as a whole has been written about to death so I don’t want to rewrite it here. 
 
Backlinko wrote the definitive guide on guest posting and built his name as an SEO authority 
through guest blogging.  
 
https://backlinko.com/the-definitive-guide-to-guest-blogging 
 
Check the article out, there is lots to be gained, but let’s add a couple of local spins to his 
tactics.  
 
Finding guest post targets: ​Backlinko gives us search strings to find bloggers looking for 
guest posts on Google and Twitter. Instead of using his [niche] search, you can search for [your 
location] instead of the niche. This will help us to find local writers who want guest posts, not just 
niche relevant websites. 
 
Pitching & getting guest posts ready ​When ‘warming up’ or pitching your guest post 
opportunities,  the strategies given assume we aren’t near the people running these websites. 
However, if we’ve searched for local guest post opportunities, we or our clients might be near. 
 


 
 
This allows us to give an even more personable ‘warm-up’ or pitch by calling or visiting them in 
person. Guest post targets respond differently to businesses 20 minutes away from them 
compared to emails they might receive from 20,000 miles away.  
 
Another manual approach we can use is by searching on the Content Explorer tab in Ahrefs and 
use search strings like: [niche] + blogger/blog/article or [location] + blogger/blog/article. This will 
bring up loads of relevant guest post prospects that you can outreach to locally or through 
Brian’s methods at Backlinko. Look for blogs that aren’t link farms that could be good guest post 
prospects. 
 
Buying guest posts - ​In general, there should be a good amount of links you can create just 
from the advice so far. However, if you are targeting a difficult niche or are having a hard time 
building links, you can purchase them. 
 
Your options are buying a guest posting service, who do the manual outreach for you, or simply 
buy a guest post on a link vendor’s website. 
 
Link vendors can sway in quality and abruptly stop offering their services. 
 
However, here are some pretty solid choices: 
 
- Get Me Links: ​Charles link building company was acquired by this Scotland based link 
building agency. 
 
- Loveto.link:​ Loveto.link allows you to see the data and pick the niche of the site you get a link 
from. They offer a fast turnaround time if you’re looking fgor quick links. 
 
- Authority Builders: ​Matt Diggity’s guest posting agency, offering a panel but you have to be 
accepted upon review first. 
 
 
Sponsored Link Building 
Sponsored link building is where we reach out to bloggers and offer to pay them to get a guest 
post on their blog. 
 
It’s a strategy that can get you cheap links from highly authoritative websites that don’t sell links 
to other websites. I like this strategy because we can find websites that aren’t selling loads of 
links to other SEOs 
 
Charles has a really good way of doing this en masse. Here’s an excerpt from his blog: 
 
 


 
 
1. You start off by setting up an email outside of your own brand – I suggest using one away 
from your brand, in case you come across a white hat warrior that starts trying to expose you or 
report you to Google for attempting to buy links. 
2. Then gather a list of blogs you want to gain a link from, and start gathering contact 
information – I suggest using PitchBox, as it tends to be the fastest tool on the market. 
3. You then want to carefully craft an email template out to approach these various bloggers 
with offers to purchase links on their blog. 
As an example of a template I have used: 
Hey there, 
I’ve been reading a few of your posts, and they’re really good – Your blog is really in-depth, and 
it just so happens to be in the same industry as us. 
We know it can be really difficult to make any sort of a living as a blogger, and we’d be willing to 
help out your cause and provide some financial support if you could in return provide a guest 
post on your blog for us? 
We aren’t asking you to build links to a spammy site or product page, just a site that’s in the 
same industry as yours, to a blog post we’ve invested 10s of hours creating. 
Thanks 
Charles 
This email alone had about a 40% response rate and we were getting TF10 – TF60 links for 
between $20 and $100. At that price, it’s often cheaper (and a lot safer) to carry out this process 
instead of using PBN links. 
 
Localizing This Strategy  
We can localize this approach by targeting bloggers in the location we’re trying to rank in.  
 
Not only are people in our location more likely to reply to us (especially if you or the client take 
extra measures to meet and build rapport), but we also get a local link, not just a quality link 
from a site that doesn’t sell links. 
 
Yes, you can still try sponsored link building all over the country, but local leads are stronger 
and you can often build better rapport with local people. 
 
 


 
 
If you aren’t where your client is and they aren’t proactive about creating these relationships 
(unfortunately, quite a lot of local clients), you can contact these bloggers on their behalf from a 
company email address or over the phone.  
 
It won’t be an unpleasant experience because you are complementing their website and offering 
them money for something they might not be getting much paid for/if at all. 
 
You’re far less likely to be reported when it’s a more human to human, local interaction too.  
 
Press Releases 
Press releases are a good tool for new sites. 
There’s always debates about press releases and if they are actually beneficial. 
The answer, as usual, depends.  
Using a cheap press release service will see your story distributed across hundreds of spammy 
sites that get press releases on them all day every day.  
This is not a good look for our domain. 
Most press release links aren’t Dofollow these days anyway, so we’re mostly looking for trust 
signals and local relevance. Using a spammy press release will do the opposite. 
A much better strategy is to pick a niche or location relevant website that allows you to do a 
press release. 
Yes, these are going to cost a lot more, but it’s the best way to do press releases these days.  
In an ideal world. Other journalists will pick up the story and potentially earn us backlinks from 
other news sites. Again, if we can achieve this locally rather than just relevant to our industry, 
we could get some excellent links. 
If I was targeting an easy niche, I probably wouldn’t bother with a press release simply because 
you have a lot to be optimizing anyway and we’ll blow the competition away without adding in 
extra measures like this.  
Get Creative 
There are hundreds of local link building ideas online. There’s no need to list all the rest of them 
here. 
 
Anything from creating events, running seminars, hosting a show, and so on is a chance to 
create a local link.  
 
 


 
 
The strategy you use is going to depend on your client, you, and what is easiest to achieve and 
gives the best results!  
 
Quick Dofollow Summary 
Overall, our easiest and best source of Dofollow leads should come from yours or your client’s 
network. 
 
The network should give you plenty of targets for potential link building opportunities.  
 
Think about it. 
 
If your client is in a 15 KD keyword niche, the main competitors have 5 good backlinks on 
average…  You only need to get links from a few suppliers, partners, friends, and family in the 
area to be equal or dominating the very best competitors in your location… That’s not hard. 
 
Then you can slowly build your local link profile organically over time as your client sees the 
value of what you’re doing.  
 
For some medium difficulty and all high difficulty niches, I would expect to be using the other 
techniques to build more Dofollow links. 
 
Our competitors will simply have too many good links for us to outrank them through our current 
network, usually. 
 
Use a mixture of the techniques above, other local link building strategies (suited to your 
client/niche) and paid guest posts, sponsored or manual outreach links to achieve the quality 
and quantity of links required to compete in the niche. 
 
While there’s nothing wrong with paid links, getting geographically relevant links from sites that 
don’t link to loads of others is really powerful. 
 
This is why I would always lean towards local organic links if you can. 
 
However, in hard niches, the sheer velocity and strength of your competitors will likely mean 
investing in paid links each month.  
 
Beginners should definitely get involved with local outreach strategies to gain an appreciation 
for link building. Link building is paramount for all types of SEO after all.  
 
Now that we’ve covered how to get Dofollow links, let’s talk about local citations and Nofollow 
directory links. 
 
 


 
 
Dofollow links, Nofollow directory links, and citations are all needed to form a holistic link 
building strategy. 
Citations  
The basic definition of a citation is mentioning a business’s name, address, and phone number, 
an acronym of “NAP.” A citation is generally left on directory websites with a Nofollow link back 
to our website. 
 
A rough ranking value estimate for citations is provided by Moz, estimating a 10% factor for the 
map pack and 8% factor for the organic listings. 
 
Google crawls the NAP information we put out onto the internet. Places we can leave citations 
are local directories, niche relevant directories, social media accounts, press releases, blog 
articles, and loads more. 
 
Any NAP citations we leave on directory type websites are considered to be structured citations. 
It’s important to keep these consistent for Google to trust your brand. More on this in the citation 
clean up section. 
 
When leaving a structured citation on any given entity, use the exact address details used in 
your GMB if it displays there. 
 
 
 
If you don’t have an address on your GMB, find out the exact address of your location so you 
can use it for citations. 
  
 


 
 
Unstructured citations are simply mentions of any part of our NAP in a news article, blog, or 
anywhere else on the internet. We or other sources might also cite any other entity that relates 
to our brand (GMB website, Google Maps URL, etc.) 
  
The more high-quality brand mentions we can accrue for a campaign, the better the brand’s 
prominence becomes. As we know, prominence as a key ranking factor in Local SERPs. 
Building consistent citations also act as trust signals in the eyes of Google, which is a ranking 
factor that most SEOs seem to ignore. 
 
Which Citations Should We Build? 
 
We want a combination of: 
 
- Taking all our competitor citations and adding more, higher quality citations 
- Niche relevant citations 
- Location relevant citations 
- Other unstructured mentions of our brand entities, like mentioning any component of our NAP, 
your Google CID or Google Maps listing URL, etc. 
 
As usual, you start by looking at your competitors, reverse engineering their approach, and 
improving on it. 
 
Do a citation analysis of the competition using Ahrefs or your preferred software. 
 
Have a look at all the main directories your competition is getting citations from. While they 
might have citations on non-directory sites, virtually all of their NAP citations and citations as a 
whole will likely mostly be on directory sites. 
 
List all the citations used by your top competitors and add them to a spreadsheet. 
This could be from 50-250+ citation opportunities depending on the difficulty of the niche you 
are competing in. 
 
I would then go through the list and roughly order them in terms of expected citation value.  
The metrics we can base citation value are: 
 
- Likelihood of indexation (some sites index our listings easily, some don’t). 
- How geo and or niche relevant the directory/website is to our brand. 
 
After compiling what looks like the best citations our competition is using, we can begin our hunt 
for other directories, newspaper websites, and more to build citations from. Again, we’re looking 
for geo and niche relevant directories, not just any old directories.  
 
Add these additional websites into the spreadsheet (sticking to our chronological order). 
 


 
 
When doing this for a client, you can go through and remove any citations they already have 
from the list.  
 
For brand new domains, I would create a plan to add the citations you need over time.  
If our top competitors all had around 50 citations, I would launch the campaign and do about 25 
citations to start off with. Then I would drip 25 more citations each month for the next few 
months. 
 
Just blasting 100 citations to a fresh domain will most likely look unnatural, considering your 
competitors likely picked up theirs over the course of several years. 
 
While I don’t think we will be penalized, citations in and of themselves aren’t going to make us 
rank top overnight… So we might as well add them in a natural way. 
Directories 
Considering we want to add more/higher quality citations than our competitors, we’ll need to find 
directories our competitors aren’t in to add citations to. 
 
The best directories (and hopefully our competitors aren’t utilizing them) are main directories like 
Apple Maps and Bing Places. Fill these out in as much detail as possible. Although we mostly 
focus on our GMB in this book, you should definitely be investing some time into these other 
major entities. If I was creating my own lead generation asset, I would personally fill these in 
without loads of detailed optimizations, then if the asset is working through organic and the map 
pack (meaning we know we can pick up valuable leads in this market), I would eventually pour 
some time into optimizing other channels like Bing Places.  
 
To reiterate, we want relevant or local directories. Avoid low-quality link farm style directories 
that are rife with spam and rubbish. These are often generic international business directories 
rather than local/niche specific directories. Also generally avoid foreign directories. This is Local 
SEO after all. 
 
There are loads of paid directory options but we don’t need to worry about those much. Free 
listings usually are just as good as the paid ones. 
 
Side note, a nice benefit of leaving citations on directory sites is another source of balancing our 
Dofollow/Nofollow link profile as most of the links we get are Nofollow.  
 
SEMrush has a feature that gives you link building ideas based on your competition. It’s a link 
building suggestion tool, but lots of your competition will likely have links from local directories, 
so it’s a good way of finding more local citation opportunities to add to your spreadsheet. Make 
the most of the SEMrush 30-day free trial if you’re on a budget. 
 
 


 
 
Here are some search queries to find more local/niche relevant directories: 
 
[location] business listings 
[location] list my business 
[location] free directory 
[industry] directory 
[niche] directory 
[industry] directory [location] 
[industry] listing [location] 
[location] [industry] council/association/group/society 
 
You get the idea. If you run out of search strings, look online for more as countless people have 
written about finding high-quality directories. 
 
Indexing Your Citations 
While some sites index our pages with citations on with no extra effort, some sites require more 
work.  
 
It’s not a complete waste of time to create a citation on a directory/website that is hard to get 
indexed. Sure, we might not get our own listing page, but we still might get onto the directory 
site’s list of all businesses on another page. So, even if we struggle to get a citation page 
indexed, it’s not always the end of the world. Even still, we are looking to get all of these 
citations indexed if possible. 
 
Citations aren’t the largest ranking factor to manipulate, but it’s worth checking you have a 
respectable amount of citation pages indexed (and ideally, more/better citations than the 
competition) after you first create them.  
 
Let’s look at how we can get these citation pages indexed! 
 
Phone calls -​ Some websites will call you after you add your citation there to verify you are a 
real person. In order to get your citation indexed, you should take the call and let them know 
you’re real. Beware, these calls are often just sales calls. Be polite, make sure they have listed 
you and be firm that you don’t want any of their upsells. These calls should take a few minutes. 
 
Verify through email - ​Lots of directories/websites will email you to verify you are a real 
person. Check your junk folder and make sure you click verify for every email you get.  
 
Unique content​ ​- ​Some directories and websites give you the chance to write a description 
about your business. Write a unique description each time to enhance the chance of your 
citation getting indexed. For the higher quality citation opportunities, I would write more words 
and for the lower quality citation targets on your sheet, I would invest less effort in the 
descriptions. Specifically, we want to avoid duplicate descriptions.  
 


 
 
 
Paid service - ​Use a paid indexing service like Omega Indexer. It’s pretty affordable, you pay 
$20 minimum for credits, then this $20 gets you 1000 links. You should have a pretty good 
index success rate using Omega. I have also had success with speed-links.net which is cheap. 
 
Link to your listing pages - ​You can use your own entities to link to the page your citations are 
on to help it index. Use Google posts, social channels, the bottom of blog articles, and so on. 
You could add a brand page to your website that links to every single directory, social media 
account, and Google entity. I would personally just use a paid service to try and save time sifting 
through blog articles and linking to different pages when the paid services get you indexed most 
of the time. 
  
Unstructured citations - ​While outperforming our competition on all their structured NAP 
citations is usually going to be enough for our citation strategy, it could be worth adding a 
handful of unstructured citations too. Obviously, if you see the competition has a lot of 
unstructured citations, you should at least be matching them if not outperforming them. I would 
be surprised to see many easy-medium niches with loads of unstructured citations, though. 
Some niches will, most won’t. 
 
Competition - ​When we did our citation competitor analysis, you may have seen some 
unstructured citations (an example would be a brand mention and website link from a local 
blogger). Hopefully you already added that blog as a link prospect to your spreadsheet. 
If you didn’t, now is the time to note down any non-structured citation opportunities to your sheet 
that the competition has. 
 
See in which context their unstructured citation was used. Why have they been mentioned? 
Emulate the way they got an unstructured citation mention. 
 
Was it because they invited the local blogger to their business and gave them great service? 
You can do the same!  
 
It might be that we can’t get every single unstructured citation our competition has, so here are 
some more ideas to get unstructured citations. 
 
Use your suppliers - ​Many suppliers will list the businesses they supply to on their website to 
show credibility. Use search strings like these: 
 
Site:yoursupplier.com intitle:”stocklists” 
Site:yoursupplier.com intitle:”suppliers” 
Site:yoursupplier.com intitle:”clients” 
 
And any other variations that might be relevant to your business. Of course, you could always 
call them to mention your site, you can do the same for them. Leverage partnerships you or 
 


 
 
your client has. This method was included in the link building section, but we should use it as a 
chance to get as many unstructured citations too. 
 
HARO - ​In the aforementioned HARO technique, the journalist will usually give us an 
unstructured mention as well as the nice Dofollow link at the same time. 
 
Press releases​ - If we purchase a press release at the start of a campaign, we will likely get an 
unstructured citation name mention in the press release article.  
Building Your Citations 
It’s time to build your citations. You have a few options available to you. Let’s look at each of 
them.  
 
Manually -​ Building citations is really simple. We just sift through our citation spreadsheet and 
add consistent NAP details to each website/directory with a description. If you’re relatively new 
to all of this, it’s worth adding at least some citations yourself. You gain the experience of how 
these sites operate and can rest easy knowing you did them correctly. Use Chrome autofill or 
similar to save you time for each citation. To save time with each description, I would copy and 
paste the last description used and mix up the sentences so it’s unique content. 
 
Use a VA -​ If you’re trying to save money but also speed up the process, you can send a 
spreadsheet to a Virtual Assistant with the citations you want then to create. There are loads of 
‘citation specialists’ on sites like UpWork or Fiverr who do a good enough job as citation building 
is a straightforward task. 
 
Pay for a citation service -​ In my opinion, this is your best bet. Use a service like SEO Builder 
(​https://www.seobuilder.com/citation-building-packages/​). They will give you a mix of generic, 
niche and location relevant citations - as well as index them. All their packages offer a citation 
audit too. 
 
Citation aggregators - ​There are citation aggregators like Factual and Foursquare. You sign up 
with them and they distribute your citations across a multitude of platforms. While it probably 
won’t hurt to use aggregators, I prefer to reverse engineer our competition. If it looks like they 
have used any of the top aggregators, we might want to too. 
 
While correlation doesn’t equal causation, in the interest of reverse engineering those 
dominating the SERPs, I wouldn’t be against using a citation aggregator. 
  
  
 


 
 
Citation Auditing & Clean up 
It’s important to keep our citations consistent across the internet. Google will crawl our citations 
and will notice inconsistencies.  
 
As ever, I think being precise with your citations is more of a factor for the medium-difficult KD 
keyword niches rather than the easy KD niches. 
 
I have some really amateur lead generation assets I made a couple of years ago where we 
changed the name of the business, the address and all sorts. The site still ranks top for the best 
SL keyword without changing any citations across the internet. This again teaches us that no 
one thing is usually responsible for dominating the SERPs. It’s a combination of multiple factors 
working in unison. The harder the niche, the higher quality and more of each ranking factor we 
require. 
 
So although SEOs harp on about needing 100% consistent citations, context matters. When 
your competition is all 100% consistent with their citations, with loads of them being high-quality 
citations, sure, we might be penalized. 
 
But it’s my opinion that a few inconsistencies here and there aren’t going to drastically harm our 
campaign either way.  
 
Even still, it doesn’t take serious effort to do a citation clean up, so we might as well do it for any 
clients or sites that we take on. 
 
The Process 
Let’s take a manual approach with this. Sure, there are loads of citation scanners out there, but 
half the time you’ll just get a sales call offering a clean up service and they probably didn’t scan 
all of your citations either.  
 
First, let’s decide what our NAP is. I strongly suggest you use the NAP in your GMB. However, if 
you have loads of directories with a different NAP, it might make more sense to edit your GMB 
NAP rather than hundreds of directories. However, if you are already ranking well, I wouldn’t 
edit the GMB as it could hurt rankings, which is the opposite of what we are trying to achieve.  
 
Search for your citations - ​Do a Google search using this query “Business Name - 
site:domain.com”. It could look like this: “MJ Kloss Painting & Decorating Ltd - 
site:https://www.mjkloss.co.uk/”  
 
There should be pages and pages of existing citations for you to sift through and fix 
inconsistencies on. Add all the incorrect citations to a list if you want to outsource this or do it 
later.  
 


 
 
 
When you begin, you will need to find the edit/change/claim listing button for your citation. This 
could require a phone call, email, or some other verification method. 
 
Once you or your VA has access to the listing, you can edit it as necessary. 
 
If I was doing this for the first time, I would do it myself and create a process sheet for VAs to 
complete in the future. If you aren’t new to SEO, you can outsource your citation cleanup either 
to a VA or a company like BrightLocal or similar.  
Social Media Accounts / Web 2.0s 
Social media accounts (or ‘Web2.0s’) seem like a tiny ranking factor, but I and other SEOs 
believe social signals help us to build trust, prominence and relevance throughout our brand.  
 
The point of social accounts isn’t to create a big following and make social media sales. It’s to 
have the accounts with correct NAP, descriptions, images and interlink between all of our 
branded entities (website, GMB website, directory citations, etc.) to boost up our digital 
footprint/online brand. Not every social account will give you the chance to include your other 
social accounts, but some will and you should try to interlink everything. Social accounts also 
give us some nice Nofollow links to balance out our link profile. 
 
While I think this strategy works, I wouldn’t go overboard on it either. 
 
Competitor Research 
As usual, although creating every single social account would help, we don’t want to waste time 
and go into overkill mode. 
 
I would search your top competitors and see which social accounts they have, at least match 
what they have, then add more accounts. 
 
We also want to optimize each account better than our competitors. At least do as much as they 
have on each account if not more (more words in your description, add images, interlink 
between our entities, etc.) 
 
As ever, the amount of work you have to put in here will depend on what KD keyword niche 
you’re targeting. I would expect easy niche competitors to have some social accounts, medium 
niches to have the essentials plus others, then the harder niches to have optimized a lot of 
them. 
 
Here’s a list of essential social accounts that I would make on virtually all Local SEO sites, be it 
for clients or my own lead generation. 
 
 
 


 
 
Youtube 
Facebook 
LinkedIn  
Twitter 
Instagram 
Pinterest 
Tumblr 
WordPress 
Amazon 
Trello 
Medium 
Blogger 
Gravatar 
Buzzfeed 
Quora 
About.me 
Reddit 
 
If you’re in a difficult niche and need even more social account ideas, I would use these 
accounts next: 
 
Slideshare

Building Social Accounts 
For beginners, again, you might want to create some of these yourself to save money, learn the 
process and peace of mind. 
 
A good tool is KnowEm, which scans the internet for any social accounts you already have, as 
well as create social accounts for you. 25 social accounts with KnowEm will cost you around 
$70, which is good value considering how long it will take to set all these accounts up. Ideally, 
we don’t want to run around doing tasks like setting up social media accounts. We should be 
outsourcing these tasks as soon as possible so we can focus on more profitable actions. 
 
Posting To Social Accounts 
Blog writing should be a part of your Local SEO strategy. A way we can multi-purpose our blog 
articles is a strategy called blog syndication network. 
 
Essentially, we will write one blog article, then use software like Hootsuite to publish the blog 
article across all of our social channels. 
 
This is to build more social signals, trust, prominence and relevance throughout our digital 
brand. 
 
At the bottom of each article, we can include links to our other social accounts, as well as things 
like a Google Maps embed, links to directories, etc. 
 
This is a great way of multi-purposing our blog articles and automating the process. 
 
Simply posting to each social media channel for each client or lead generation asset is going to 
be incredibly inefficient.  
 
However, using software like Hootsuite is going to mean we just have to publish a blog article 
once, then it can populate across all our social channels that allow posting. 
 
This, again, is somewhat of an overkill strategy for easy niches. You also might not want to fork 
out for another monthly subscription service while you have a low number of clients of lead 
generation assets, which is understandable. 
 
If I was targeting a medium-hard KD keyword niche, I would definitely be using a blog 
syndication network. No, the blog network alone won’t rank us, but it’s efforts like these 
performed overtime that solidify our brand as the main players in a local niche. Also, our 
 


 
 
competitors are more likely to be taking sophisticated actions like this (especially in hard 
niches), so we’ll be forced to make plays like this anyway. 
YouTube 
While YouTube is its own valuable search engine with lots of money to be made by ranking 
high, most of our target local search queries will be made through Google. That’s not to say that 
you won’t make sales for your local business through ranking high on YouTube, but a higher % 
of sales will likely come as a result of dominating the local Google SERPs. 
 
Still, YouTube is owned by Google and is a powerful tool to help us dominate Local SEO. 
 
We can use YouTube videos to: 
 
- Link to and reference our brand (building trust, prominence & relevance) 
- Actually rank a video in the Google SERPs  
 
Competitor Analysis 
As ever, we should assess what the competition is doing. 
 
Google search the main keywords you’re targeting, as well as analyze your competitor’s 
YouTube accounts to see how much activity they have. 
 
Some Local SERPs have no YouTube videos, nor do your competitors, some are rife with 
videos. 
 
After seeing what the competition is doing, we can outperform their videos with the following 
strategies. Thankfully, I wouldn’t expect to see most competitors using YouTube videos so it’s 
not something we have to do in order to compete. This also gives yet another opportunity to 
leapfrog the competition if they aren’t utilizing YouTube.
