Create higher quality, longer videos. ​This is self-explanatory. Make your videos better than 
theirs and even steal the topics they’ve spoken about. 
 
Create more videos than them. ​If your main competitors have 4 videos on average, start 
adding a YouTube video once a week for a few months. 
 
Use clever titles. ​Keyword stuff your title as naturally as possible. If the competition didn’t have 
many videos, I would make a generic SL style video, plus videos about specific services the 
business provides and or topics relevant to the area (then embed these videos into their 
respective pages). Keep the title under 70 characters long so Google can rank you in the 
SERPs. 
 
 


 
 
Optimize Your Description. ​Start your description with a website link. This is the first thing 
customers will see before clicking ‘see more’, so you might get some traffic. Add your NAP 
citation, as well as a link to your GMB maps URL. Then you write a description relevant to your 
target keywords and location. An easy way to get a few hundred words for your description is to 
transcribe the video you created. If you want to get really detailed, you can do things like adding 
the longitude and latitude of your NAP address. 
 
Settings. ​Go into video settings/advanced settings and optimize that. There will be options like 
tagging and adding keywords. Don’t overdo it, but add the main keywords that your video was 
created for. 
 
Use your blog syndication network.​ After creating a video, we can multipurpose it with our 
blog syndication network. Create a blog article, write a description about your video, then 
embed the YouTube video into the page. Distribute the article across your network and we are 
powering up our online brand without too much additional effort after making the video. 
 
Link to your videos. ​Not only can you use your branded entities to link to your YouTube 
account and videos, but you could purchase links to power them up too. Making your video 
something people want to link to locally/in your niche would be the most ideal way of gaining 
links, but it’s not always possible when working with a client. Either way, getting links to your 
YouTube videos is only going to boost your efforts in either ranking the video or just boosting 
your website + GMB authority in the niche/location you’re targeting. 
 
So, see what the competition is doing and outperform them. 
 
Some niches have absolutely no YouTube videos in them. 
 
We still want to make at least one or two YouTube videos just because of all the great 
interlinking we can do. Also, being the only competitor with well-optimized YouTube videos is 
going to be a needle mover in the SERPs. 
 
Getting clients to make a video (especially in a niche where nobody has bothered with them so 
far anyway) can be a pain. It might fall on you to create the video.  
 
I think YouTube is an easy way to set ourselves apart from the competition and get to the top of 
the SERPs. Don’t avoid it!  
 
Charles is releasing a full YouTube optimization presentation soon, so be sure to grab that for 
really detailed YouTube SEO insights.  
 
 


 
 
Full GMB Optimization 
The GMB is a really brilliant/unique tool in the world of SEO. If used correctly, it can become a 
high powered customer generating machine. 
 
It’s an absolutely vital component of any Local SEO project!  
 
We’re going to look at factors that rank a GMB, creating a GMB, optimizing, lead generation 
GMBs and more! 
Factors That Make A GMB Rank  
We already discussed how proximity, relevance and prominence are the three key ranking 
factors for our GMB, but let’s refresh our memory and go a bit deeper. 
 
It’s our job to manipulate these three factors to gain the highest rankings across the most 
keywords in the most locations possible. 
 
Proximity ​- How far away the searcher is from our GMB’s perceived proximity. Proximity is 
somewhat fixed as you can’t rank in the map pack from hundreds of miles away from a search. 
 
Remember that for a service area business, where we register and get our GMB verified is 
where Google perceives you to operate from. So even if you set your radius to cover a city 
center that is 40 minutes drive from you, Google will class your proximity as stemming from your 
registration point, rather than that city center position you would prefer.  
 
However, we can use strategies to enhance our proximity and cover the widest radius possible. 
 
Relevance - ​Just how relevant is your GMB listing to the specific search that was made. We 
went deep in the keyword research section because we want to focus our relevance on the 
keywords we want to rank for. 
 
Below are what I would expect to have the most local traffic for your GMB, starting from the 
highest: 
 
SL terms: 
Builder Amarillo 
Builders Amarillo 
Amarillo builder  
 
 
 
 


 
 
Local terms: 
Builders near me 
Local builder 
 
Service terms: 
Extension builder Amarillo  
Amarillo bricklayer 
Housebuilder Amarillo  
 
Long tail terms: 
Custom house builder Amarillo 
Commercial property builder Amarillo  
Cheap home renovation specialist  
 
We want to be as relevant as possible to these kinds of terms.  
 
We’ve already looked at ways to become more relevant from a linking and OnPage perspective.  
 
A niche and location relevant link to our website, or using a target keyword like house builder 
Amarillo as a page name for our OnPage strategy would be two examples. 
 
As we delve through this chapter, we’ll be looking at ways to manipulate the creation and 
optimization of our GMB. 
 
Prominence​ ​- ​Prominence refers to how much trust and authority our brand has within our 
niche and location. 
 
This is manipulated by factors like reviews, links, citations, DR, articles & news written about it, 
etc. 
 
Google released this patent which tells us how the algorithm understands prominence: 
https://patents.google.com/patent/US8046371 
 
If you’ve never read this before, it’s worth checking it out. 
 
While we have already spoken about quite a few factors that influence our prominence, there 
are ways to enhance it more below. Reviews even have their own chapter! 
Tackling different difficulty levels 
Before we jump into setting up our GMB and optimizing it, what’s the optimal amount of 
optimizations we should make? 
 
 


 
 
As usual, it depends on your competition. Simply search your top competitor brands and see 
how optimized their GMBs are.  
 
Your competition will have very few optimizations in easy niches. 
 
Ultimately, we’re trying to get into these weak niches because they’re fast and easy to rank in, 
so sinking all of our time into complex GMB optimizations isn’t necessary when the competition 
has such a poor all-around strategy. People in this level of difficulty have low DRs, awful 
OnPage, half-filled out GMBs, no reviews, etc. - so we just need to take a look at what everyone 
else is doing and do more/higher quality optimizations. Again, this will likely just be filling out the 
GMB correctly, periodically updating it and remembering to link to our Google entities in things 
like blog articles. This shouldn’t take endless amounts of time. 
 
In medium KD niches you’ll start to see more activity in competitor GMBs. They will actually be 
creating posts, they’ll be collecting reviews and so on. It’s likely that you’ll have to step your 
game up and start going the extra mile with your GMB. You should be fine though as you could 
just be up against someone in the office filling their GMB in, replying to reviews, making the odd 
post, etc. - you will know if you are competing against SEOs or someone in the office. So see 
their approach, how many posts they’re doing, if they’ve created a drive stack etc. and outdo 
them. 
 
For hard KD niches, you should do everything listed below. 
 
We have to remember that multiple ranking factors are considered to rank organically and in the 
map pack.  
 
Let’s imagine you are targeting the keyword “web development London”, a KD 71 niche. Really 
hard. All the competitors have DRs between 40-80. Our website is 6 months old and has a DR 
of 8. 
 
You could do all the GMB optimizations listed below - but without a more thorough link profile, 
your GMB won’t rank. 
 
You could do all the GMB optimizations listed below - but without more trust signals from really 
authoritative websites, your GMB won’t rank. 
 
You could do all the GMB optimizations listed below - but without topical authority, our GMB 
won’t rank. 
 
You get the idea. 
 
So although we can do everything under the sun to optimize our GMB, it won’t matter if other 
parts of our strategy are really lackluster compared to the competition. 
 


 
 
 
That’s why I like taking portions of hard niches, rather than tackling them head-on. 
 
A niche is competitive usually because the traffic is valuable. 
 
However, by taking a sub-niche within the hard niche, we still gain the benefits of the valuable 
traffic, without directly competing for the keywords a 70DR authority site is targeting.  
 
An easy example of this is law. The main authorities struggle to spread their authority across all 
the sub-niches, therefore leaving weaknesses for you to rank sites and GMBs easier where they 
are less authoritative over the keywords you target. 
 
With all that in mind, let’s look at how we can set up and optimize our GMB. 
Filling Out Your GMB 
There are hundreds of guides on filling your GMB and most of it is self-explanatory, so I don’t 
want to go over every single input option.​ ​We will go over the parts that aren’t self-explanatory 
and we can use to boost our GMB. But for self-explanatory parts like opening times - use your 
actual opening times and keep this consistent with the citations you build. For your display 
picture, use your logo. When filling in your business opening date, use the day you actually 
opened your business etc.  
 
A good general rule for your GMB is that we want to use everything Google gives to us. See 
these labels over here?  
 
 
 
 


 
 
Instead of leaving them blank, we may as well add some keywords related to our niche and 
competition. It certainly won’t hurt us and it could help us. 
 
See the online estimates button on the Info tab? If your business can offer online estimates, use 
that feature. 
 
There’s a chat feature under ‘messaging’. If your business can take incoming messages, add 
messaging! 
 
Most of the GMB isn’t there to trick you - it just needs the basic details of your business. 
 
With that said, let’s go through the parts of our GMB that we CAN optimize… 
 
Name  
We know how relevant we are to a search is a major GMB ranking factor. This goes for the 
words used in our GMB name especially.  
 
Google definitely knows this to be true as they warn against ‘keyword stuffing’ your GMB name.  
 
I’ve seen instant ranking improvements by simply adding a niche/location keyword into a GMB 
name. The listing suddenly becomes a lot more relevant to the keywords it is trying to rank for. 
 
You can modify a client’s GMB name within moderation.  
 
Let’s create a scenario where you’ve gained a local builder client whose business is called 
Jack’s Builder Services. 
 
Most of his keywords are SL builder style keywords in Denver. 
 
By changing the listing to Jack’s Builder Services Denver, he would have a significant relevance 
push towards his most popular keywords.  
 
It would mean doing a citation clean up, but it would be worth it. 
 
Google warns against keyword stuffing, but I’ve never had a penalty despite making some 
dramatic GMB name changes to existing listings.  
 
Just like we added the location keyword in, we can add a niche keyword in if we wish. 
 
There are some instances where you might not want to change your client’s name. Perhaps you 
think it seems too unnatural, or you don’t want to risk getting their listing banned. That’s fine. In 
those cases, if my client was called Jack’s Services, I would just add either the location (Jack’s 
 


 
 
Services Denver) or the niche (Jack’s Builder Services). Perhaps you could then add the other 
keyword after a few more months. We certainly don’t want to change our name more than once 
a year or with any sort of regularity. 
 
New client GMBs -​ When a client approaches you with a new business they want to make, you 
can explain the pros of naming their business something that will be extremely relevant to the 
local revenue generating keywords. If a client wanted to start a landscaping business in Boise, 
I’d be telling them to call it “The Boise Landscaping Company” - or something to that effect. 
 
Lead generation GMBs - ​Just like our new client GMBs, we can think the same way with our 
lead generation GMBs. The strategy behind naming my GMBs which has worked the best is 
getting the niche & location keywords within the name - while trying to keep it natural. “The 
Boise Landscaping Company” is a perfect example. Calling your GMB listing “Boise 
Landscaping” is more blatant keyword stuffing and likely to get investigated by Google (although 
you see GMBs like this existing, so it can still work, but I think it’s asking for trouble). 
 
Locations 
When adding the locations you target, start with the most populous location and work your way 
down. This is just a basic optimization trick that won’t take long and could help us to be more 
geographically relevant to areas within our target locations. 
 
Start from largest to smallest: 
 
1. State 
2. County 
3. City 
4. Zip Code 
 
For example, if I had a service area business in Oxford, which targeted the entirety of Oxford, I 
would set up my locations like so: 
 
 


 
 
 
 
We start with the most populous area, then slowly add each area within or around our main 
target area.  
 
Categories 
Categories, like names, are a significant factor to consider. We can’t possibly rank within a map 
pack for our target keywords if we use a completely random category. 
 
However, what you will notice, is a range of categories appearing in the map pack. So you don’t 
know how to pick your main category or your subcategories. 
 
Each category is displayed to the right of the reviews. 
 
 


 
 
 
 
I like to use the average of all my competitors in order to decide on my main category. 
 
Search all of your main keywords, write down the companies that appear in the map pack as 
well as their main category​. 
 
See which appears the most across your main keywords and use that. 
 
Subcategories 
 
When creating a GMB, you’ll notice you can create more than one category further to your main 
category. These are your subcategories!  
 
Subcategories are hidden but you can see which subcategories your competitors are using 
through GMBspy. It’s a free chrome extension. Use it to see which subcategories your main 
competitors are using - then decide on the most suitable subcategories according to what the 
competition ranking in your target map packs are doing.  
 


 
 
 
 
Service & product listings 
When filling out your service and product pages, try to mirror the page names of your site. 
 
So if you have these pages: 
 
- Letting agent  
- Real estate agent 
- Home evaluations 
 
I would add services within your GMB with the exact same names. Remember, all of our entities 
work together in unison. We want it to be easy for Google to understand what we do and where 
we do it. 
 
Then, I would add the link to the specific website service page within our GMB service 
description.  
 
The same goes for our products, make them as similar to what your website displays as 
possible. 
 
Images 
Google My Business wipes your niche/location optimized EXIF data if you upload images 
straight to your GMB. However, there are paid services on the internet that allow you to optimize 
images and upload them through their API. Local Viking offers an image optimization service. 
Who knows how long this will be allowed. 
 
For easy niches, I wouldn’t bother with this (but I would be adding images to the GMB if they 
looked good or added relevance to a post, etc. - again, having an active account and using the 
features Google gives us, like image uploading will only help us). 
 
For medium niches, if I was struggling and the competition seemed to have a lot of images in 
their GMB, I would buy optimized images, budget allowing. 
 
For hard niches, I would most likely optimize images, again, budget allowing.  
 
Description 
Sterling Sky (prominent Local SEO agency) did testing and thinks the description bears no 
relevance to map pack ranking. While this might be true, I would still be adding your main SL 
keyword into your description. It’s not a bad thing that description doesn’t necessarily boost 
ranking as we can simply optimize our description for conversion rather than trying to place 
unnecessary keywords throughout. 
 


 
 
 
 
Questions & Answers 
Though not a vital optimization, I would add questions and answers to your GMB as it’s pretty 
easy to do. Hopefully customers are already asking questions. 
 
If they aren’t, you can simply manufacture questions from other active Google accounts.  
 
While the SEO benefits might not be vast, it will help us when someone makes a branded 
search and our GMB appears. 
 
Also, it certainly can’t hurt us and builds our niche/location relevance. 
 
Google Website 
You can find your free Google website on the left side of your GMB menu under “website”. 
 
We can use this to keyword optimize our GMB. 
 
After you publish your Google site, the URL will be ​url.business.site​. 
 
Here’s how I optimize any Google site: 
 
Button -​  Select ‘make an appointment’ as your button and link back to your website. 
 
Headline​ ​- ​I like to use my main SL keywords here, naturally. 
 
Description - ​I like to use my primary category. 
 
Summary header -​ I would use either my location or main service keywords in here, naturally. 
 
Summary body - ​Keyword optimized article for summary description. I go for 300+ words, 
describing services/products, talking about the areas we serve from our location section, and 
mentioning our primary category. 
 
I would also link to our Google Maps URL and website at the bottom. You can also add some 
local and niche authority outbound links here, too. 
 
We can also embed our Google Website into an iFrame, meaning we can embed our Google 
site into different entities, like our Web2.0s or best citations.  
 
 
 
 


 
 
GMB Posts  
You can make several kinds of posts depending on the business activity.  
 
The easiest posts to make are the “Add Update” or “What's New” posts. Ideally, we will add 
locally/niche relevant keywords, our NAP citation and maps listing, as well as make use of the 
learn more link, where we can link out to an entity in our network (Website, CID, another post, 
Google site, Web2.0, citation sites etc.) 
 
Typically the more we post to our GMB the better. Any campaign I’ve run seriously, optimized 
everything and posted a lot has gone well. You should aim for a post a day, but you could still 
get away with as little as a post a week for good effect. 
 
Each post is a chance to link back to your branded entities, add a citation, show Google activity, 
write about local/niche relevant topics, and so on. 
 
Remember, the more we can interlink everything, the more prominence we are building in 
Google’s eyes. Even though the business down the street from us has twice as many 
employees, Google sees our fully optimized website, GMB, link strategy, posts, blog articles, 
etc. and will HAVE to think we are the authority, so as we are outperforming their efforts.  
 
Easy post idea - ​In an industry where you don’t know what to post about? An easy way around 
this is by searching a question related to your niche/product/service into Google. 
 
Google will bring up the FAQ section. 
 
When you click on these boxes, more appear! 
 
There are endless relevant and authority questions with answers you can copy and paste into 
your post. 
 
Here’s the trick. 
 
Each time you open one of those boxes, there’s a link to the original source. 
 
We can cite the source by linking to their website within our post because this is a Nofollow 
link… 
 
Then we can use the C2A link back to our home/service/location page which is usually a 
DoFollow link!  
 
 
 
 


 
 
This is what an FAQ post could look like:  
 
Ever wondered why XYZ? [brand name] gives a concise answer: 
 
“The Quote” 
 
So if you want X service in [location], give [your brand] a call today! 
 
NAP 
 
C2A: [homepage] / [service page] / [location page] etc.  
 
 
I like to also interchangeably throw in my maps URL and or website URL at the bottom of the 
post. 
 
On newer GMBs you might get flagged for making really obviously optimized posts. You can still 
use your NAP, but you might get flagged for including a few of your own links. 
 
For lead generation assets I would be extra cautious in getting posts flagged because we don’t 
want any Google representative manually inspecting our listing.  
 
Siloing Posts 
A strategy you can use for your Google posts is siloing your posts together to send a chain of 
relevancy to a specific page on our website. 
 
Let’s use our FAQ strategy above. 
 
Take a keyword you’re trying to rank for, like tree removal. 
 
Create an FAQ post and send the C2A link to our example.com/tree-removal page. 
 
Then, create another FAQ post about tree removal and use the C2A link to link to the original 
post. 
 
Go into your Google site, look at posts, click on the original FAQ post, then grab the URL for 
you C2A. 
 
 


 
 
 
 
 
Then we can create loads of other FAQ posts about tree removal and link them together like a 
chain. 
 
This is just one of those easy tricks we can incorporate into our processes that doesn’t take 
much effort, but could pass some relevance throughout our Google entities. 
 
The length of the silo is as long as you want to take it/until you run out of content etc. 
 
You can also create your top post as an event rather than just a “What’s New” basic post. 
 
The posts can also be valuable to anyone that makes a branded search and sees your posts. 
 
… You could even then curate a post silo into a blog article, gaining niche relevance by linking 
out to the sources you used! 
 
That’s a low effort way to create an article, which can turn into its own Google post with a link to 
the new article, as well as being syndicated across your social accounts, etc. 
 
Any time there is news, an event, a change, industry news, a new blog article, etc. - these are 
all easy to write a quick post about.  
Google Stacks  
Google Stacks, what are they? 
 
Google Stacks are when you create Google entities, fill them with niche and location rich 
content, internally link them together, get them indexed, then send a link from your Google 
website to power up your actual website. 
 
We are using Google’s own selfishness (Google loves Google) against itself. 
 
 


 
 
We optimize all these Google properties, internally link them, then channel the prominence, 
relevancy, and authority through to our website. 
 
You can see an excellent example of a Google Stack being embedded and channeled through a 
Google website here:​ ​https://sites.google.com/site/houstonseoconsultant/  
 
There are loads of resources online that show you which Google entities to fill out and how to do 
so. 
 
The Main Things To Remember 
- Updates this year deem that you should only internally link your Google entities between 
themselves. 
 
- The only time we link out from our Google entities is from our Google site - we don’t like out 
from any other entity, we only internally link. 
 
- You used to be able to link to everything from your different entities but now you should only 
link from your Google site. 
 
- You don’t need to publish anything, e.g. a Google Doc, you simply have to make it public and 
accessible via link, which still indexes it. 
 
I will say right now, I’ve had limited success with Google Stacks.  
 
Despite creating all the entities, linking them together, channeling the power through the Google 
website into my website - I never saw huge ranking gains. 
 
However, if you channel tier 2 links into your stacks and even create multiple stacks - this would 
likely give you strong results 
 
I personally wouldn’t use them in easy niches (too much time effort, not needed), would strongly 
consider using them in medium niches (depending on the competition) and would definitely use 
them for hard niches. 
 
GMB Linking 
When trying to boost our GMBs ranking power, we can build the prominence of our Google 
entities by linking to them.  
 
We only need to do this for hard and maybe medium difficulty KD niches. Virtually no easy 
keyword niche is going to link to their Google entities.  
 
They never link to their website let alone their extra Google entities! 
 


 
 
 
There are a few different linking opportunities to use if we want to power up our Google entities 
to strengthen our GMB. 
 
Think of any naturally built links, blog article outbound links, web2.0 links, tier 2 links, PBN links 
etc.  
 
All of these links can be sent to different pages, like our Google Maps CID URL, best citation 
accounts, best social accounts, Google Website etc. 
 
Still, I would be focusing links on my website before powering up Google entities, unless I had a 
large budget/accessibility for links. 
Increasing GMB proximity  
When targeting a large state/county/city, we want to rank for as many keywords as possible… 
 
SL keywords​ - SL keywords are a prime example of this.  
 
We want to rank in map packs when people search SL keywords in locations ​within​ our 
state/city… and the SL keywords in locations ​near​ our target state/city.  
 
We achieve this by creating location pages, as already discussed in the OnPage chapter. 
 
Creating these pages allows us to rank organically, but it also allows our GMB to appear in 
nearby areas too. 
 
Let’s use Manchester as an example again.  
 
If I had a commercial cleaning client in Manchester, I would hope their office is near the center 
of Manchester, optimize for Manchester keywords, then begin adding extra website locations 
pages: 
 
 


 
 
 
 
- Stockport  
- Ashton-Under-Lyne 
- Failsworth 
- Eccles 
- Prestwich 
- Pendlebury 
- Stretford 
 
The closer the area is to your GMB, the more likely you will rank for it as we still have some 
proximity on our side. 
 
The further away the area is, the more prominence and relevance we rely on as our proximity 
diminishes. 
 
Still, by creating these pages we increase the chance of our GMB ranking for SL keywords for 
even more local areas! 
 
Remember, our GMB is completely associated with our website and these location pages. 
We’re powering up our website location pages with: 
 
 


 
 
- NAP somewhere on the page (so Google knows you are within a reasonable distance to that 
area within your city) 
- Google Map embed (same as above) 
- 500-1000 words of location/niche relevant content 
- Blog articles pointing towards location pages with SL keyword anchors 
- Can send links to these location pages 
- Likely to have less direct competition in these smaller areas 
 
It’s worth noting here that, yes, our proximity will eventually run out - we can’t extend one GMBs 
proximity forever through location pages. 
 
However, I would keep adding local nearby locations until you stop ranking in them. 
 
The more DR, trust, relevancy and prominence we have, the easier we will rank into these extra 
areas we’re targeting.  
 
It’s not really a problem that we will run out of map packs we can rank in as most local 
businesses only want to target a certain radius anyway. 
 
Should they want to expand into another city, you can create their GMB to work from day one! 
 
Long tail keywords -​ Just like we create location pages, by creating blog articles and 
supporting service pages, we can rank organically - but also rank our GMB in the map pack too. 
 
Keywords like:  
 
Local commercial kitchen cleaner Failsworth 
Chimney repair Eccles 
Cheap boiler installation Prestwich 
 
All of these searches produced map packs that we could rank in. 
 
So not only do we want to rank for more SL keyword map packs, but for all of these long tail 
keywords in the map pack too. 
 
You could create more pages associated with your extra location pages.  
 
I wouldn’t be creating endless pages associated with every single service associated with every 
single small location page you create. I would be tempted to create specific service pages to go 
with these location pages if there was a good amount of traffic for that keyword, perhaps like a: 
 
cleaningexmaple.com/kitchen-cleaning-eccles 
 
 


 
 
But I wouldn’t be making a page like: 
 
cleaningexample.com/commercial-kitchen-cleaner-failsworth  
 
Instead, I would be making our Eccles location page more relevant to those kinds of long tail 
terms by creating blog articles with aggressive anchor text and linking them to our location page 
in Eccles. 
Lead Generation GMBs 
One of the best parts about lead generation assets is being able to select our niche and 
location.  
 
The other best part is creating our GMB for optimal effect from day one to work in perfect unison 
with our website and overall strategy. 
 
Client brands already exist so they have limitations, like their GMB being registered in the 
middle of nowhere.  
 
However, a lead generation asset gives us the chance to manipulate our GMB from day one for 
optimal Local SEO benefits!  
 
Picking an area - ​I’ve already stated it once in this book. I like central city location GMBs. There 
are loads of benefits: 
 
- Most populous  
- There is wealth in cities 
- Easier to rank in nearby areas  
- Commercial sales to other businesses 
- Dominate cities and become the authority in the region 
- Business/property owners from out of state/county make city specific search terms  
 
So ideally we will set up any GMB as close to a city center as possible. 
 
The next hurdle to get over is GMB verification. 
 
How do we pass Google’s verification test…? 
 
 
 
 


 
 
Verifying GMBs 
I like to verify GMBs in the most white hat possible way. I don’t want to lose many GMB assets if 
any at all. 
 
If you take every step to appear just like any other business, you shouldn’t have a problem. 
 
Sure, I’ve lost some GMBs, but that was through trying spammy approaches - we’ll look at those 
attempts too. 
 
But ideally, you’ll follow my guidance and create GMBs that look just like any other business out 
there… 
 
This means we blend in perfectly with every other ‘normal’ business getting verified every single 
day. 
 
The key to this is having an address we have access to where we can receive a postcard.  
 
A house, a flat, an apartment, or a virtual office. 
 
If we use one of those places as our address, we can receive a postcard from Google, verify our 
GMB, then set our service area radius after getting verified. 
 
We no longer have that property associated with our business after verification. No one will 
appear at the address to be served. 
 
But this is a completely natural way to create a GMB. Many service businesses are registered to 
their home address. 
 
And if we need to register a business to the address to make the lead generation asset 
completely legit…We can do that!  
 
(More on company registration in the lead generation specific chapter at the end of the book!) 
 
There’s simply no way of Google knowing if we are like the commercial cleaner down the road 
or not.  
 
All they know: a credible looking business is creating and verifying a GMB at a credible, real 
location. 
 
Google sent the postcard to the address, we typed in the verification code and we are good to 
go! 
 


 
 
 
Let’s look at the different ways we can get our GMB verified in more detail… 
 
Network  
We know we want to use properties to register our GMBs at. Who has property? 
 
Your network.  
 
This is why I stressed the importance of your Gold Mine Area and building real-life connections. 
 
Ideally, you’ll have friends, family, connections, people you know, business partners etc. that will 
have homes or locations you can use. 
 
There’s likely to be some footprint of the business being registered from that address if people 
search for it, but that’s it. 
 
If you don’t have someone willing to just give you their location to receive a postcard from, you 
might have to bargain some money or percentage shares with acquaintances or people you 
have less rapport with. 
 
Still, I think it’s an easy sell. They won’t have anyone showing up to their home and they could 
get paid out of it.  
 
I’ve managed to mostly not pay for my listings with friends and family so you should aim for that 
rather than giving away equity/cash flow shares. 
 
Using your network is the best strategy in my opinion. You already know a whole load of people, 
there could be some great GMB opportunities within your existing network. 
 
My friend’s dad managed to get three GMB addresses just by asking his friends. So don’t forget 
to use your network’s network too.  
 
Everyone you know probably also knows someone you could do this with! 
 
Rental properties - ​Using a rental property is a bit riskier. Someone you know can take the 
postcard, but they don’t own the property. 
 
There’s no way for Google to know, but it is starting to cross the line of things you may/may not 
be comfortable with. 
 
I would personally be happy to verify a GMB at a property a friend is renting. Most landlords 
would never Google their address anyway and it doesn’t harm them.  
 


 
 
 
At the worst, the friend moves out in a year, the landlord discovers there’s a business registered 
there, I explain the process to him and offer a small % of sales. 
 
It’s just even more passive income for his investment! 
 
If the landlord was really angry and didn’t want to continue - I would relocate the GMB if it was 
doing well.  
 
I have changed addresses, phone numbers and all sorts on GMBs (not that you should ever do 
this, but if it was the only choice, you won’t necessarily lose the asset. Businesses relocate after 
all).  
 
If Google does a manual review of you, there shouldn’t be much of a problem. You can receive 
a verification postcard at your new address. If they really want to see a registered business at 
the new address (now that they’re sniffing into you), you can simply register the business. No 
problem.  
 
Don’t be picky -​ Any time we can get a low hassle GMB, take the opportunity. If Uncle Roger is 
happy to receive a postcard, give you the code and know what you’re doing… Go for it. The 
beauty of GMBs is that we can set something really basic up if we don’t want to fully commit to a 
GMB. Even without a website and just some basic GMB optimizations, you could still pick up 
some leads through proximity alone. 
 
If a family member has a property 1 hour away from a big city but they live in a town with 
neighboring towns, this is a perfectly good GMB opportunity. Sure, it’s not in the city center, but 
it’s an easy GMB opportunity that will collect leads void of your time (after you set it up). Then, if 
it’s picking up some traction, you can invest even more time into it. Cities also means more 
competition, so we’ll have an even easier time getting leads from these smaller areas. 
 
Now, if Uncle Roger is in the middle of nowhere, some town with 10,000 people in it and no 
other location for hours, it’s not worth it. 
 
Networking  
If your network is looking weak, you should network through events, the places you go and any 
time you are out and about. Again, most people have an address, or they know loads of people 
with addresses that might be able to help you out - or you can offer it as a partnership if you feel 
cheeky just asking your friends and acquaintances. I think offering some sort of percentage gain 
for them, even if small, is a nice gesture and will help you close loads of good GMB addresses.  
 
I expand on networking in the sales chapter!  
 
 


 
 
 
Virtual office  
Most of my virtual office verification attempts have been successful. You find a virtual office in a 
good area, ideally, an office that doesn’t seem really popular… You register the GMB to the 
virtual office, pay for the month, collect the postcard (they scan and email you any post) and 
cancel the monthly subscription!  
 
In order to register most businesses, they will want proper licensing and insurance details. I 
avoid this by saying I’m a third party business, like a comparison website, that refers people to 
“real businesses”!  
 
Ultimately, the company doesn't care that much about what you’re doing with the virtual office. 
To them, it’s an easy $30 a month, or however much it costs in your city. 
 
Crucially, we are going for virtual offices who also have physical offices with “real” businesses 
operating with staff in the building. This means Google will be giving out GMB verifications at 
these addresses… 
 
So when the virtual office generate you a new address (​#12,​ ​123 AB Street), Google will simply 
assume it’s a real location and you can get the postcard. We effectively blend in and ‘could’ be 
one of these other legit businesses who operate at the building. 
 
This strategy only works for a service area business. We can hide our location after being 
verified and we should be under the radar from there.  
 
If Google did investigate you, their guidelines are that people need to be operating at the virtual 
office in order for it to be legitimate. The problem? Google can’t keep track of every single 
business at every single virtual office. The worldwide scale is massive. 
 
It seems the US is getting stricter with virtual offices, especially with niches like plumbing or 
locksmithing, but you should still manage to get some GMBs verified. Just keep trying more 
virtual offices. 
 
Recently I tried 5 different virtual address locations around London. 4 were successful and I 
couldn’t get one verified. Not bad.  
 
Thankfully, virtual offices are on our side. Again, to them, it’s just easy monthly cash flow to 
have people using their building as a virtual office. This is great for us. 
 
If Google did somehow completely crack down on virtual offices, we should be able to escape 
the culling. 
 
 


 
 
Loads of “legitimate” non-lead generation assets verify their businesses at virtual offices. This 
means Google won’t just shut down all businesses verified at virtual offices. 
 
What might happen is Google wants more proof of your business as a “legitimate” entity in their 
eyes. 
 
You can just register the business to a physical location and show them you are a real business 
registered to a real address. They might want to send another postcard to prove that, but that 
won’t be a problem as you actually have access to this address. This combats them thinking 
you’re just a faceless business operating out of some sort of fake address. 
 
I would say: “Oh, we verified our GMB there because that was our first office. Now we have our 
business registered at XYZ address, I will send proof.” 
 
I’m speculating on the above. Who knows how Google will decide to combat virtual offices. For 
now, they are relatively safe ways to get GMBs verified.  
 
If Google did ever do a review of my virtual office GMBs, I would ditch any that weren’t 
performing and make sure I found a proper address for those going well. 
 
I would verify one GMB per virtual office, generally. 
 
Recently, I even got a refund from my first month payment for the virtual office subscription! So 
overall, I like the strategy. I prefer a physical location, but virtual offices are a close second. We 
can create a lot more quantity through them (as most people don’t have unlimited access to 
properties to verify GMBs through). They are probably less secure, but they are decently secure 
for now and we have our backup plan if things do go wrong.  
 
One other thing to note, I would avoid creating multiple GMBs for the same brand through 
different virtual offices and connecting them together. It could still work, but I would rather 
diversify my risk when using virtual offices. The fewer eyes and attention we have on our listing, 
the better. Expanding our brand and continually using virtual offices for new locations might 
seem strange and get flagged. I would personally rather get a GMB verified at a virtual office, 
dominate that city, add the local location pages to nearby areas, then diversify to the next easy 
GMB option I have. 
 
Landlords  
A great partner for your network is a landlord. My cousin has an excellent connection with a 
large property landlord in a really prominent UK city. This landlord has endless city center 
properties and they are registering lead generation assets at each of them. The landlord is 
already involved in lots of different types of business, like construction, so he loves the idea of 
 


 
 
utilizing his rental houses for generating leads. Now not only does his house generate rental 
cash flow and appreciation - but they’re now also being used as primetime GMB asset locations.  
 
So, if you can partner up with some sort of landlord or property owner, you can get their 
properties earning in more than one way (you give them a percentage of income), they lose 
nothing and you get loads of properties to create GMBs from!  
 
Adverts 
If you exhaust the options above or don’t like the sound of a virtual office, you can find a 
homeowner through an advert. 
 
You could use Facebook marketplace, Gumtree (UK) or Craigslist (US). 
 
The process is simple. 
 
Run a job advert with the amount of money you would offer to someone for verification at their 
address, let’s say $25. 
 
Be coy about what you’re doing in the advert, just let them know they need a property or access 
to a property and there’s no work involved. 
 
Then explain the concept to them. You don’t have to let them know it’s a ‘black hat’ strategy or 
there’s anything wrong with it. 
 
Explain the premise in a way you think they will understand what is happening.  
 
I really think you shouldn’t have too many problems getting a GMB verified with someone.  
 
At the very worst, you can offer an upfront payment plus a small ongoing percentage of income, 
so the homeowner is massively winning. 
 
I’m only putting emphasis on paying because people struggle to get GMBs verified. 
 
Ideally, you’ll get loads for free/near free. If you have to pay out a bit or come to some deals, 
that’s fine too, especially if they’re in great locations.  
 
But you should aim to get a load without paying a dime upfront!  
 
Spammed Listings 
You used to be able to verify a GMB over the phone. 
 
Instead of getting a postcard to a specific address, Google could call you instead. 
 


 
 
 
It became a total free for all and people were getting GMBs verified left right and center.  
 
There are still some ways you can get your GMB verified by phone, but they’re usually hit or 
miss and don’t work as well as the postcard. So I would stick with the postcard verification 
method. 
 
PO Boxes -​ PO boxes are similar to virtual offices, but Google is a lot stricter with them 
considering they are just buildings designed to give people mailing addresses. So although you 
can get a verification code sent to a PO box, you often get flagged and can’t blend in as 
smoothly with Google’s virtual office rules. I’ve recently had some GMBs verified at PO Boxes 
but I don’t love the strategy at all. It was more of a test. The GMB is still alive a few months later. 
It’s collecting impressions for its service keywords but I don’t want to invest much time into it as I 
think it will be delisted or penalized eventually. I believe America is quite strict with PO boxes so 
you might not even get a verification code sent to a PO box address. Still, this strategy could 
work in other countries, but I much prefer the other strategies above.  
 
Vendors 
I’ve bought several GMBs from companies like GMB Swarm and various others. Most go extinct 
because their way of getting GMBs verified gets shut down by an algorithm update. 
 
I have a couple of successful GMBs that I purchased from vendors still in action.  
 
However, the cost tends to be in the $75-$100 range. 
 
This price isn’t horrible for a GMB verification alone, but vendors come with baggage. 
 
For a start, you don’t really know where they are going to verify the listing. Normally you could 
pick the zip code but sometimes the location would get random. 
 
Then you have the added weirdness that they likely used a spam technique to get the GMB 
verified in the first place... 
 
I don’t like building on unstable foundations. 
 
Overall, I would avoid vendors, especially if you’ve never made a lead generation GMB. Get 
your free GMBs through connections, then test some GMB verification purchases when you 
have more experience dealing with them.  
 
 
 
  
 


 
 
Starting a GMB 
I like to avoid making any really obvious GMB optimizations in the first 30 days. I go easy on it. 
Sure, I fill everything out how I said above, but I don’t start adding loads of posts, Drive stacks 
through my Google site etc.  
 
I’ve had experience in optimizing GMBs from day one which has also mostly been void of any 
major issues. Still, it’s my preference to go easy on the optimizations in the first few weeks. 
Especially if we have a really good GMB location. We don’t want to do anything that might 
jeopardize ourselves and be put on the manual review radar.  
  
Duping Google Support 
Avoid speaking to support if possible. We don’t want Google looking into our lead generation 
assets. Ideally, we will blend in with the other thousands of businesses in any given location. 
When support starts doing manual reviews of your account, they might start getting… Weird. 
 
One thing I notice when dealing with Google is how we can get around some of their demands. 
 
I had a recent mishap where I was fooling around, registered two businesses at the same 
address and got Google involved because one wasn’t appearing for Google Maps searches. 
 
So they first asked me to “kindly” remove one of the listings. Great. 
 
Then for my other business, they wanted to see proof I had a business registered at the address 
in question. 
 
Naturally, I didn’t.  
 
So when I went to register my keyword stuffed GMB name as a legit business, I noticed the 
generic business name was already registered as a company, so I had to pick something 
different. 
 
I asked Google if I could keep my GMB name despite my company’s registered name. No was 
the answer. 
 
No matter which way I tried to spin it, they wanted me to change my GMB name to my new, less 
optimized GMB name. 
 
Eventually, I gave in and played ball. I changed my GMB name to my new registered company 
name. 
 
One thing you’ll notice when you speak to Google in these email threads is how you’re never 
speaking to the same people. 
 


 
 
 
In most interactions, you’re getting some random support team member responding to you. 
 
After a few weeks of having this new GMB name, I changed it back to my optimized name and it 
got approved! 
 
By simply playing along with Google initially, changing the listing name as requested, then 
changing it later, I got my desired GMB lead generation name.  
 
Local Reviews & Reputation Management 
 
Local reviews, and specifically Google reviews are an integral part of any Local SEO campaign.  
 
The two key reasons we want reviews are: 
 
Ranking - ​Our Moz pie chart estimates that reviews (review quantity, review velocity and review 
diversity) account for 15.44% worth of ranking factor for the map pack, while contributing 6.47% 
for organic ranking. Clearly, they are a major ranking factor for both aspects of local.  
 
I read a lot online about how reviews aren’t a major ranking factor. In my experience, they are. I 
have a roofing lead generation asset with over 20 five star reviews, while the competition mostly 
doesn't have under ten. Unsurprisingly, we dominate most of the SL keywords, service 
keywords and other long tail keywords in that location. 
 
Conversion - ​Remember what the map pack looks like. If a potential customer doesn’t want to 
click on a website, they have a few pieces of information to analyze before picking a company to 
call straight from the search results. The quantity and quality of your reviews is the primary 
selling tool available to you. If your client or lead generation asset has eight 5* reviews and your 
competition has 4 reviews with a 3* average, who are they more likely to call? Humans have a 
herd mentality - we don’t want to try something untested. We would much rather go with a safe, 
reliable option. 
 
Another factor to consider is when someone makes a branded search (searches your lead 
generation asset or your client), the GMB listing will appear. 
 
 


 
 
 
 
Having your branded searches powered up by reviews is just another way to increase sales! 
 
Google reviews are excellent credibility builders and definitely boost conversion rates. 
 
A final factor is how it makes our lives as traffic generators easier. If 1/100 customers who see 
your GMB call you, but having excellent reviews means 5/100 customers call - this saves us 
having to generate 500 customers for the exact same result! 
 
Why else should you get reviews? 
One of the best parts about Google reviews is they don’t cost us a thing. They are a product of 
doing business. 
 
All we need to do is be diligent about collecting reviews and we can receive excellent 
SEO/conversion benefits without tons of effort invested. 
 
Also, if you have a large backlog of reviews, it means your average review rating won’t 
dramatically drop if somebody leaves a bad review on any of your GMBs. 
 
For the easy KD niches, it’s common to see your competitors with very few reviews. They don’t 
understand the benefits and aren’t diligent in collecting them. This is just another ranking factor 
we can outperform our local competitors on. It’s common to see competitors all with less than 
5-10 reviews in a given niche and location. 
 
 


 
 
When you take on more difficult KD niches, your competitors are likely to have a more holistic 
SEO strategy and therefore have more reviews. If they don’t, excellent. If they do have lots of 
reviews, we can still remember that there are several other primary ranking factors that have 
more of an impact that we can be manipulating while we build up our reviews. 
Getting Reviews 
On your GMB Home page, you will see a link that takes a customer directly to the review section 
on your GMB. 
 
 
 
The reviewer will need some sort of Google account (usually Gmail or YouTube) in order to 
leave the review. This can often be a barrier, so it’s worth writing a quick instructional on how to 
create a Gmail for the customers without a Google account. 
 
The formula to get reviews is: 
 
- Provide a great service. 
- Incentivize customers to leave a review. 
- Send them the review form. 
- Follow up with them to leave a review. 
 
Work with your client to create a recurring review process. This will look different for every type 
of niche out there. For some businesses collecting reviews will be easy, for others it might be 
more of a challenge. 
 
Again, in these easy-medium KD niches, a lot of our competitors won’t be diligent about 
collecting reviews. It’s just another metric we can destroy our competition in. If our competition 
doesn't have a Local SEO helping them, they won’t know the importance of reviews. If they hire 
you, you can constantly remind them and ensure their reviews surpass all local competition. 
 
 


 
 
You can also diversify your reviews by encouraging customers to review on other websites, like 
Facebook, Yell and Yelp. 
 
Having reviews across multiple trusted platforms is going to enhance the trust filter Google 
subjects your website/GMB to. Google ideally wants to send the most trusted and credible 
websites to the top of Google, so diversifying our review effort across multiple platforms is going 
to really help our ranking. 
 
Again, the weaker competition are likely going to have very few of any of these.  
 
Reply to reviews - ​A best practice for any review is to reply to all of them. The more activity we 
can show Google, the better. Also, some potential customers will analyze reviews, so if we are 
leaving detailed responses to customers, it builds trust for potential buyers. 
Black Hat Reviews 
There are loads of review vendors on the internet you can buy fake reviews from. 
 
I don’t love the black hat review approach. Reviews are free and only require a small amount of 
diligence to pick up. 
 
If you’re working with a company that nobody wants to review, that’s a problem I would address 
rather than trying to supplement them with fake reviews. 
 
Not only do some countries try to fine you if you’re caught buying reviews, but I don’t like to hurt 
the longevity of most SEO I’m doing. 
 
Value is often realized with time in this industry, so risking penalization hurts our ROI either for 
client GMBs or our own. 
 
Buying from a fake review vendor puts you at risk because they might do it really obviously or 
blatantly. Google is on the lookout for people leaving fake reviews and will unlist you if they 
catch you. 
 
My Experience With Black Hat Reviews 
The most black hat I’ve gone with reviews is by asking people with active Google accounts who 
live in the same area as a lead generation GMB to leave a review. Remember I spoke about 
your Goldmine Areas? This is a prime example of the benefits at work. Some of these reviews 
were made over two years ago and the GMBs still haven’t been penalized.  
 
The main things Google will be looking for are if the review is coming from a ‘natural’ 
(non-blatant spam) account and if it makes sense that account would leave a review for the 
 


 
 
business. There is no review process for people leaving reviews, I think this is your best way of 
getting away with any black hat style reviews.  
 
Overall I think the best strategy is to simply leverage real reviews, especially on client sites. 
When building your own lead generation assets I would get people in the area with an active 
Google account to leave a review. 
Reputation Management 
Unfortunately, there’s not a whole lot we can do to combat bad reviews. If someone leaves a 
legitimate negative review, we can’t magically remove it. 
 
The best thing we can do is respond to the review in a positive way, while actively showing we 
are solving the issue. 
 
There are thousands of articles online about replying to bad reviews but it basically just boils 
down to solving their complaint and trying to put a positive spin on it. 
 
The real damage is done when potential customers now see us in the map pack and our 
average 5* rating goes down to a 3.5*. 
 
Suddenly we’re way less credible than the competition. All we can do is emphasize the 
importance to clients about keeping the review slate clean and getting even more 5* reviews. If 
you have fifteen 5* reviews, and someone leaves a 2* review, your average rating that goes 
next to your name will be considerably less damaged than if you had six 5* reviews. 
 
Imploring your customer to get reviews (or getting reviews for your lead generation asset) has 
dual benefits - we build lots of valuable reviews for our brand (helps ranking + conversion) while 
becoming less fragile against a rogue reviewer. 
 
Fake Reviews 
We can report fake reviews. If you think a review is fake, go to the support function in the GMB 
interface and detail the fake review.  
 
Communicating with Google is pretty random as different employees jump into your email 
thread, but you might have some success. 
 
When you get a fake review, detail all the reasons you think the review is fake to help your case. 
Then call them and cite the email to help them take the correct action.  
 
 
 


 
 
Negative SEO 
I had an old web development client from back in the day contact me. They had a huge accident 
at a construction project and a contractor’s leg was crushed.  
 
Large newspapers picked up the story, as well as websites like the construction index. It made 
for a good headline, “contractor suffers severe injuries, company fined £70,000.” 
 
All of a sudden, when you typed in my client’s company, all these damming articles appeared. 
The articles would be a huge turn-off for any other construction company wanting to hire them. 
 
As this business applied for large construction tenders from other businesses, their reputation 
needed to be strong to keep winning tenders. 
 
Before handing over a $1m+ deal to my client’s company, businesses are likely to do their due 
diligence on them. A Google search is generally the first step in looking up any business. 
 
Protecting a business’ branded searches can be worth a lot of money. I charged $2,000 and 
removed all the articles from their branded search variations. 
 
Now the articles are on page 3 for branded searches which, in Google terms, means they no 
longer exist!  
 
This is how I got rid of the negative articles: 
 
Firstly, I noticed the company didn’t have many social or business accounts. The damning 
articles were able to rank because they only had a few web properties ranking (their website, 
Facebook etc.). 
 
This meant creating social accounts like LinkedIn, Tumblr, Medium, Twitter and so on. I also 
created more directory citations that seemed like they would rank well (very niche specific). I 
linked to the social accounts from the most powerful page on the website, the homepage. The 
homepage is the most powerful page in this context because it is the page that ranks for 
branded search terms. 
 
Next, use ‘sameAs’ schema to closely connect your website with the accounts you create. This 
helps google to assimilate the accounts to your brand. It’s an additional measure to just indexing 
and linking to the accounts. 
 
Then, I bought 4 articles to rank for KGR style keywords, while also adding the social account 
links and NAP to the bottom of each article. After that, I posted the article on every social 
account created. 
 


 
 
 
Keep the profiles at least semi-active with a quick post every few days.  
 
That should do the trick and worked for my client. The social accounts ranked higher than the 
articles, therefore pushing them off the first page for branded searches. 
 
Note: I believe the main needle movers were linking to the social accounts from the client 
homepage and using sameAs schema. 
 
However, an extra measure you can take if this doesn’t work is to create websites with domains 
that include the brand name, e.g. brandnamecompany.com or brandnameco.com. 
 
Keep your website and the new website you make separate. Don’t link your new fake website. 
 
Now create social accounts to your new fake brand in the name of your fake brand.  
 
You can then build links to the fake website as well as the fake social accounts you built to try 
and outrank the articles. 
 
The concept is these fake brands are going to be more relevant to the branded search terms 
than the articles currently ranking for the branded search terms.  
 
If all this still didn’t work, you would need to build links to these properties and use branded 
anchor texts. This will boost each social account’s authority and the anchor will communicate 
which keywords the accounts should rank for.  
 
If you ever spot a company with a horrible branded Google search, you could pitch them on 
removing the damning articles. 
 
It’s an easy pitch because it’s self-explanatory why a toxic branded search will hurt your 
business, it doesn’t take endless hours to solve the problem and now you know the easy steps 
required to solve the problem! 
Getting Local SEO Clients 
Before we look into how to get Local SEO clients, I want to preface why we want them.  
 
You want to make money in Local SEO. Client work or lead generation assets are two key ways 
we can do that (aside from employment). 
 
And we touched on the pros and cons of client work and lead generation assets at the start of 
the book, but now let’s focus purely on client work.  
 


 
 
 
A Local SEO client is someone who pays us a monthly retainer to continually improve their 
organic traffic to get them more clients. 
 
From my experience pitching and speaking to lots of different sized businesses, this is generally 
how much they can afford to spend on SEO: 
 
- Small businesses (less than 5 people at the company) can afford the $200 - $1,000 range 
 
- Medium-sized businesses (more than 5 people at the company) can likely afford the $500 - 
$3,000 range.  
 
- Then large businesses (20+ people at the company) can afford the $3,000 - unlimited range. 
 
As usual, context applies and these are just guidelines. 
 
I started some early clients on as little as $150 a month when I first started. 
 
I still have most of those clients, but they’re paying three times what our first invoice was.  
 
Clearly, there is money to be made by providing either small, medium or large-sized businesses 
with Local SEO. 
 
With just a few clients, you have a respectable side hustle or even full-time income. 
 
For the newer SEOs, you’ll want to target a mix of the small - medium-sized businesses. 
 
For the SEOs with a couple of years of experience, you can target all three. 
 
For the extremely proficient, experienced SEOs, you should have a portfolio that attracts all 
three. 
 
A quick reminder: our efforts compound over time in SEO. Our clients get better and better 
results (bar any major penalizations or mistakes). Our lead generation assets continue to grow 
and make us money, attracting clients through handing out leads or staying rented and building 
good relations… Then we have a portfolio that speaks for itself, marketing dollars to advertise, 
SEO skills to generate clients, testimonials, possibly content on YouTube, blogs or other 
mediums… 
 
My first three months in the Local SEO space were a lot harder to generate results in than the 
last 3 months of business I just undertook. 
 
So with that said, let’s look at why we want Local SEO Clients…  
 


 
 
Why We Want Local SEO Clients 
 
Instant cash flow - ​When you sign up a client, they will start paying from day one. Cash is 
oxygen in any business and taking a month’s worth of pay from day one isn’t bad. Lead 
generation assets often won’t pay us particularly quickly (although they will last for a long time 
when they DO start paying you), so client work is the answer to the cash flow problem you face.  
 
Although clients are cash from day one, there is the occasional incident where you do some 
unpaid work to provide value in order to gain a client. This strategy could be used when you 
have 0 experience especially. 
 
Sometimes it’s good to do favors in business. 
 
I would consider doing free work to gain clients if there is an easy value add. If you can just 
spend a bit of time (perhaps adding their target location into their GMB name to make their GMB 
more relevant, linking their website into the GMB and fixing a few H tags) on their business and 
get fast results, it could be worth offering your services for free.  
 
Businesses in easy niches at the bottom of page 1’s for SL keywords and top of page 2’s are 
pretty easy to get results for. They would be good candidates to do free work for. 
 
Generally, we want to avoid this, but if you have a client who seems like they’re close to signing 
or you think they’d be a great client, it could be worth going the extra mile to land them.  
 
Easy to rank their sites - ​Doing an SEO audit, optimization and management service for a 
local plumber is much more simple than that of a large product affiliate website. 
 
Now, we aren’t lazy, but we do like easy work.  
 
Business is all about providing value. If we can double their inquiries in a few months by fixing 
their glaring errors, optimizing everything then improving every month… We will soon be 
providing a boatload of value to their business.  
 
The ongoing work is chargeable and we likely don’t need to spend lots of hours on the site as 
we did initially.  
 
This mostly goes for easy-medium difficulty KD keyword niches. Hard KD keyword niches will 
require more initial work and more ongoing work, hence we charge more to handle these kinds 
of contracts.  
 
 


 
 
So not only can we gain loads of traffic for our client relatively easily in easy - medium KD 
niches, it’s also not going to be rocket science. Hopefully you’ve highlighted this potential client 
because they have mistakes - and because they’re in an area/niche where increasing organic 
traffic will make them more money.  
 
Another reason improving a local site is easy, is because we aren’t starting from scratch. 
Working on a brand new domain is harder because you have to input the setup and 
groundwork. However, when working with a client with an aged domain, link profile, pages 
created with content etc., we don’t have to do much groundwork. We can focus our attention on 
fine-tuning a machine that already exists, which has a faster and smoother path to results than a 
brand new domain/GMB usually will.  
 
Here’s more reasons Local SEO clients are good: 
 
Referrals - ​Each client we land gives us a chance to be referred to more clients. Acquire one 
client, get several free! 
 
Testimonials - ​Testimonials will help our future efforts in acquiring clients. Obviously, we can 
get GMB reviews, not just website/social media testimonials. 
 
Links - ​We touched on this in the link building chapter. Being able to access loads of local sites 
to create mutually profitable linking opportunities is another tool for your local arsenal.  
 
Up-sells - ​If your client likes how you work, you get them results and they like you - they will 
probably come to you for all of their other digital marketing solutions. This means you can 
become their digital marketing consultant, too. You can facilitate any new website, Google Ads, 
Facebook Ads, design or branding they might need should you want to offer more services.  
 
Lead generation asset partners - ​These clients often have multiple addresses. They have 
home addresses and business addresses. They might have a spare GMB opportunity in a good 
location to create a lead generation asset with. You could even charge them as you create the 
lead generation asset, as they get to be a % partner for yet another lead generator in their area.  
 
Charge more - ​If you make a growing business more money through Local SEO, they will trust 
and reinvest into you and your service. Basically, if you can help your client make more money, 
they will happily spend more on you. However, this is not a steadfast rule. Many ‘small’ 
businesses are happy staying small, so there might not be as much room for exponential growth 
with these kinds of clients. Most are not actively trying to grow like medium - large-sized 
businesses. 
 
Tie all these together and you can see why gaining clients is an attractive proposition.  
 
 


 
 
But yes, clients can be a ‘pain’, but you’ll get better and better clients as you progress. I had 
some horror shows at first (one thought he could speak to the dead) - but now I’m working with 
very reasonable and integral people.  
 
Don’t underestimate how valuable business relationships are as the years go on.  
 
Knowing businessmen is a lot more profitable than knowing your friend Freddy who still lives in 
his Mom’s basement. Sad but true.  
 
Business owners reinvest into their marketing, start more businesses and refer you to their 
network.  
 
Lots of opportunities can be opened up, not just business ventures. I’ve made memories and 
friends from all over England and around the world.  
Local SEO Sales 
Let’s talk about sales in relation to Local SEO.  
 
Back when I started with my business partner, I saw him making cold calls and immediately 
knew I could jump in and close deals.  
 
That week, I sold a website for £1,000 over the phone by simply picking up the phone and 
calling local businesses from the Yell directory.  
 
My first client was great. He loved my voicemail pitch and called me back. This was my pitch: 
 
“Hey everyone at [business name] I saw that you don’t have a website associated with your 
business on the Yell directory. I recently graduated from university, and I’m making websites for 
hundreds not thousands. Give me a call back if you’re interested in talking more!” 
 
This guy was a gem to us. He took us out for lunch and he was the one that volunteered to pay 
£1,000 for the project. I’m still in contact with him today.  
 
The moral of the story is: action is king in sales.  
 
The pitch, strategy and thought process wasn’t particularly good, really. I virtually made it up on 
the spot.  
 
But if I never called that guy, if I never left a voice message and if I never took his call back, I 
wouldn’t have had a sale in my first week.  
 
In order to land Local SEO clients, we will need to take a lot of action.  
 


 
 
 
Whether it’s cold calling, making video pitches, knocking on doors, harassing your network... 
Whatever.  
 
Clients very rarely fall at your feet. It’s your job to get out there and prove to people you are 
worth paying for.  
 
Although I’m going to give you my best techniques for landing clients, they all require lots of 
effort and action to pull off.  
 
There ARE ways to get clients simply by utilizing Local SEO itself, but if you want to kick off your 
agency faster, you’ll need to adapt a sales-based mindset. 
 
When I first started and knew I wanted to do this full time, you couldn’t get me off the phone. 
 
I was doing cold outreach every single day.  
 
It wasn’t fun, but it was necessary. 
 
Now I don’t have to do that anymore.  
 
I was in that cycle for about 6 months whenever I wasn’t working on clients already amassed. 
 
If you are scared to death of cold calls, that’s fine, you don’t have to do them. 
 
But you WILL have to take a lot of action for the strategies you do decide to go with. 
 
Right now, if you have zero or very few clients, not enough people know you or what you do. 
 
Whether you’re cold calling, emailing, building SEO lead gens or anything else, you need to 
become more known. 
 
If 1000 more local business owners knew you tomorrow, you’d have new clients.  
 
So whichever strategies you go with, utilize them to the best of your ability, be creative, spend 
time on them and you WILL get new clients.  
Utilize Your Network  
This should be the most painless way of getting a client. 
 
 
 
 


 
 
Rack your brain. Think of every single person you could contact:  
 
- Friends  
- Family 
- Associates 
 
Think of things you do: 
 
- Coffee shops 
- Clubs 
- Sport 
- Work/business 
- Restaurants 
- Other activities 
  
Write all of their names down. 
 
Reach out and find out if Local SEO is applicable to them. 
 
Obviously, don’t go steaming in, blurting about Local SEO in the first few seconds. 
 
Catch up with them, see how business is going and add value. 
 
See if it makes sense for them to use your service. 
 
You could do an audit for them. Point out their mistakes, potential traffic opportunities and a 
friendly discount.  
 
Your network should be inclined to give you a shot because they already like you.  
 
For each person that we contact, even if they don’t need your services, ask if they know 
somebody who does.  
 
Occasionally you get lucky. They recently heard their friend needs a new website or wants to 
grow their business. 
 
These are going to be warm leads and you have the added benefit of being referred by your 
friend.  
 
Like with all sales, most people won’t sign a deal after your first contact. 
 
Most sales take between 6-12 contacts, so hang in there with all of your leads and be prepared 
to deliver pockets of value, like the initial audit. 
 


 
 
 
Tastefully (again, contacting with value is never distasteful) stay in contact with these leads until 
they are ready to be closed. 
 
It’s ethical to try and sign these clients if there is a genuine ROI to be gained from your services. 
 
Show them why they need you, should trust you and how they will get an ROI and you stand a 
great chance at closing leads. 
 
Your network represents loads of potential clients either through them or someone they know. 
Utilize it! 
 
Existing Social Media Channels  
This is an extension of the last strategy.  
 
Use your social media accounts (Facebook, LinkedIn, Twitter, Instagram) to let people know 
what you’re doing. 
 
Not only can you broadcast your services, but you can also post about what you’re learning, 
doing and any results you achieve. 
 
Again, leveraging people who are already aware of us will shorten the sales cycle. 
 
Old school friends I messaged on Facebook have become clients for me. 
 
Always Be Pitching  
After a year of starting my SEO venture, I invested in a $100 a month gym membership. 
 
This was one of my biggest monthly expenses. 
 
However, it paid off massively. I landed a few medium-sized clients that year, and still have 
people from that gym in my pipeline. 
 
I picked that gym because it put me in front of loads of business owners to build relations with 
and eventually pitch. 
 
Again, we aren’t steaming in with Local SEO megaphone announcements, we’re seeing where 
we can add value and who we can make mutually beneficial relationships with. 
 
As you build your network with the people you meet, you can mix in other strategies listed in this 
chapter, like giving them an audit through a video pitch or passing them a lead relevant to their 
business. You could give out free GMB optimizations for all the business owners in the gym. 
 


 
 
Then you’re known as the Local SEO guy! The possibilities are endless with a bit of self-belief 
and creativity.  
 
This gym was a bit like a health club, so there was a bar (healthy!), people sat in the lobby, 
there were sport/yoga classes, and there was a pool/sauna area. It was easy to bump into 
people and start chatting. 
 
If you don’t like gyms, just be mindful of the places you go and the people you bump into.  
 
It could be coffee shops, events, family gatherings, martial arts training, gaming friends… 
Everybody. 
 
All of these people may either need Local SEO or know someone that does. 
 
Always be asking yourself if you can do business with the people you are coming into contact 
with. 
 
This sounds robotic and inhuman, but there’s nothing wrong with pursuing mutually beneficial 
relationships that could be great for both parties. 
 
Also, any massive company like Apple does exactly the same. 
 
They want to do business with everyone, that’s why they make their products attractive to most 
people. 
 
We want to do the same. Be comfortable with that if you want more clients! 
Create An SEO Lead Generation Asset 
If Local SEO gets your clients sales… You can just do the same for your SEO agency! 
 
A quick side note: many locations are dominated by larger agencies that have been established 
for years. 
 
Therefore, ranking in large cities for obvious SEO keywords isn’t necessarily going to be easy. 
 
Not to mention, we are competing directly with SEOs on their own sites as opposed to other 
low-level local sites. 
 
But as you should realize from this book, there’s an abundance of opportunity in everything 
SEO.  
 
These are the ways you can create your own lead generation asset to generate SEO leads: 
 


 
 
 
Agency site - ​When you set out to get your first clients, you can create an agency website for 
your business. Not only will this get local clicks for SEO and digital marketing, but it’s another 
tool to prove your legitimacy as a business. The same rules apply for your SEO agency site as 
working on a client site. Perform the best practices laid out in this book and you’ll get results. 
 
You will need to be smart with it though. Like I said above, it could be a competitive niche, 
especially if you’re living in a popular city. Most of these agencies will get some kind of link from 
their client, meaning they will have a strong local link profile almost by default, let alone 
whatever else they are adding. 
 
You can still take parts of the market by targeting long tail keywords and expanding your GMB 
proximity through optimization. 
 
Another option is to add more location pages either within your city or areas nearby, where your 
competitors are less prominent.  
 
Lead generation asset -​ If your home location is a bad area for collecting SEO leads, you 
could register a different lead generation asset to your home address (perhaps there’s a great 
opportunity for an essential niche service site/GMB near where you live…) and find a better 
property to register your SEO agency asset at. As usual, do the keyword/market research and 
create your sites where it’s going to be most profitable. If this means your agency website is in 
the next city over to you… And you use your home address for a rank and rent site - so be it.  
 
You also aren’t limited to one SEO agency website. 
 
If you spot several good opportunities in several locations, you can go ahead and make them as 
long as you follow the principles laid out in the book. 
 
Although presumably searchers are looking for an SEO firm local to them, most people are 
happy to do business over the phone, so that won’t be much of an issue. 
 
You have also proved your services because they found you through a Google search.  
 
In digital marketing niches, I like to create a GMB name that targets mid tail keywords. The main 
SL keywords are usually quite dominated, meaning we can’t just jump in the map pack with a 
keyword stuffed name. However, we probably can jump in for variations of SL keywords. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 


 
 
Here are some mid tail keyword examples: 
 
Web Design Agency [location] 
SEO Agency [location]  
Local Marketing agency [location] 
PPC Company [location] 
 
Although these kinds of keywords won’t have as many searches as SL keywords, they should 
have some (do your research) and have the same search intent. 
  
If you keyword stuff your GMB name, there’s a good chance you’ll rank for that mid tail keyword 
quickly. 
 
A word of warning though, if our competitors get suspicious we aren’t a real entity, they might 
report us to Google. 
 
So I would keyword stuff the GMB title, but not be too blatant about it.  
 
You could use other words, like below: 
 
Wolf Web Design Agency [location] 
Smith’s SEO Agency [location] 
Red’s Local Marketing Agency [location] 
Pear PPC Company [location] 
 
Keyword stuffing is going to dramatically improve your chance to rank your GMB, but you also 
don’t want to be reported. If your market is extremely uncompetitive, you can get away with 
keyword stuffing. If there are loads of high-level SEO agencies around you, they might be on the 
lookout for black hat GMBs, so it could be worth being less blatant about your GMB name as 
demonstrated above. If you throw in an extra word, it will help to disguise your intentions, but 
you still get to keep the service + location mid tail keyword you were targeting.  
 
I did a pretty blatant keyword-stuffed GMB name for a mid tail SEO keyword in a large city in 
England that ranked very fast. It never got reported either despite high-level agencies being 
nearby. Still, it’s not worth the risk - try and cover your tracks.  
 
Create a digital marketing asset - ​There are lots of digital marketing related searches, not just 
SEO searches. I have several lead generation assets and GMBs dotted throughout London that
