rank for web design and development keywords. All development projects can be outsourced to 
the developers I know, and we can take care of any SEO projects that come through. 
 
So when doing keyword research to find goldmine locations to pick up SEO leads, don’t neglect 
other divisions of digital marketing which overlap with SEO (e.g. website design/development).  
 


 
 
Use A Lead 
Using a lead to introduce yourself to a company is extremely powerful.  
 
Instead of simply pitching a prospect, you are coming to them with value and more specifically, 
what your service will ultimately provide them. Business owners like resourceful people. Coming 
to them with a lead is much better than outright asking for their business.  
 
Here are ways you can get leads: 
 
Your lead generation assets - ​Give out leads to business owners in the area you want to strike 
up a deal with. 
 
Other digital marketing strategies - ​If you’re a wizard at anything other than SEO, use these 
skills to generate leads. At one point I was sending out thousands of emails a day from fake 
business aliases offering cleaning, CCTV and other local services. When a business would 
email me back, I’d send the lead to any interested prospect I already had, or use the lead to 
start a conversation with a new business. 
 
Keep your ears open - ​Whenever I hear someone needs XYZ service, I wonder if it could be of 
benefit to linking that person with the service provider. Again, this is not being mercenary, this is 
being resourceful. The person needing XYZ service has a problem and the service provider 
wants to make money. You are facilitating this exchange. Even though you aren’t directly using 
your SEO or marketing skills, it’s a great way of introducing yourself and showing you are 
resourceful. 
 
You become the lead - ​Spending money at a business you want to work with is making 
yourself the lead. You are giving them value by helping their business turn a profit. If you are a 
valued customer, these people will be happier to listen to you and your money making ideas for 
them. Look to the places you already spend money at or think about new businesses you could 
be spending at to pique their interest in you.  
Trade Services  
I really like this strategy when done correctly. Instead of charging for your service, trade your 
Local SEO skills in return for their service. Perhaps there’s a restaurant you like eating at. Or 
maybe you like to get your haircut every week at the same barber’s.  
 
Any local business that you spend good money on or want more of is worth considering. 
 
As with any business transaction, we want to make sure we are getting a high ROI. 
 
 


 
 
I personally target businesses where I can make easy wins without having to put in tons of 
groundwork. 
 
For example, helping your local barber who’s just missing out on some map pack spots and 
sitting on lots of page 2’s would be easier than setting everything up from scratch for a local 
takeaway.  
 
Don’t go steaming in, either. Businesses are happy to consider trading service for service, but 
mostly after they know, like and trust you. 
 
For example, I’m doing a service for service deal here in Chiang Mai. 
 
I rented a basic scooter for a couple of months. When the third payment was due, I sent the 
owner an email detailing the problems with his website and SEO.  
 
He liked my insights, knew I was involved in SEO and we agreed on two upgraded bikes. I gave 
my web developer a friend a bike - he made some nice improvements to his site. I made some 
GMB changes and then we’ve had free bikes for 5 months since. 
 
He’s happy because the site is excellent, he’s ranking better and we’ve directed anyone who 
needs to rent a bike towards them (leverage leads!). I know at least four people have gone there 
from our recommendation, netting the bike company some good monthly income. 
 
If I walked in and offered the trade of services on day one, he would have most likely said no.  
 
Instead, I built my value and our relationship on the occasions I met him, then pitched him the 
trade of services when it seemed like it would work.  
 
Fiverr  
I acquire a client or two each month from Fiverr and outsource the work.  
 
The reasons I like websites like Fiverr: 
 
- Fiverr has huge traffic. I got 2k impressions over the last month for my gigs 
- The landing page they give you is relatively good 
- Fiverr account only takes a few days to set up and fully optimize 
- You can apply SEO tactics to your account to rank higher 
 
The reasons I don’t like websites like Fiverr: 
 
 


 
 
Clients are cheap -​ Cheap clients means they disrespect you and your time. Even if your prices 
are high (they should be after you pick up some reviews, or prepare to be at the mercy of 
difficult people), you will still get worse clients than those you meet through other means.  
 
Fiverr takes their cut -​ Fiverr takes 20% for doing business through their platform. 
 
Poor communication - ​It’s against TOCs to take communication away from Fiverr. They don’t 
have a calling or video service to make communications clearer. However, other variations of 
Fiverr have calling capabilities.  
 
Competition -​ Although we can manipulate the search results to a degree, there’s certainly less 
ways to dominate the competition as we can in Local SEO.  
  
Legiit  
Charles has been having success lately using Legiit marketplace. This is an online gig economy 
like Fiverr but specifically for SEOs. If you’re planning on offering Local SEO services on one of 
these kinds of sites, Legiit might be your best shot. 
 
Engaging With Other Digital Marketers 
I’ve had great success in landing clients and other benefits through becoming friends with other 
SEOs and digital marketers. 
 
Other SEOs and digital marketers often have work to outsource, or clients they can’t serve. 
 
I made a good friend who is a web development specialist. He gets clients who need Local SEO 
to go with their new website. 
 
We’ve helped each other multiple times where I need a WordPress specialist and he can’t 
personally provide SEO. 
 
If you don’t immediately know other digital marketers, your best bet is probably Facebook 
groups. You’ll find loads of digital marketers across a variety of groups. If you prefer to do things 
in person, I met quite a few agency owners in city center coffee shops and just generally being 
more inquisitive about the people around me. 
 
Just one connection could be a slew of opportunity!  
 
Blog Writing 
It’s somewhat hypocritical of me to write this because I haven’t done enough of it, but it’s an 
excellent strategy. 
 
 


 
 
When you have a successful project, you should be adding a case study to your blog. 
 
As you know, adding local, relevant blog posts is going to help your SEO. You might also pick 
up traffic from non-local searches as well as boosting your local ranking.  
 
Look at Charles’ blog. He can get clients at a click of a finger if he wishes because he has 
proven his worth to virtually anyone to manage their SEO. 
 
Content writing is something I’m getting more and more serious about. I have several blog 
articles in draft format, but Charles and I wanted to get this book out in August, so it’s taken 
priority.  
 
I suggest you document your story, insights and case studies as your venture progresses. 
 
Your blog is an asset that can help your local agency site rank, as well as rank for non-location 
keywords, where you can collect email addresses and eventually sell products/services through 
your list.  
The Perfect Cold Video Pitch 
This is one of my preferred ways to pitch a prospect. 
 
The premise is that you send a perfectly crafted sales video, created through Camtasia or 
similar. You can either buy or torrent any top recording software to make these videos happen. I 
personally bought Camtasia. Investing cash into your SEO business makes you more time 
invested, in my opinion, but you can torrent these things if you wish. If I was new, I wouldn’t 
want to fork out $200+ for a recording software either! 
 
Anyway, with these kinds of software you can record your screen while also recording your face, 
like so. 
 


 
 
 
 
Show clients what they can do to make more revenue. 
 
Here’s the general sequence to pitching someone Local SEO: 
 
- Fast greeting and introduction, building authority and credibility 
- Highlight their problem & why their SEO isn’t doing as well as it should 
- This could be a lack of a GMB or optimizing it, poor OnPage, weak link profile etc. 
- Perhaps show them the top ranking competition & why they’re ranking above them 
- Then show the clear process to outrank the competition & take their traffic 
- It helps to then articulate what this new traffic could be worth to them 
- Aim for around 4-6 minutes. 8+ is too long and less than 3 minutes is too short. 
 
As ever, we’re looking for clients that we know we can help without tons of hours involved, in KD 
niches that we are comfortable tackling. 
 
The perfect cold video pitch was one of my most successful sales strategies. 
 
I closed multiple 4 figure deals because video pitches convey a lot more trust and authority 
compared to an email or phone call.  
 
The largest deal I closed with the video pitch was $2,500 a month. I combined giving out a lead 
with a cold video pitch. 
 
The strategy also has a high open/response rate because you can simply upload the video to 
YouTube, then send the video to the prospect’s email, Facebook and LinkedIn accounts for 
 


 
 
larger businesses. They can open and watch the video in their own time. Be sure to send your 
YouTube link with an intriguing message. 
 
It also feels good to put out value into the marketplace rather than just pitching every minute of 
the day. 
 
Just like other strategies, you shouldn’t expect clients to fall on your lap. 
 
I sent out a lot of videos and continually refined my strategy to make it work.  
 
Another benefit of the perfect cold video is that you can use it in multiple instances. 
 
- You can pitch your network. They know you unlike a cold audience and will be impressed by 
the work you’ve put in to create them a video (eventually you can steam line the process so 
you’re only spending 5-10 minutes on preparation and roughly 5 minutes per video. You can 
communicate a lot of value in this time and get a high ROI for your time invested into your video 
pitching process). 
 
- Great for doing a site/GMB audit presentation for anyone you meet in the future. 
 
- Gets you prepared for recording engaging content that you can use to promote yourself/your 
agency/your blog etc. 
 
Improving your presentation skills while landing more leads and clients is only a good thing. 
 
One thing I will mention is that I had considerably less luck with small businesses when using 
the perfect cold video pitch. 
 
You can still get results, but these seemingly ‘complex’ plans won’t interest them.  
 
Many small businesses are small for a reason. They don’t want to grow. They are comfortable. 
 
A 6 minute video talking about SEO is off-putting. They have their clients. They don’t care. 
 
However, medium to large-sized businesses love the video pitch because they are actively 
trying to grow. 
 
They will thank you for taking the time out of your day to highlight something to them that may 
not have been aware of. 
 
Targeting medium-sized businesses is a good idea for beginners.  
 
Those with more experience should target both medium and large businesses. 
 


 
 
 
Overall, I think the perfect cold video pitch is worth doing if you are hungry for clients and have 
the time. 
 
Cold Calling 
Good old fashioned grit is required for cold calling.  
 
There’s a reason so many businesses still use telesales.  
 
It’s time consuming, you get a lot of rejections, but it works. 
 
Although there are better ways of contacting businesses, building your cold calling skills won’t 
ever hurt you. 
 
Even if you created your own agency site or SEO lead generation asset, you’ll eventually speak 
to prospects on the phone. 
 
It’s good to be ready for a sales situation, be it on a cold call or an in-person meeting. 
 
Cold calling also gives you instant market feedback.  
 
Let’s say you were calling trade businesses in your local city.  
 
If you scheduled 50 cold calls a day for two weeks, you would know more about your target 
market AND have leads in your pipeline (can follow up with more impactful strategies, like a 
visit, video pitch, video audit or passing them a lead). 
 
Although it’s painful, I wouldn’t completely rule it out.  
 
I still have many of my first clients from cold calling.  
 
I’ve still never met most of them in person as they live across the country! 
 
If I were to start cold calling again tomorrow, I would call businesses advertising in their local 
Facebook ‘business advertisement groups’. 
 
We at least know these businesses want more sales… 
 
And the list should be relatively fresh compared to directories like Yell/Yelp.  
 
Companies on Yell/Yelp get bombarded with calls every day! 
 
 


 
 
 
 
Cold Emailing  
Lots of SEOs and digital marketers have success with cold emailing. You can be a sniper, 
writing unique emails for each prospect, or you can be a shotgun through an automation tool. A 
friend of mine is doing well with the snov.io email automation tool. 
 
I personally never had much success with cold emailing as it’s a battle to capture people’s 
attention. However, if you are ready to put in the time, I think any of the strategies listed will 
work. Email is no different. It will require you to capture attention by words, be proactive and 
probably send a lot of emails.  
 
A relatively black hat strategy I used to pick up local clients was by sending thousands of emails 
to businesses, while pretending to be a trade business.  
 
I would email a huge list of people in a city (recipients usually scraped from Yell) pretending to 
be a commercial cleaning company, or CCTV installation company. 
 
Businesses would reply back interested, then I would use this lead for the ‘Use a lead’ model 
described above.  
 
I hired a scraper from Fiverr to scrape emails from select Yell categories (US readers could 
likely use Yelp or similar directories), uploaded these audiences to Sendgrid ($10-$30ish dollars 
a month), then sent hundreds/thousands of emails a day to the different categories. This is a 
relatively time consuming/messy strategy so I’m not doing it now, but it illustrates the different 
ways you can combine these tactics together to get more local clients.  
 
Another thing to consider is Charles’ book ‘Niche Agency Assault’ on how to build a 6 figure 
agency. Both Charles and the co-author Shen Pollard have made 6+ figure agencies so there 
will be a ton of insights to add to this section! 
Lead Generation Assets 
 
Throughout the book we covered the most important ranking factors for Local SEO in different 
keyword difficulty niches. Ranking a lead generation asset and ranking a client is effectively the 
same thing. The fundamentals of SEO don’t change. You still need to outperform their GMB, 
OnPage, link strategy and so on in order to dominate the SERPs. 
 
That being said, there are some contextual differences seeing as we don’t want to be climbing 
the roof ourselves, or pulling out someone’s teeth. We just want to be the digital marketer 
behind the scenes, creating leads and getting paid for them.  
 


 
 
 
Let’s have a look at lead generation assets in more detail. 
Registering Companies 
Google wants us to have one business registered at an address per GMB they give us. Or we 
need multiple addresses under one business in order to have multiple GMBs (think real estate 
agents that have multiple offices in multiple cities/states). 
 
However, when you set up a GMB, they won’t ask to see any official documents. So you can 
start any GMB without an actual company. 
 
I recently set up two GMBs at one address. One listing wasn’t appearing in Google Maps, so I 
spoke to support and they wanted me to cancel one of them in place of the other. They also 
asked to see details from Companies House the listing I wanted to keep was a registered 
business.  
 
This only happens if you speak to support and they look into your listing manually. If you never 
speak to support, you are free to create GMBs without having an official company registered. 
 
Personally, I don’t bother setting up a company with a GMB until it is making money. Setting up 
a company is one of those procrastination tasks that you don’t need to do. Start making money, 
then make the set up more official.  
 
To make your asset more salable, when you are making money, I would register the business at 
a property you, your family, friends or business partner own. Although you may have created a 
‘black hat’ GMB by verifying it at a virtual office with no staff on site, I don’t see it as a problem 
to then register the business to a new address at a later date. If Google had some sort of update 
or was suspicious that you started at a virtual office, you could simply say that’s the only office 
we could afford when we started, but now we have a registered office. Google is really just 
looking for people who are gaming the system. If you have a legitimate GMB at a legitimate 
address, there’s nothing they can really say. You’re a real business listing at that point, just like 
all the other GMB owners. 
 
I’ve heard the idea of setting up a lead generation company at one address, then registering 
each lead generation asset as a subsidiary of that main business. The benefits would be less 
hassle and less business addresses to register from. 
 
I don’t like that idea. Selling any of the lead generation assets individually would be a problem. 
Also, Google is trying to stop non-local businesses from generating leads from Google. By 
openly admitting that’s what we’re doing (whether legitimately or not) is asking for trouble in my 
opinion.  
 
 


 
 
Domain Choice 
We should think ahead when choosing a domain for any lead generation asset we make. I 
made several city brand rank and rent lead generation assets when I started. Now I can’t really 
push the site into new territories because the domain and whole site is branded towards one 
city. I’m not particularly mad because there are quite a few smaller locations within and around 
my city, but we could be adding new cities to this aged, authority domain had I not branded it so 
narrowly. Now if I use the same niche in another nearby area I’ll have to start a brand new 
domain. 
 
With that said, I still like the idea of keeping each lead generation asset relatively small and not 
venturing into too many states/cities. The reason is simply diversification. Rather than having 
one lead generation asset targeting 20 large states/locations, I’d rather have 5 sites targeting 4 
locations near each other. 
 
Overall, I like a domain which gives me access to all monetization routes. The simple formula for 
this is having a generic name, while still having your main niche keyword in the domain. 
 
Here are some examples: 
 
- yourlocalroofers.com 
- theplumbingcompany.com 
- thedentistcompany.com 
- thecleaningcontractors.com 
 
If we go for a branded name, like jacksvetclinic.com - we can’t rent the site out to another brand, 
whereas when we have a generic name, any business could conceivable put their brand to it.  
Ranking A Lead Generation Asset 
Let’s use our generic domain ‘theroofingcontractors.com’ from above. 
 
We set up our website as if it is a client site called Your Local Roofers.  
 
We do everything we do for a client site if it was called EC Roofers, but we do it for our site 
‘Your Local Roofers’ instead.  
 
NAP 
We’ll need an NAP,  just like any client business. 
 
Ideally, we have a legitimate address to work from (home, family, friend, business partnership), 
so that’s our address sorted. 
 


 
 
 
If I was having huge problems getting a legitimate address or didn’t want to go particularly black 
hat, I would be picking a really easy niche (like a niche/sub-niche essential service) if creating 
citations was going to be a problem. 
 
I’ve got lead generation assets that were completely canonicalized and had citations done 
completely wrong… Even scoring a 0/100 on a local citation checker! They still topped the 
SERPs for SL keywords. 
 
I don’t think this would fly for harder industries, though. 
 
Still, ideally we would secure some sort of legitimate address for our strategy.  
 
Name is obvious, that’s our business name. 
 
The phone number is a little more tricky. 
 
Twilio - ​I use Twilio for all of my lead generation assets. With Twilio, we can purchase a city 
specific phone number for each location landing page we’re going to use. 
 
The beauty of Twilio is that we can redirect this number to any number we choose. That means 
we can either redirect the landline to our mobile, or we could redirect it to a roofer or whatever 
type of business we are working with 
 
Having a city specific number also gives us some local relevancy in Google’s eyes. It also gives 
potential customers confidence because it looks like we have a local area code number. 
 
By keeping the same number on the site, we avoid penalizations from changing our number on 
our GMB multiple times if we ended up renting the site to different people. 
 
Twilio is pretty cost-effective. It’s something like $10 per month and you pay per minute on the 
phone. Don’t worry though, the per minute rate is $0.0130.  
 
Twilio normally takes a bit of time and testing to get set up correctly. It’s not a plugin and play 
software. There are these on the market, but they tend to start at the $40/50 per month range. 
 
Now that our NAP is set up like any other business, we can just optimize the site like any other 
local site 
 
 


 
 
Monetization Strategies 
After we’ve dominated the SERPs using the strategies from this book, we need to get our asset 
monetized. Let’s look at how we do it. 
 
All of our strategies (rank and rent, pay per lead/conversion) all follow a similar path to 
monetization. 
 
I mostly like to redirect all my leads to the partner. This makes for a rank and rent. Even if we 
are getting paid per lead/conversion, redirecting everything to the client is the most passive way 
of running your asset. 
 
You can either choose to be paid a flat monthly fee (low hassle), or you can track emails and 
calls and be paid per conversion or lead.  
 
Twilio documents all your calls and can even record them too. 
 
All emails can easily be tracked too. Have a company email as well as a contact form. All these 
emails can go to you while also being redirected to the client. This leaves no ambiguity 
regarding where leads came from.  
 
Will it get weird? - ​No. Let’s say someone calls Your Local Roofers and the number redirects 
to a ‘real’ local roofer. The local roofer takes the call, books in the job and gets it done. Let’s say 
the customer notices (very unlikely) how their van has a different name than the company they 
remember calling. The roofer can simply say “ah yeah that’s one of our websites” or something 
to that effect. The customer won’t care. They have a problem. They need it solved. A roofer is 
right there to help them. 
 
I’ve almost never had a problem with leads being passed around and I’ve done it en masse by 
this point. Many businesses give other businesses work anyway. Referring customers to other 
businesses isn’t a new concept.  
 
Rebrand to the partner - ​If you get a good partner, someone who pays you, communicates, 
and doesn’t give you hassle, you could offer to rebrand the site for them. 
 
You can add span tags, images etc. to make the site look like it’s their site, at least when 
customers land on the page. 
 
By using span text, Google won’t know the site identity has partially changed. Span text is used 
for decoration, so we can take advantage of that and rebrand to the client without creating 
identity/NAP mishaps.  
 
 


 
 
You would rebrand to your partner for a few reasons: 
 
- Smoother transactions because there’s continuity between your lead generation asset and 
their business (again, not that it’s a problem without rebranding). 
- Gives your partner more confidence in the continuity of what you’re doing for them. 
- You can upsell this exclusivity to them. 
 
I would only do this if I had a really good partner who had proved themselves across a 6+ month 
period.  
Giving Out Leads 
Before you are able to get someone renting your site, you will likely need to take the inquiries 
yourself first. This means redirecting the assets’ number to your mobile. Don’t worry, we’ll look 
at alternatives if you don’t want to take the phone calls! 
 
Taking The Call 
I personally don’t have a problem taking calls for a business, even without a business in place to 
take the leads. Hopefully you will have someone in place to handle any leads that come through 
your asset. This is where your Gold Mine Area becomes useful. If you don’t already know 
someone that provides the service, someone you know might. 
 
If you don’t know someone who provides the service yet, don’t worry. 
 
Customers are likely to call you and say things like this: 
 
“Hi is this X service?” 
 
“Hi do you do XYZ?” 
 
“Hi there, I live in X and my X needs X needs XYZ doing to it. Can you help?” 
 
At this point, we are trying to qualify if the lead is legit. If the lead has no value, there’s no point 
in trying to find someone to fulfill the work. 
 
If the customer hasn’t opened with their problem (which they do a lot of the time), you can 
simply say something like: 
 
“Hi, yes it is, how can I help?” 
 
“Hi yep we do that, what’s the problem?” 
 
 


 
 
We want to know that their problem is worth passing to other businesses. Sometimes you have 
people calling with ridiculous requests, things that wouldn’t be worth even traveling for.  
 
After discovering the problem, we want to know where they are and make sure they are in our 
local area. You get the old call from out of state/city. I tend to not bother with these as much just 
because they’re a rarity and won’t be consistent. Spending loads of time finding a lead 
generation partner out of your area will be a waste when the majority will be within your target 
area. 
 
After discovering they do have a real problem and they’re in your location, you can say the other 
team members are out of the office now, but you will call them back later. You could even ask 
them to email or text you  
 
Getting questioned - ​If your customer starts asking specific questions about the business you 
can’t answer, you can simply say you only take the calls and are unsure. There’s no need for 
any interaction to ever go wrong or leave yourself feeling stuck. You can always just assume the 
position of ignorance!  
 
“Hmm I’m not sure, I just take the calls. I can get the other guy’s to call you when they’re back in 
the office though?” 
 
If you’ve made multiple assets - ​If you’re smart and set up multiple assets, you might not 
know which site is getting the call. If the customer doesn’t declare their problem, they might ask 
you who you are and what you do. You can simply say you own X, Y and Z businesses, can I 
help you with any of those? It’s perfectly reasonable to own multiple businesses. 
 
Basically, when taking the call, we are just trying to qualify the caller to give us a real lead to 
take to business owners. Getting leads proves our concept and we can now approach 
businesses with our leads, knowing there should be more soon as we rise up the SERPs. 
 
One strategy I used when I didn’t have a partner in place was to book the quotation date myself. 
I’d ask when the prospect is free (good information anyway for a business who might go and 
look at one of your leads) - then just say sure, we can come at one of those times. This 
pressured me into finding a business to take care of the leads. Pressure creates diamonds!  
 
I Don’t Want To Take The Call! 
While I was abroad I used this strategy and it went better than I thought it would. 
 
Instead of taking the phone call, I would simply log into Twilio, see who called, then add their 
mobile number (~80% of local calls seem to be through mobile) to WhatsApp. Virtually everyone 
seems to have WhatsApp, even the older generations. The nice part about adding them through 
WhatsApp is that you can see their profile picture, which gives you an idea of who called you.  
 


 
 
 
If they don’t have WhatsApp you can just text them, especially if you’re local and not abroad. 
WhatsApp also allows the customer to see a face, which looks more personable than a text. 
 
I would simply message them saying: 
 
“Hi do you need XYZ service?” 
 
Almost every time they would start replying with their problem.  
 
I think most people would rather text their problem rather than having to call around anyway, so 
it works out well for them too. 
 
The same principles apply as to if you took the call. Find out where they are and what their 
problem is. It’s also pretty reasonable to ask for a photo at this point they have a problem they 
can take a picture of. 
 
Ideally, you would take the call for the sake of snagging the lead before they call someone else, 
but this strategy worked probably 80% of the time, more than I guessed it would before trying it. 
 
Replying to emails - ​If someone emails you, I would stall by thanking them for getting in touch 
and ask for more details. Now it’s on them to reply to you! This will buy you time to find a partner 
and you may even find someone to take this project on. 
Finding A Partner 
You can always try finding a partner before you get your first lead. Unless the asset was in my 
Gold Mine Area and I could easily text a few people to see if they know someone offering XYZ 
service, I would wait until a lead came in. 
 
We already discussed the power of having a lead in the sales chapter. The concept is the exact 
same when trying to find a lead generation partner. 
 
Calling somebody saying you have leads and actually having one right now is different to saying 
you will have one in the near future. The first call is valuable, the second call will be perceived 
as a waste of their time, most likely.  
 
Mind you, I’ve always been surprised how kind everyone is over the phone (in England at least). 
Not many will curse you or be particularly aggressive. Even when I cold called I didn’t 
experience too much hostility.  
 
By having a lead ready to go, you prove your concept of having this lead and more to come. 
 
 


 
 
It’s not a mad dash either. If you still haven’t found someone to take the lead after a few days, 
the lead might have ‘expired’ but you can still just say “I have a lead, they called a few days ago 
looking for XYZ” - so you still gain the credibility over the phone even if the lead isn’t still hot. 
 
But who should you call? 
 
Google - ​A straightforward way of finding partners is to Google your SL keywords and start 
calling businesses. I would go for businesses on the second page and beyond because they’re 
unlikely to be getting many inquiries digitally.   
 
Facebook -​ Another strategy you can use is to post to the local Facebook group of that location 
and ask if anyone does the service your lead was asking for. Local commenters will start giving 
you their recommendations. You also might get the people who offer that service/product getting 
in touch with you. I got a really good partner because I took this approach once and about 10 
people recommended this one company. So the Facebook route is good because you get a 
trusted business in the area.  
 
Directories - ​I would be seriously surprised if you can’t find someone through either of those 
two options that want a lead generated for them. Normally you can get people pretty interested 
in your lead within a few calls. If you do run out, use directories like Yell and Yelp to find more 
businesses offering your service. 
 
Emailing the leads - ​I tried a lot of emailing to give out leads but you have to do this en masse 
to work. It’s the business model that companies like Bark use. They get a lead, then they send 
an email to every tradesman in the area offering to sell the lead. 
 
I never had much success with emailing because you can’t build up a rapport and the offer just 
looks spammy. I much prefer calling.  
 
If you really don’t want to call, the Facebook approach should work and you can send people a 
message through their business or profile page. It’s a bit more personal than an email as your 
face and profile will be shown. Emails are a bit faceless.  
 
The pitch - ​When calling a business I just keep it really basic: 
 
“Hey, do you guys do [whatever the lead wanted]?” 
 
At this point you’ll get a few variations of replies: 
 
“Hey, yeah we do!” 
“Yep we do but we’re really busy right now, we’re booked up for the year” 
“Yes where are you based?” ​etc.  
 
 


 
 
If they want to know more, I would say something like: 
 
“Ok great, well the person that wants it is based in X and he specifically needs XYZ doing. I 
don’t personally need the project doing but they called me for it.” 
 
If the business is interested they will keep replying inquisitively: 
 
“Ah okay, yeah I can help them out, how do you know the customer?” 
 
This is where we have to be cautious and protect our own best interests. Most businesses aren’t 
going to care where the lead came from. There’s no need to say you made a business to collect 
local leads from the top of Google to then outsource to the businesses in the area. That sounds 
callous.  
 
Instead, while we don’t know these people, I would simply say something like: 
 
“Ah yeah I’m running digital marketing Ads to collect leads and these guys called me. There’s 
going to be plenty more but I wanted to start passing them out.” 
 
This is where you will find out if the business is interested in your kind of offer. They understand 
what is implied. You are a business who is offering a service and there will be some kind of 
charge for the lead. We aren’t offering marketing, we are offering what marketing provides. We 
effectively have the sale, we aren’t selling a service. 
 
Those who want more work will still be interested. Those who have enough work and don’t want 
to pay for any inquiries will tell you at this juncture.  
 
Pay attention to this point in the conversation.  
 
We will be giving out leads for free at first to build relationships, but we want to find out if the 
business is still interested in the conversation while knowing there could be a payment for the 
lead… 
 
If they instantly start disregarding your notion of collecting leads yourself, you don’t need to 
mention how you will give it to them for free. They probably don’t want to pay at any point in the 
future because they have enough work or don’t like the sound of the agreement. Thank them for 
their time and move on. 
 
Now, if the prospect still sounds interested knowing there could be some sort of paid deal here, 
you can ask if they can take the lead on. 
 
“So can you take this project on? I can always message them and pass your phone number 
over.” 
 


 
 
 
Some businesses have long waiting lists before they can take on more work and we don’t really 
have time for that. If the waiting list isn’t too long, they sound professional and will still give a 
quote or whatever their niche does to contact the prospect, it would still be fine to pass them the 
lead. Being busy is actually a good sign. However, if they have a 6+ month waiting list, there 
probably isn’t much point. We need conversions and payments faster.  
 
After finding out they want to use the lead, you can find out if they would like more leads soon. 
 
“Ok great that’s that one sorted. Could you take on more of these leads?’ 
 
Hopefully they’re still keen. Lots of businesses will be! Imagine if someone just called you out of 
the blue tomorrow, offering you an SEO client with the promise for more. You’d be delighted!  
 
If you feel like the other person over the phone is reciprocating and feeling like you would if 
someone was giving you SEO leads, you can tell them how you want the project to be 
monetized.  
 
“Awesome, well I’m happy to give you this lead for free, maybe you could throw me a little 
change if it converts, but then I’d be interested in setting up some sort of model that works for 
both of us when more leads do come!” 
 
There’s no need to be shy or scared here. You have a valuable offering, you’re happy to give 
him a “tester” of your lead with no upfront fee and you would like to be compensated for your 
hard work!  
 
Still, you don’t need to be overly aggressive. I mention that I want to get paid and we can 
discuss that later, but we will keep it reasonable, of course. 
 
A strength of giving out leads is that you can now send the next lead to the next business you 
find. Before you know it, you have 5 or 6 leads being pursued by several companies.  
 
You’ll get a really good feeling for the person you want to give more leads to and who sounds 
honest/genuine.  
 
I like asking for a kickback off any project because that’s the ultimate test to see if someone is 
worth working with. The first person to send you a commission is the person you can start 
channeling the majority of leads to, while perhaps dripping the occasional lead to any of the 
other seemingly credible businesses. 
 
Many people in business will be happy to pay you, especially if you have effectively made them 
money for free. Now whenever they deal with you... They are in profit already! You have already 
made them money, it’s only up from here. 
 


 
 
 
I have sold a couple of leads over the phone by asking for a payment, but it makes the 
relationship sour as you’re instantly ‘winning’ in the value exchange. They’ve ‘lost’ money, 
you’ve gained money and all they have is a hope. Charging for leads up front is also a losing 
battle if the lead doesn’t convert.  
 
Give your leads out for free, but first qualify them so they aren’t just taking the lead because it’s 
free.  
 
The lead expired - ​If you get back in contact with the customer to recommend your new partner 
and they’ve found someone else, don’t worry. You can call back your partner, explain to him 
they’ve found someone else, but now we know each other and I can just give the next inquiry 
straight to you. This is still a success because now you have someone ready to take your next 
inquiry. 
 
After you have an established partner or two, it becomes easy to give out the leads. You can 
take the call and say: 
 
“Ah we’re out of action at the moment, but we would recommend XYZ business, want their 
email?” 
 
Or, now that you have a partner, you can use the WhatsApp technique and simply take landline 
calls. Emails are the same, you just say you’re out of action and recommend your partner. If I 
pass on any call or email, I give my partner a quick message to say Bob will be emailing you 
about his XYZ problem, by the way!  
 
Eventually your partner might be more curious about what you do. If the relationship is good, 
you can tell them and offer exclusivity if you wish. Or you can keep using your lead generation 
asset to speak to more local business owners and land more Local SEO clients. The choice is 
yours. I would be using it to speak to more business owners and creating a few partners if I was 
new to all of this, personally. 
 
When you have several lead generation assets and several clients, that’s when you might want 
to put them on autopilot and simply redirect the inquiries to a specific partner, count the leads at 
the end of the month and count your profits!  
 
Wrapping up Monetization - ​So that’s the formula I use to harness leads and create 
partnerships. Each niche might be slightly different for how you approach the situation, but the 
principles should crossover with most local niches for your lead generation asset.  
 
 


 
 
More Lead Generation Ideas To Consider 
 
Partnering up - ​In the ‘local lead generation gold mine niche’ section towards the start of this 
book, I spoke about how you don’t want to partner up on a lead generation asset with a friend 
who’s just looking for free leads. 
 
However, partnering up with them for a fee can be a great option. Instead of making your asset 
alone, you can charge a small monthly fee for it to be created and they get exclusivity of leads. 
Think about it: 
 
- You are going to make the asset anyway, but now you are getting paid for it. 
 
- You get a partner in place from day one 
 
- They might have a spare address to create the GMB from  
 
From their perspective: 
 
- They don’t have to pay full price for SEO 
 
- They are getting exclusivity when the leads come in 
 
- They are getting an extra lead generation asset for their business, too 
 
Other things to consider: 
 
Obviously, go with someone you trust. Don’t do this with someone random. Even if the 
relationship goes pear-shaped, you shouldn’t be tied to them. Use your own NAP if possible.  
 
Be clear that you own the asset, they are simply paying to speed up the process (so you can 
reinvest into the site) as well as getting exclusivity of the leads when they come.  
 
You don’t have to charge a whole lot. Even $100-$200 a month is going to help. You can also 
put an agreement in place for how they will pay you when the leads start coming in. This could 
be a higher monthly fee or switching to a pay per lead/conversion model. 
 
This strategy is a win-win for both parties. The faster you can get your lead generation assets 
monetized the better. I’ve used this strategy about 5 times and will continue to do so. 
 
 
 


 
 
Separating assets - ​I wouldn’t be keeping all my assets in overlapping Google accounts. So 
instead of adding all your different sites and GMBs to the same analytics, webmasters etc. - I 
would reduce the footprint of any asset you make by creating dedicated accounts per asset. 
 
I would even use Ghost Browser cloaking software when phone verifying GMBs a couple of 
years ago. However, when using an actual physical address like at a friend/family’s house, you 
don’t need to use cloaking. I would still keep all my assets separate. 
 
Definitely don’t have more than one GMB in the same Google My Business Locations Manager 
page. 
 
Speed matters - ​With your first project you want to be meticulous. You don’t want to screw 
anything up. While this is natural, you should be cautious about the right things and speedy 
about things that don’t matter as much. 
 
Basically we want to be careful with everything that moves the needle - and speed up anything 
that doesn't. 
 
Before our site is getting impressions, clicks and leads - we should not be worrying about 
anything that doesn't help us with that goal.. If we aren’t getting traffic, we don’t need to worry 
about conversion optimization. Focus on things in chronological order. I’ve seen loads of SEOs 
come along and make a lead generation asset in a month which is far too slow. Especially for a 
basic site in an easy niche. 
 
Things that do matter: 
 
- Having a good niche 
- Knowing why it’s a good niche (e.g. low competition, good keyword volume) 
- Good content & OnPage strategy 
- Optimizing your GMB  
- Having strong local link opportunities 
 
Things that don’t matter until we’re getting traffic: 
 
- Finding the perfect images (any images take time to find, let alone good ones) 
- Branding and colors 
- Styling your website to have great proportions 
- Anything that doesn’t help us generate more traffic!  
 
How can I say I’m an experienced business when I’m not? - ​So you’ve gone to write your 
content for your new tree surgery lead generation asset. You realize you can’t say 0 seconds 
experience. But you also don’t want to say 20 years of experience. 
 


 
 
 
What do you do?  
 
Be honest. Don’t lie. But don’t tell the whole truth, either. 
 
You are a local tree surgery company who is interested in helping people out with their tree 
surgery needs! How you go about doing that is beside the point. 
 
Best lead generation asset niches - ​My favorite style of lead generation asset is a service 
area business because we don’t need a storefront. 
 
Further to that, I like industries that don’t have much personality or branding, meaning I can 
blend in.  
 
You can opt to simply dominate the SERPs in any industry and rent out the position to any 
niche, but I like the flexibility of service area businesses in that I can give out leads to different 
businesses. 
 
Here’s a list of niches that would work well as lead generation assets. I either own assets in 
these niches or believe they would be a suitable niche. Of course, some locations/markets will 
be much better for some niches than others. That part is for you to figure out! 
 
Commercial cleaning 
Window cleaning 
Carpet cleaning 
 
Carpentry & joinery  
Loft conversions 
Bathroom/kitchen remodeling  
Roofing 
Fascias and guttering 
Siding 
Flooring 
Windows 
Tree surgery 
Landscaping 
Fencing 
Painting 
Plumbing 
 
Security systems 
CCTV installation 
 
 


 
 
Legal (there are many subdivisions within law!) 
Mobile mechanic 
Tax advice 
Pension advice 
Buying/selling property 
Wedding events (DJ, photographer, equipment rental) 
 
You can get really granular with most niches. I would spend a decent amount of time picking my 
niche. If you’re going to own this site for years, it makes sense to invest a lot of time picking a 
great niche and location.  
 
Common Mistakes To Avoid 
 
Picking a rubbish niche - ​Like I just said, you might own this asset for years. Put the time in at 
the start by researching loads of niches and weighing your options up against each other. 
 
Not taking it easy - ​Yes, you have some great strategies from this book, but beginners should 
stick to easy niches. You can still make hundreds if not thousands a month with a single asset in 
easy niches. There’s no need to try anything really complex. Prove to yourself the concept 
works, develop your skills, pick up clients - then look at more advanced lead generation asset 
strategies. 
 
Just relying on one strategy -​ Although you can dominate easy niches without much 
advanced SEO, it’s still better to take the approach laid out by the book. Don’t worry, it shouldn’t 
be a lot of work to dominate your competition in terms of content, OnPage, links, citations, GMB 
optimization etc.  
 
Investing time incorrectly - ​Remember the Moz pie chart. Sure, the percentages can’t be 
gospel, but they’re a good general guide. An hour spent trying to build a local link is infinitely 
more valuable than an hour spent on Pixabay looking for the perfect image for your site which 
has no traffic right now.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 


 
 
Conclusion 
 
So that’s everything you needed to know in order to create your own Local Kingdom...  
 
- You know how to do local keyword research, enabling you to identify great opportunities. 
 
- You know how to dominate your competition through all the major ranking factors.  
 
- You know which niches you should enter according to your experience level.  
 
- You know how to get Local SEO clients. 
 
- You know how to create your own lead generation assets. 
 
All of these skills combined allow you to make a serious living from Local SEO alone.  
 
Whether you want to work at an agency, start a side hustle or build a booming business - 
there’s nothing holding you back now.  
 
I want to stress two final points to you. 
Firstly, to all beginners, don’t be intimidated by Local SEO. It’s actually very, very basic. You 
should start in easy niches and you shouldn’t have a problem dominating them if you follow the 
advice from this book. You aren’t competing with SEOs for the most part! So how can you lose? 
 
The second point is how we should push for these easy wins. Yes, high paying clients in hard 
niches are good. Yes, having a lead generation asset in a hard niche is good. But one of the 
main reasons we get into Local SEO is because of how easy most of the landscape is.  
 
There are unlimited local businesses that have 0 SEO. Many of these businesses are making 6 
figures plus a year. You now know how to either team up with these businesses - or dominate 
them yourself.  
 
It’s actually hard to fathom how many local businesses there are out there - and how many lead 
generation assets could be made. The possibilities are truly, truly endless in this industry. I 
really hope you are able to lean into it and make all the money you ever wanted. The 
opportunity is there - you just have to go and grab it!  
 
-
Edward Rostron 
 
 
 


 
 
What we didn’t cover in this eBook is how to compete in competitive local SERPs (Like real 
estate or lawyers) and how to compete in large cities where you have hundreds or even 
thousands of competitors. 
 
In the 2 FREE bonuses that are included in Local Kingdom, these 2 things are exactly what we 
cover. 
 
I hope you enjoyed this eBook, and thank you for purchasing with us!

---

title: The Six-Figure Freelance SEO
content:
The Six-Figure Freelance SEO
By Charles Floate


Introduction
Going it alone in any field can be extremely scary, and SEO can be even more
so… You’ve got expensive subscriptions, evolving algorithm updates, headache
clients and much, more to deal with... It’s the “what if’s?” that will keep you up at
night if you aren’t careful and prepared.
This eBook is meant to answer all those questions, squash all those fears and
set you up for success in your freelance career.
Please bear in mind that you’re going to be a unique case, and everybody
reading this is going to be a unique case. Things do very much so depend on:
●Your Location
●Your Language/s
●Your Expertise
●Your Previous Experience
●Your Own Work & Case Studies
●Referrals & Testimonials
That all being said, we’re in the best time ever to be working remotely and with a
real abundance of opportunities in the SEO space - There are currently more
freelance SEO contracts in the UK up for grabs than there ever has been before.
Depending on how far you are on your path to freelance freedom, you can use
the Table of Contents on the next page to skip to the parts that are most relevant
to you - If you’re just beginning out, then you should always start from the, you
guessed it, beginning.
Competition can feel fierce when you’re just starting out, but once you have a
solid brand and some case studies under your belt, you’ll often find yourself
overwhelmed with work. I decline about 98% of the leads I get nowadays, though
even those are not lost in value to me, which I’ll explain later on.
I’m not going to teach you how to actually do SEO or the strategies I use to get
results for websites and clients. Instead, I’m going to show you how to pick the
most profitable client route, how to set up your business, how to build a brand,
how much you should be charging, how to get clients and so, so much more!


I really hope you enjoy this eBook, get the most out of it you possibly can and I’d
like to thank you for purchasing the copy.
Without further ado, let’s jump into it.


Introduction
2
What Services Should I Offer?
5
Niche Down
5
Using Google Trends
6
Using Ahrefs
9
Single vs. Multiple Services
13
Service Provider vs. Consultant
16
How Do I Set Up And Run A Freelancing Business Technically?
18
Signing Up For A Business Entity
18
Establish Your Brand
22
Set Up Your Website
22
Create And Update Your Profiles
24
How Much Should I Charge? (Quoting)
26
Scouring The Competition
27
My Rates Over The Years
31
Should You Publish Your Rates Or Not?
32
Charging Higher When You’re Starting Out
33
Leverage Social Proof
33
Write Case Studies
35
Where Will I Get Clients?
37
Referrals
37
Job Boards
38
Search Engines
43
Social Media
47
Cold Email Outreach
49
How Will I Manage My Projects?
58
How Should I Deliver My Projects?
63
How To Get Paid In Full?
70


How Do I Manage Clients’ Expectations?
72
Hiring VAs & Apprentices
73
Next-Level Freelancing
76
A Freelancers Toolbox
77
Transitioning Your Skillset Into Other Projects
78
Thank You & Conclusion
79
What Services Should I Offer?
The very first thing you should be deciding in on your freelance path is which
service to offer. This is going to be the backbone of your business, and the route
to profitability in your first few months.
I don’t advise trying to be a full service one man band - There are very few
people who can start out and build a website, do all the graphics, social media
and all the intricacies of SEO on top, and even fewer who are just starting out.
Likewise, SEO can be extremely competitive in big cities like New York, Toronto,
Sydney or London. It’s going to be much more difficult to both acquire the clients
because of your competition, and it’s going to be more difficult to deliver results
because of their competition.
Therefore, the first thing you must do as you venture into this new career is to
determine the kind of SEO freelancer you want to be based on the services you
will offer.
Having a clear offer on the table will set in motion the other aspects of your
business.
Below are ways to help position yourself that will help you stand out from the
pack:
Niche Down
The key to getting your SEO freelancing career off the ground is finding your
niche and sticking with it.
As mentioned, SEO is a very competitive industry. Somebody is most likely
providing the same services that you plan on offering to your prospects. And


trying to out-SEO more established freelance SEOs out there is going to take a
lot of work.
However, instead of worrying about your competitors, focus on what you can do
for your target audience. In this case, it’s less about standing out from the crowd
and more about finding your crowd and marketing yourself exclusively to this
group of people.
To do this, you need to find a segment in your target audience you want to focus
on — The more granular, the better.
You could do client SEO for tenant lawyers in America, you could do SEO for
construction companies in London, or you could build a personal SEO brand
locally (Host meetups, do Zooms, giveaways, videos, blogs etc) and get put on
retainers to help digital agencies in your city with their SEO clients.
Ideally, the more you know about something, the better. One of ViperChill’s most
successful clients was a dentist turned dental marketing agency that ended up
selling for a cool $28 million.
Using Google Trends
From here, head on to Google Trends to compare the popularity of these niches.
The great thing about Google Trends, aside from being free, is comparing
keywords side by side to see which one is more popular.
For example, let’s say you worked campaigns before for a personal injury lawyer,
printing company, and pest control business.
Entering these keywords in Google Trends, we get these results:


Take note that the United States is the default location when you search Google
Trends. If you live in a different area, make sure to change the settings to your
country or region.
Using this information, you should determine which industries you entered as
search terms are the most popular.
In the examples above, “printing” has the highest interest compared to the other
two. However’ “printing” is very broad and could mean a lot of things. Therefore,
it’s best to narrow your search to “printing services.” By adding the last word in
the search query, you’re referring to businesses that offer printing as a service.
Using this new search term to compare with the other two, Google Trends
showed drastically different results:


From the new results, “pest control” now has the highest interest, with “personal
injury lawyer” not far behind.
Scrolling down the page, you will see the breakdown of searches across the
different regions of your chosen country for the search term.
It is crucial if you want to offer your SEO services to specific regions or states.
Ideally, you want to target states where the search term has the highest interest.
But for now, you can just keep this in mind and create landing pages for these
regions once you start doing local SEO.
At the bottom, you should also find the top searched terms related to the keyword
you entered.


From here, you will see the type of keywords people are searching for in your
respective industry. It gives you an idea as to which terms you should target.
Some of the terms include the location, ex., Pennsylvania personal injury lawyer,
which ties back into the local SEO I mentioned earlier.
Using the data from Google Trends allows you to see which states or regions
have search queries for the industries that you can leverage moving forward
should you decide on offering SEO services for them.
Using Ahrefs
Once you have decided on a niche to target with your SEO services, use a
keyword research tool to outline the type of keywords you can optimize for clients
in this industry. You can also look at competitors ranking for the top keywords in
your industry.
Log in to your Ahrefs account and enter the niche keyword on the text bar before
clicking on the magnifying glass icon.


In the example above, you should see the keyword data for “pest control” on the
next page.
However, what we want to see are the keyword ideas related to it. Therefore,
scroll down the page to the Keyword ideas section to see how many related
keywords to tool found for your query.
The volume of keywords found by Ahrefs for your industry could indicate how
profitable it is to focus on this niche. The more keywords the tool returns, the
more opportunities you can create pages for each one, the more work you can
get from clients.
However, you still need to verify if the keywords have all the data you need to
determine which you can create and optimize for potential clients. In this case,
you need to analyze the results and find which among the keywords you can use
for your campaigns.
Click on View all to check out all the keywords under each. For this example, let’s
view all question keywords. These are the keywords you can use to optimize
your informational blog posts.
You want to filter them from the list to show you keywords with at least DR 0 and
a search volume of at least 10.
The idea here is to trim keywords that don’t have values for their DR and search
volume. It isn’t because they are the wrong keywords, but Ahrefs doesn’t have
the information for these keywords to provide you with a proper value for each
variable. Therefore, it’s best to leave these keywords for now.


From 7,217 keywords, the filters we set narrowed the results down to 181. From
here, you can export the keywords for your reference later. Do the same process
for the other keyword ideas in the tool.
Next, you can compare the data you gathered from the previous industry to the
next. After going through the keywords for “pest control,” let’s see if “personal
injury lawyer” has more keywords or not.
From the onset, it seems like “pest control” has more keywords than “personal
injury lawyer.”
Narrowing down the question keywords using the same filters we used above,
“pest control” has more keywords and total search volume compared to this one.


From the data we’ve gathered, does it automatically mean that “pest control” is a
much better niche than “personal injury lawyer?”
Not necessarily.
As mentioned, this information tells us that there’s more opportunity for you to
create campaigns for clients. However, we’re not considering the budget a
regular client in a niche has for SEO.
The average pest control case can range from $100 - $5,000, but the average
personal injury lawyer fee will be on the lower end of a few $1,000 at the very
minimum - Meaning the average law firm will likely have a much higher budget
per lead and monthly marketing budget than the average pest control firm will.q
There are no right or wrong answers here. It’s up to you to decide which among
the following niche you should target with your SEO services. You’ll also want to
review the actual people already ranking in the positions you’re reaching out to
clients in, if the SEO agencies have been putting in work for a while it might be
an uphill battle in that niche.
At the very least, you can use the process above to help you narrow your search
to industries that you’re confident in working on and that will provide you with the
best opportunities from financial and professional standpoints.


Single vs. Multiple Services
As an SEO specialist in your previous work, you may have handled different
responsibilities to help run campaigns for your clients. You have experience in
keyword research, content creation, link building, and other SEO-related tasks.
On the one hand, you prefer focusing on offering a particular aspect of SEO as a
service. For example, you are good at keyword research but don’t consider
yourself a good writer.
On the other, you’re confident in your ability to create and run successful SEO
campaigns from scratch. All you need is the client’s site, and you’ll take care of
the rest, from auditing the site for issues to reporting how your campaign fared
over time.
From here, you have to decide whether to focus on promoting a single SEO
service or offering multiple services that clients can pick and choose for their
strategy and campaign.
Single-service SEO freelancers like content writers and link builders are often the
most in demand as it allows you to focus exclusively on one specific SEO task to
sell to your audience, and become the best at that specific task or tactic.
An example of a single-service SEO freelance is SEO copywriter Jessica Foster
of Key&Copy. She offers different content writing services and packages to
clients ranging from content planning to content overhaul aside from blog posts
and landing page content.


Below are some of the things you must consider when limiting your offer to a
single service as SEO freelancers:
●Simpler and more straightforward - All you need to worry about is your
core competency, making this perfect for newbie SEO freelancers. For
example, if you’re a writer, you simply need to request the content briefs of
the topics they want you to write for them. Included in the briefs are the
keywords you must optimize for each content. You could offer keyword
research since it’s connected with content creation, but you can offer it as
an add-on service to clients.
●Greater opportunity for specialization - You can position yourself as the
go-to SEO service provider for a particular niche and industry. Since you’re
not bombarded with other SEO tasks, you can develop and refine your
skills for this specific service so you can eventually offer a higher amount
to upcoming clients in the future.
●Lower earning potential - While you can make a sizable income just by
offering a single SEO service, it could pale compared to SEOs providing
multiple services. It is due to the degree of difficulty required to develop
and launch a comprehensive SEO strategy.


On the other hand, offering complete SEO services gives you full control over
which tactics to implement, which you feel will help the client’s site and how you
plan on rolling them out.
Most multiple-service SEO freelancers own agencies where they work with
employees and other lower-level freelancers to help execute their campaigns. An
example is Robbie Richards, who manages his blog but is a co-founder of Virayo
Marketing.
But before you can hire him for his services, you must book a strategy session
call with him. If you click on the “Work With Me” button, you will be redirected to
Virayo’s website, where you can fill out the strategy session form.
The process allows Robbie to determine what the issues of your site are and how
his services can help improve your site’s performance on SERPs.
If you check his website, you won’t see the prices published on the site. The only
way you’ll find it is if you signed up for a free strategy session with Robbie.
If you decide on offering multiple SEO services as a freelancer, you need to be
away wary of these factors:
●More complex and complicated - Since you’ll be dealing not only with
implementing tactics here but also developing strategies for your clients,
you’ll have your hands full from the very start. You will most likely need
help from VAs or other freelancers to help you get the job done. Due to the


degree of difficulty required to complete the project with flying colors, SEOs
with experience managing multiple clients and connections with other
freelancers in the industry will benefit the most from this track.
●Higher earning potential - The scope of work should result in a much
higher payday from the projects and clients.
●Scalable - Once you reach a point when you can’t handle all of your
projects, it’s normal to transition from being freelancers to becoming
agency and business owners. From here, you can work near other
freelancers on your project. Aside from earning more, you can delegate
your tasks to other freelancers or your employees and oversee the projects
and find ways to generate leads and clients to your business. However,
you may have to register using a different business entity once you launch
an agency.
For starters, it’s best to offer a single service to your clients as an SEO
freelancer. It allows you to focus on honing your craft while learning the tricks of
the trade.
Once you’ve gotten a better handle of things, you can offer additional services to
clients on top of the current work you’re doing for this.
For example, if you’re creating SEO content for them, you can offer to do the
keyword research and outline subject to their approval. Whatever will help make
the process much easier and more convenient for your clients, suggest that as
an extra service to help you earn more from them.
If your clients are looking for services that you don’t offer, you can tap into your
network of SEO professionals and collaborate with them on your projects (and
potentially theirs). This way, you continue to provide value to clients while
learning new things from other freelancers you will be working with.
Service Provider vs. Consultant
Most of the things we discussed above apply to service-based SEO freelancers.
However, what if you’re more interested in offering bespoke strategies for
businesses? Instead of implementing the process, you’re responsible for
developing a custom strategy for clients based on the information you gathered
about the site.


It is precisely what SEO consultants do. They review a client’s site and provide
them with a plan designed to help the site increase its search engine rankings
once implemented.
I have been offering my SEO consultation services on the website on top of the
courses I sell.
My goal is to leverage my knowledge in SEO as a subject matter expert by
offering my advice to clients through hour-long calls. Over time, I’ll follow up on
them to see if they implemented my recommendations and whether or not they
need another strategy call with me to continue improving their site’s performance.
That’s the scope of my work as a consultant. I can recommend them SEO
service providers who will help execute the strategy for them. But I can’t
personally launch the campaign for them.
The SEO consultant model is a fascinating approach to one’s SEO freelance
career. But if you want to pursue this track, below are things you must know first:
●You don’t have to do the heavy lifting - The beauty of being an SEO
consultant is you simply provide them with a map to follow and help guide
their website to success. It’s up to them on whether on not to act up upon
your recommendations. But that’s beyond your responsibility once you’ve
shared your advice with them.


●You provide a holistic approach to SEO - Let’s get one thing straight:
SEO consultation is not just lip service to clients. Not only are you giving
them the keys to ranking their sites on the first page of Google, but you’re
also allowing them to pick your brain for questions they have. And the most
challenging part of all is you need to provide a sufficient answer to their
query. You can’t say you don’t know the answers because that defeats the
purpose of booking a consultation with you. And expect clients to ask you
questions about web design, coding, and other factors that aren’t ranking
factors but help influence their performance on SERPs.
●You need authority to succeed - Ultimately, being a consultant means
people have to trust your advice and recommendations. And to achieve
that, you must build clout first before you can expect to get clients. I
developed my authority through the years with my case studies, courses,
client work, and testimonials. That’s why I’m able to provide consultation
services for various clients with my pricing. Therefore, if you’re brand new
to the game, you need to find a way to build your authority fast so you can
get high-ticket clients coming your way.
In other words, being an SEO consultant demands an incredible amount of
knowledge about the SEO industry as a whole so you can provide all the help
your clients need. If you’re not yet up for the challenge, it’s best to offer SEO
services for now until you’re ready.
How Do I Set Up And Run A Freelancing Business Technically?
Now that you’ve decided on the type of service you want to offer, you can now
proceed with setting up your business the right way.
This section discusses the business side of being a freelancer and how you can
make your initial splash as an SEO freelancer in the market.
Signing Up For A Business Entity
The biggest concern you should have when becoming a freelancer is its legal
aspect. You can’t practice as a freelancer without the necessary licenses from
your state and not get caught.


For instance, if you don’t pay your annual taxes, the IRS will eventually find out
and send you a letter demanding that you pay the fee plus interest and penalties.
Not paying for this amount may result in jail time.
To avoid all this hassle, you need to comply with the rules and determine the type
of business entity you want to sign up. It is important because it allows you to
receive tax benefits and deductions. At the same time, some clients would like to
work exclusively with freelancers operating as a business.
The most common entity that freelancers in the US take is sole proprietorship
(sole trader in the UK). Filing federal taxes is more manageable, and the overall
tax rate is the lowest compared to other business entities.
The biggest drawback of sole proprietorship is there is no legal separation
between the business and freelancer.  You, as a freelancer, can be charged for
liabilities caused by your business.
There are other business entity types to choose from, all of them with their pros
and cons.


Source: Upwork
However, since signing up for sole proprietorship is the simplest, you should go
with this business entity for now.
How to sign up as sole proprietor depends on your location. In the USA, you
must go to your nearest Small Business Development Center for advice on the
next steps you must take in your city and state, as each has its own rules and
processes.


Net, you need to come up with a name for your business. Usually, you can just
use your full name for this. But if you want to brand your SEO services, you can
brainstorm for a name you can trademark.
Search the United States Patent and Trademark Office (USPTO) to find out
which names are available. You can use the name you will find here for
inspiration to come up with brand new ones.
You can also use a domain registrar like Namecheap to search for a name you
can sign up for. It will be important as well once you start setting up your website
later on.
Note: When coming up with a domain and business name, you don’t have to
include words related to your chosen industry. For example, if you wish to offer
your SEO services exclusively to pest exterminators, you don’t have to include
the words “pest” or “pest control” in the business/domain name.
Once you brand your name with your industry, you’re painting yourself into a
corner that you can’t get out of. Assuming you want to branch out to other
sectors, you won’t be able to do so if your domain name alludes to pest control.
In this case, you’ll have to buy a new domain name altogether.
Therefore, to avoid additional trouble, come up with an inclusive domain name
that best describes your service and allows you to branch out to other industries
as well.
After coming up with a name that isn't trademarked yet, file it with the USPTO as
soon as you can to secure the trademark before somebody else does.


The name that you will trademark here will also be your Doing Business As
(DBA) name. You will most likely be asked to create separate business and
personal funds for yourself if you continue this route. In this case, there will be
additional steps you must take after registration.
Establish Your Brand
As a freelancer, you need to consider yourself as a brand. Crafting a brand that
not only speaks volumes about your services but also resonates with your
audience will help you set yourself apart from the pack.
I can’t tell you how you’re going to create your brand, you need to show your own
personality and get your own writing style over time.
What I can do, is show you the basics of how to set your online branding up.
Set Up Your Website
After securing your DBA and creating your logo, it only makes sense to create a
website using the same name you signed up for. If you already have a website,
trademark the domain name you used for your website, so you don’t have to
create another name. But if you want to start from scratch, secure the trademark
of your DBA first, then buy the domain name.
Secure the domain as early as now makes sense before you don’t want
somebody else scooping up the same name as yours before you. There are
other domain registrars where you can sign up your DBA aside from
Namecheap.
Among top-level domains (TLDs), .com is the most common and the most
popular. However, new and trendy TLDs like .io have been picking up steam
lately because it helps them stand out from the pack. Ideally, .com remains the
best choice if you’re starting because most people associate websites with this
TLD instead of anything else.
Also, look for registrars that offer website hosting so you can set up your website
in the same place to make things much more convenient for you.
Better yet, look for the best-performing web hosting providers that will offer your
site the best loading speed and customer support. As you should know now,


having a fast-loading and efficient website will help it rank higher on Google,
thanks to the Page Experience Update.
To help you choose your host, here’s a list of the best hosting companies I
personally use, without affiliate links, in no particular order:
●FlyWheel
●Kinsta
●WPX Hosting
●SiteGround
●NameCheap Hosting
●A2Hosting
●LiquidWeb
●WPEngine
●CloudWays
●DigitalOcean
Once you’ve secured your domain name and web host, you can easily set up
your website from here. Most hosting providers have a one-click WordPress
install, which I suggest you use for your website. It’s relatively quick and easy to
set up, and customizable for SEO.
You don’t have to create a website as there are other things you need to
determine up. However, assuming that you already have your WordPress
website up and running, you can create a Coming Soon page for it using a
plugin.


Among the different plugins above, I prefer SeedProd. However, you can’t go
wrong with most of them since they’re set-and-forget plugins. After downloading
any of the coming soon plugins, just activate them and choose the design and
text people will see after visiting your page. And don’t forget to upload your logo
here.
While setting up a coming soon page isn’t necessary, it helps you brand your site
as you figure things out.
Create And Update Your Profiles
Next, you can begin updating your business profiles. The goal is to let people
and employers know that you’ve set up shop as an SEO freelancer, and they can
hire you soon.
Start with your social media sites like LinkedIn, Twitter, and Facebook. In
particular, you should put more weight on LinkedIn, which is the most prominent
social networking site for professionals.
Change your bio on all these sites to inform them that you’re now accepting
freelance work.


Also, don’t forget to link to your website from here so they know where they can
reach out to you. While your site doesn’t have any content yet, it’s still good to
point to your site from your social media profiles. The worst they’ll see here is
your coming soon page upon clicking on your link.
Also, consider uploading the logo you created as your profile picture on some of
these pages. If not, you can create a Facebook and LinkedIn business page
separate from your profile to brand it with your logo, colors, and more.
Next, update your profiles on Indeed, Monster, and other job sites that you used
to apply for jobs back in the day. Aside from updating your resume, you want to
ensure that employees know you are available for freelance work. Most job sites


require you to select an option from your profile to show that you accept
freelancing work.
Finally, consider creating profiles on freelance marketplace sites like Upwork,
People Per Hour, Freelancer.com, etc. These are places where you can apply for
freelance jobs to help you secure clients as you jumpstart your career. Later on,
we’ll discuss how you can apply for jobs relevant to your SEO skills.
How Much Should I Charge? (Quoting)
The next step in establishing your SEO freelance business is identifying your
price point.
How much you will charge for your SEO services works as a positioning strategy.
It helps shape the perception you want your target audience to have towards
your business.
At the same time, your price should also indicate the quality of work you will
provide clients.
There are two spectrums in SEO pricing: the cheap ones and the premium ones.
Typically, lower-priced services are looked down upon by the industry. As the
saying goes, “you get what you pay for.” Those who bought from sites like Fiverr,
whose providers are selling services cheaply, are most likely seeing decreased
rankings across search engines.
While there are a few exceptions, the consensus towards lower-priced SEO
providers is to simply avoid them.
On the other hand, there are premium SEO service providers that are still total
garbage - Look at services like TheHoth that are overcharging clients by 5-15x
on link building services.
The more years of providing consistent SEO success for their past and current
clients, the higher the price for your services should be. As another saying goes,
“the proof is in the pudding.”
Now, you must find where you currently stand as an SEO freelancer. At this
point, you are an unproven commodity at best as a freelancer due to your limited
experience.


You may have had success working at an agency, but the game is different when
working solo in the industry. You don’t have the resources and financial backing
of a company as everything — from managing client expectations to executing
the plan — falls solely on your shoulders with no one to help you carry it, at least
while you’re starting.
Therefore, you’re most likely to find yourself charging on the lower end of the
spectrum. You have to start somewhere as a freelancer. And unless you have the
clout and experience to boot right off the bat, charging a premium for your
services is like putting too much food in your mouth and choking. You might bag
clients at the end of the day because they are unwilling to pay your asking price
with nothing to show for.
Scouring The Competition
