●
The articles containing the backlinks are thin and of inferior quality. 
●
The above practices are prevalent on most of your site pages. 
●
You or someone else reports your site and practices to Google. 
The last one will only happen if someone’s out to get your site penalized so badly. But the 
chances of that happening are slim to none. 
Also, to help you decide further whether or not to accept their offer, here’s a question to ask 
yourself: 
If you’re not getting paid to publish those links, would you still do it? 
If the answer is “yes,” then you shouldn’t be accused of participating in link schemes if you have 
a good reason to include the links in your blog. 
Keep in mind that you won’t be able to know which links to include unless you agree to the 
terms of the link building. However, my point still stands because you reserve the right to accept 
or reject the proposed links. 
More importantly, the link should be relevant to the content’s topic, if not your blog’s entire 
niche. 
As well as the last and most obvious fact… ​The money​. 
Depending on the metrics your blog has and the niche it is in, you can sell backlinks & posts on 
your site from $20 to $2,000 each! 
I understand that is a wild field, but so is the internet… People likely won’t pay you $500 for an 
article in the video games niche, but crypto or gambling? That’s still a fairly low rate for some 
sites... 
Now that you have good reasons to sell backlinks on your blog, you need to know who you’ll be 
dealing with and how credible they are. This will determine whether you’ll be making money on 
your blog by selling link placements or burning your years of hardwork into the ground. 
How To Vet Link Sellers? 
If it’s a singular site owner/affiliate owner reaching out, then you can skip the seller vetting and 
go straight to vetting the actual website you’ll be linking to. If you can have them agree on your 
price and show you their site first. 
As mentioned earlier though, link sellers will serve as the intermediary between their clients and 
your blog. If they can get a link from your blog pointing to their client sites, they (and you) will 
get paid for the trouble. 
However, this only takes place if the link seller is legitimate.  












How they construct their pitch should determine if their proposal is worth considering. 
The email of Fat Joe is a good example of a link building pitch because it contains everything 
you need to know about their offer. 
If you look back at the screencaps above, it tells you what you can expect from Fat Joe on its 
end. At the same time, it shares with you what you must do to get paid by publishing their article 
with backlinks to clients. 
From there, it tells you how much they’re willing to pay for every successful article published. 
Through its transparency, Fat Joe gives you more incentive to trust them. 
This is in stark contrast with the email sent by “Michael Hussey.” 
Aside from the ridiculous lowball offer, the email appears demanding, as if the recipient owes 
him anything. 
Also, phrases like “I need this site,” “please accept my offer,” and similar sounds like a person 
whose sole purpose is to get a link from your site and be done with you.  
To be fair, that ​is ​the objective of every link builder whenever they reach out to bloggers.  
However, a more concerted effort to keep bloggers up to speed as to what the deal is would be 
nice. 
In either case, I suggest that you consider emails similar to Fat Joe’s offer, which meets all the 
criteria of good outreach. For emails that read like Michael Hussey’s, run away as far as 
possible from it. 
How Much Should You Charge for a Backlink on Your Blog? 
Once you’ve narrowed down the list of link builders whom you want to work with, you need to 
know the most important question from this:  
How much will you be getting per published post/link? 
Obviously, you want to get as much as possible for each.  
At the same time, however, you need to temper your expectations. 
You can’t demand hundreds and thousands of dollars per link if your site doesn’t have the 
authority to command such a price. 
So how much is good enough for your site? 
Here’s a typical cost of a link from the blog depending on the niche. 








Therefore, to know how much you can earn per link from your blog, you must look at your site 
according to different variables​. 
As some overall examples though: 
If your blog has at least 1,000 visitors per month from Google, as well as DA (by Moz) and DR 
(by Ahrefs) metrics of 25 and above then there’s no reason you shouldn’t be able to charge at 
least $100 per blog post. 
How To Value Your Site The Right Way 
Niches, traffic, link authority, metrics and how Google’s algorithm has affected your website’s 
traffic numbers over time are the biggest factors people will look at when buying backlinks from 
your websites. 
By finding out these variables, you’ll have a clearer idea of how much you can charge per link 
on your blog and what other blogs are charging for. 
This way, you can veto offers that pay below the minimum amount that your blog is worth. 
At the same time, you will know how you can improve your blog’s authority and field more offers 
from link sellers in the future. 
Below are some of the most common ones: 
Organic Traffic 
The volume of traffic that Google and other search engines refer to your sites is considered 
organic traffic. 
This means users found your site on Google by typing in a search query.  
And if you have lots of organic traffic, your site is ranking for multiple queries or are ranking on 
top of search engine results pages (SERPs) for a handful of keywords with lots of monthly 
searches. 
These reasons are why link sellers are inclined to reach out to these sites.  
They want to leverage the traffic your blog gets and get more eyeballs and clicks on the links 
pointing to their client sites. 
More importantly, the fact that your blog is ranking on Google for multiple keywords means that 
the search engine loves your site, thus giving link sellers more incentive to reach out to you and 
secure a link.  
Once they get a backlink from your blog to their client sites, they can expect their clients to get a 
much-needed boost on their rankings as well! 
















If lots of people recommend your services, for example, you can expect more people to inquire 
about them based on the glowing reviews you received. 
At the same time, not all recommendations are the same.  
For instance, thousands of recommendations from people with no influence means nothing 
compared to a handful of positive reviews from influencers.  
Their recommendations can help you get more clients for your services - more than what 
thousands of people can say. 
The same concept applies to backlinks. 
The goal is to get backlinks from websites that Google deems authoritative. That means those 
that rank on organic search for multiple keywords on top of having a link profile from other 
authoritative sites. 
It’s easy to know which sites link back to a site. What’s not is organizing these backlinks and 
finding out which ones among them have the most value. 
In that case, we need to rely on tools that will help us determine the value of your blog in 
relation to its backlinks. 
To do this, you must know which metrics to use for measuring the quality of a blog’s link profile. 
Below are some more popular ones you can use. 
Note:​ While using all three metrics below is ideal, you don’t necessarily need to. Just two out of 
the three should be good enough since you just want to take a peek at how authoritative your 
site is according to these variables.  
Also, to view these metrics, you will need to subscribe to the tools, which are expensive for the 
purpose we want to achieve. 
Nonetheless, we’ll break down the different metrics to help you decide which among them you’ll 
use for evaluating your site’s backlinks. 
Domain Rating 
Assuming you’re already using Ahrefs, you need not look further than its Domain Rating. 
Also known as DR, Domain Rating is the aggregated score computed by Ahrefs regarding the 
strength of a site’s link profile. The higher the score, the more authoritative its backlinks are. 
You will first see the DR of a site from the Site Explorer overview after entering the URL of your 
domain. 














Niche Difficulty 
As seen earlier in one of the case studies, it’s possible to charge higher for a backlink if your 
blog is in a very competitive niche. 
For example, if you have a finance blog that has a DA/DR 40 and has moderately high traffic, 
you can charge much higher than $250-400 for it due to its highly competitive niche. 
Since backlinks help site owners tip the scales to their favor, ​authoritative​ and ​relevant​ links are 
few and far between for those in difficult niches (at least, for the price they’re willing to pay for). 
And since the niche dictates the pricing, you can hike up your prices and still get link sellers to 
agree to your rate! 
However, I must reiterate that you can only do this if your blog is covering a difficult niche. 
Therefore: 
We need to know what makes a niche “difficult.” 
There are different factors involved in defining a niche as such, and we’ll break them down 
below: 
Lots of Authoritative Competitors 
There are various ways of how bloggers settled on a niche. 
Some chose their topics based on their knowledge about and interest in it. Others decided on 
niches that had the most potential for monetization. 
While there’s no wrong answer as to how one comes up with a niche, it dictates how much 
money he or she makes for selling backlinks from the blog. 
In this case, for bloggers such as yourself, you must figure out how many authoritative 
competitors you are up against in your niche. 
To do this, you can use Ahrefs to identify: 
●
The competitiveness of the niche is general 
●
The competitors vying for the same keywords as you 
For the first one, go to Keyword Explorer and enter your blog topic.

---

title: Local Kingdom: The Big City
content:
Approaching, or being approached by a prospect, that wants Local SEO in a big 
city can be daunting. Even starting a lead generation asset can seem like a bad 
idea. 
Fear not, because there are ways to compete in massive cities, too. 
Let’s think of these big cities as having 1m+ population. These are super cities. 
We should not neglect super cities just because they are large. That would cut off 
a huge amount of potential clients and lead generation assets. 
Why Would We Target A Super City? 
Although there are loads of competitors in a super city - there is a lot of demand, 
too. Super cities give us the chance to compete for a lot more searches 
throughout the whole locale.  
If we execute our plan correctly, this means higher fees from clients and more 
leads for our lead generation assets.  
Also, even though there are more competitors, that doesn’t necessarily mean a 
higher quality of competitor​. 


Niches that generally have bad Local SEO (commodity service businesses) don’t 
suddenly become great because they exist in a big city... There are lots of 
competitors, but many still have a non-existent/weak Local SEO strategy.  
Sure, there are likely to be a few more big players because they have market 
share and can now spend on SEO... But still, in certain niches, there will be lots 
of competition, but not necessarily lots of good competition.  
This benefits client work AND lead generation assets. For both, we are working 
with higher populations (more demand) but with more supply (however, in our 
case, the supply is not created equally as many still won’t invest in SEO).  
This makes super cities pretty fruitful for the right niches and markets. This isn’t 
an ironclad rule. You still need to research and ensure the market is right to get 
results for a client or for your own lead generation asset! 
Another benefit for our lead generation assets in big cities is having lots of 
businesses to send our leads to. In small locations, everyone knows everyone 
and it might be weird if some random website whom no one knows is dominating 
Google. However, in 1m+ cities, there are endless businesses scattered all over 
Google. This allows us to fly under the radar and not come under fire from any 
angry locals. The main benefit, though, is being able to test lots of different lead 
generation partners and simply sticking with the companies who we like and 
actually convert our leads. 
It’s worth noting, I would still always opt for the very best lead generation asset 
opportunity rather than mindlessly picking a super city just because it is large...  
Whichever opportunity pays the quickest (low competition), the most (good 
niche) and will stand the test of time (evergreen niche) are what we want to base 
creating our next lead generation asset on.  
With all that said, let’s look at an example of a super city and how we effectively 
tackle it from all angles.  
 
 
 


Super Competitors 
In order to understand how to take as many eyeballs, clicks, leads and sales out 
of a big city, you need to understand the competitors you’re up against.  
Let’s use the super city of London. London has a population of about 9 million 
people. We used roofing as an easy example throughout Local Kingdom, so let’s 
use roofing again and assume we have a city central GMB and can operate 
throughout London.  
First, let’s use Ahrefs to see what the guys dominating the London SL keywords 
are currently ranking for. All I’ve done is put three main competitors into Site 
Explorer on Ahrefs and exported the list of keywords they get organic clicks for. 
Here they are: 
 




 
This already gives us a good idea of which keywords in the niche have search 
volume and how we should be optimizing our site. We can do this for all the 
different roofers in and around us who serve the same target areas as us. 
We can then look at their specific pages ranking for these keywords and assess 
how strong each of those are individually. Not only do we want to see the specific 
UR (the target page’s link profile strength), we also want to see how many pages 
internally link to that page (lots of in-depth blog articles?), how much content they 
have on the ranking page itself and so on.  
Slowly but surely, we can gather an overall picture for how the keywords are 
spread out across the niche/location, which look most fruitful and what kind of 
strategy each keyword requires to be dominated.  


Remember everything we learnt from the book? In order to beat competitors, we 
need to ​outperform​ them. 
So yes, this means building citations, links and everything else mentioned. 
However, getting to grips with all the keywords in the super city and what it will 
take to rank for each keyword will supercharge your campaign. Too many people 
jump into a project without having a good idea for what they’re getting into. 
Planning effectively will set you apart in SEO, I guarantee it. 
 
Missed Keywords 
Now let’s see where these main competitors are NOT ranking.  
Generally, in a niche like roofing, the domain with the highest DR is going to be 
dominating the SL keywords.  
This means these other random keywords they’ve missed are probably going to 
be dominated by weaker competitors.  
Their loss is our gain! We can do a big audit on all of the top guys and which 
keywords they’re missing, then devise a plan (outperform weak competitors and 
their specific pages) to take these keywords too!  
Let’s follow the instructions already provided in the keyword research section and 
use the Content Gap tool in Ahrefs. These are just some keywords the main 
competitor isn’t even ranking for. 


 
 
 
 


 
These two examples simply use the next 6 organic listings below one of the top 
competitors for Roofers London. They aren’t ranking for all these different 
keywords. This is supposedly one of the top companies!  
We could keep doing this for every listing in the area to find loads of opportunities 
and keywords the main players aren’t ranking for that have low keyword difficulty. 
Again, the benefit of these super cities is the sheer volume and diversity of 
searches! Understanding this, and planning accordingly is vital. To gain the 
benefits of these super cities, we want to rank for loads of keywords and appear 
in loads of map packs. 
For this reason, I really like a content heavy approach to have the most topical 
relevance in the niche/location and dominate all of these different searches. 
If we look at most of these top roofing competitors, they actually don’t have much 
content on their site. Not many are utilizing supporting pages or blog articles. 
They’re listing their services and stopping there.  
We would only need to completely outperform their link profile (many of the 
competitors have <20 DR), get all of the other basics right listed in Local 
Kingdom, and have really good topical relevance to start doing really well for the 
main keywords, as well as all the niche keywords the main competitors are 
missing. 
 
Go Niche 
To build on the point above, we can afford to go for more niche keywords in 
super cities. There are simply more searches for more things, meaning a content 
heavy approach makes sense to get in front of all of these varied keywords.  
If you aren’t talking about lead roofing repairs or gutter cleaning, how can you 
rank for those services?  
You can’t.  
 


So, plenty of pages and supporting content in these super cities makes a lot of 
sense. Obviously we don’t neglect any other part of the strategy, like dominating 
the competition through links, but it’s worth noting how important content 
coverage of all the different niche search terms is. And yes, we would still ideally 
take the SL keywords as they are the most plentiful, but a significant difference 
for huge locations is how many more niche searches there will be. 
 
As we know, Ahrefs and similar aren’t going to pick up every single keyword 
searched through Google. 
Something like garage roofing London has “10 monthly searches”. I’m certain this 
is not true. With one of my roofing lead generation sites, I get multiple garage 
repair/installation calls per week. That asset is in a far smaller city than London, 
too. So I would be very confident there are a lot of searches each month for 
garage roof replacement between all the different search term variables, 
especially if you ranked in the map pack AND organically.  
I’m not trying to dupe anyone into believing that there is search volume there that 
isn’t, but the likelihood that you’ll get 10 people searching for a variation of 
“garage door repair london” is extremely unlikely… Meaning people may search 
for their area, like “Greenwich” inside of London, but your GMB might show 
depending on the variables we’ve already covered in this course. 
My point is, don’t be disheartened if you think a big city niche has potential and 
then Google makes out it doesn’t… Because we’ve made hundreds of pounds 
each month from those exact sorts of situations. 
Dominate Multiple Locations  
We’ve spoken about it a lot in Local Kingdom, but going after smaller areas 
within and around your super city is going to be vital too. We pick up SL keyword 
clicks in these areas as well as all the other niche terms people are searching. 
Not to mention all the localized “near me” and “local” searches people are using.  
London is a prime example of having loads of small locations within the main city. 
It is made up of loads of local boroughs - all with their own SL keywords in each 
area. 


If our GMB is close enough and our entire website and Google set up is strong 
enough, our GMB might stretch into these other locations nearby to where we 
first verified our GMB from.  
In the big cities, it can be hard to get massive GMB coverage just because of the 
amount of competitors.  
However, there’s no stopping us in the organic listings appearing in loads of 
locations for loads of keywords.  
I like a city central verification because it makes it easier to rank our GMB in map 
packs all around us. If the GMB was verified over in West London, it would be 
extremely difficult to rank in the East side of London. However, when we’re in the 
middle, we have good proximity to loads of local boroughs all around us. This 
isn’t necessarily a steadfast rule. For different cities, different areas will be better 
- you need to figure out why and make the best decision.  
A lot of the time you’ll have a client in an imperfect location, or perhaps you have 
a friend with a property who will do an easy verification for you in a fringe area. If 
you can’t get the centre of a super city it’s not a huge problem because there 
should be a decent population around you no matter where you are within the 
super city.  
Still, we can push our location pages for nearby areas. 
It looks like the competitors we looked at earlier are doing some of this, but not 
massively extensively. Just like Local Kingdom describes, we can put more effort 
into locations close to us with good search volume and put less effort into 
locations further away with less search volume. We can then power up all of 
these location pages with our niche/location specific blog articles to create topical 
authority, strong internal linking and power everything up with good anchors. 
Although some competitors are trying to rank in nearby boroughs, many aren’t. 
ALL of our competitors are slipping up in some way between: 
 
- Niche specific content (most have very thin content) 
- Location dominance (most don’t have multiple location pages) 
- Link strategy (most have low-medium DRs) 


This means we should be able to smash the main competitors on the SL keyword 
front, then also capture loads of niche specific keywords the best competitors 
missed AND enter local markets our competitors also haven’t dominated as they 
should - hopefully with our GMB (proximity can run out eventually) but almost 
definitely with our website organically.  
For what it’s worth, I think there’s a good amount of opportunity in the example 
above. I didn’t have to scour through endless niches and locations to do this, 
either. This was the second example I looked at!  
This general approach for figuring out the opportunities in your niche work for 
cities with 100k - 1m populations, too. The first example I looked at was 
Glasgow, but then I realised it has a 600k population. That’s also a great niche 
that would be pretty easy to dominate lots of niche and location keywords. 
Get More City Coverage 
I have two lead generation assets sending leads to the same client in a large city 
in England. I adopted 50% of the site from a friend (he didn’t look after it and 
GoDaddy deleted his site. I got it back online and monetized the leads by 
sending them to my current client). My site was already dominating all of the 
main SL keywords, while his is doing particularly well for more niche searches. 
He has an absurd amount of reviews and content, so his GMB appears in a lot of 
map packs.  
We both have GMBs in the centre of the city. 
If I were doing this by design rather than by accident, I would be inclined to get 
each GMB as far away from each other in the city as possible. Or, if you’re 
targeting an even larger super city (3m+), you could consider adding a GMB to a 
large subsection of the city rather than verifying two GMBs in the main city 
centre. Obviously, it depends where you can get a GMB verified (for your client 
or your own lead generation asset) and where makes the most sense for your 
niche/location.  
Remember, don’t create two GMBs under the same brand in a city (unless 
15km+ away from each other), but you can create a lead generation asset under 
a different brand to generate leads alongside your client’s brand.  


Of course, ideally, your lead generation asset and the client site will be on 
seperate servers so we can link them together and earn an easy, niche and 
location relevant link. 
Also, for some niches you won’t want to start two lead generation assets in the 
same niche and location. It would probably make more sense to diversify your 
assets unless you’re certain it would be a good idea to have two assets in the 
same niche.  
If you have a great client in an easy niche, it could be worth creating a lead 
generation asset to go alongside the Local SEO work you’re doing on their site.  
You can even sell the concept to them.  
Instead of having one site to dominate all the keywords in your big city, you will 
create another entity (lead generation asset!) to corner more of the market! You 
keep the asset, but they get all the leads the asset produces. This technique 
gives value to the client, while allowing you to get paid to create a lead 
generation asset!  
Wrapping It Up 
That’s it for dominating big cities. The main takeaways are: 
- Super cities aren’t necessarily bad 
- Super cities have lots of demand  
- More supply doesn’t mean better supply

---

title: Local Kingdom: The Competitive Citadel
content:
This guide is meant to help you with localized niches that are competitive, and by 
competitive I mean they have other companies doing active SEO campaigns as 
well. 
Usually, these sort of “citadel niches” (A term that is exclusive to this book and 
not a generalized SEO term) are things like local real estate, exotic car hire, 
personal injury lawyers or accountancy firms… Expensive, but high demand 
companies that will normally have more than one company servicing an area. 
Doing a local lead generation site for a citadel niche is more like an affiliate site 
you’re going to invest into.  
As we’re tackling a high keyword difficulty niche full of websites that have or have 
had SEO campaigns, we can’t get away with writing basic content, acquiring a 
few links for free and dominating. No, we’ll need budget, processes and if you 
want to go after multiple citadel niches, then you’ll likely need a team to 
dominate. You could do it all yourself but it would certainly consume a lot of your 
time.  
 
 


Tackling The Competition Head First 
As we’ve described throughout Local Kingdom, tackling the competitors head-on 
requires us to outperform their entire strategy to assure beating them in the 
SERPs. 
Aged Domains 
Ideally, you’ll want a site or domain that has some age and isn’t a fresh 
registration.  
If you can find companies that have gone out or are going out of business, 
picking up their domains is your golden ticket. 
That is rare though, and normally you can just pick up niche domains that aren’t 
localized to the area but are relevant to the topic you’re trying to target. 
I recommend using ​Odys Global​ or an auction service to find these sorts of 
domains, though fresh domains are fine if you have the 6 - 12 months to build 
them up. 
Local PBNs 
Covering PBNs is literally another course in itself, and you can check that out 
here: ​Safe & Strong​. Which includes a bonus module specifically on local PBNs. 
If you can build a localized private blog network of domains all targeted around a 
specific area, you should theoretically be able to rank ANYTHING in that area, 
with the right OnPage skills as well of course. 
Niche Edits 
Niche edits are a posh SEO word for inserting links into existing pages, or simply 
“link insertions”. 
They’re way cheaper than guest posts and as long as you’re diversifying your link 
profile, you can get utilize 5 - 10 into your link building strategy and save 
hundreds of dollars on guest posts. 
I recommend using either SERPWolf for cheaper edits or Undrcut Digital for 
higher-end edits. 


Topical Authority 
The OnPage fundamentals given in Local Kingdom work for easy niches and 
hard niches. But take a website like ​https://www.slatergordon.co.uk/​.  
 
They are a leading attorney website in the UK. They’re a good example because 
not only do they dominate large cities like London and Manchester, but they 
actually get into most of the other largest English cities 
Start going through the pages on their site. You will realize the depth of their 
pages, the content on each page and the internal linking strategy between the 
pages. Then they have all their city-specific pages and GMBs set up too.  
 


When you compare Slater and Gordon to other attorney websites, you start to 
see how much topical authority they have around the whole subject of law. Each 
page has its own silo of pages and supporting content. It’s impressive. The 
content is also pretty useful, answering your questions and steering you into 
more specific problems and solutions you might need. 
In order to rank for so many keywords in so many locations you need to be 
viewed as an authority by Google - excellent in-depth content, as displayed by 
Slater and Gordon, is a definite way to achieve this.  
You can check out ​Charles guide on topical SEO here​. 
Corner Off The Market With Longtails 
Rather than tackling our competitors headfirst and competing with the very 
hardest keywords they rank for in a location, we can section off portions of the 
niche to rank for instead. 
Even these massively authoritative sites like Slater and Gordon can’t capture the 
entire legal market. Sure, they have a majority of the most profitable keywords, 
but that doesn’t mean the other keywords they don’t dominate aren’t valuable. No 
single legal site is going to dominate every single legal keyword in every city. 
There are an abundance of opportunities to corner off a small section of a 
profitable market, like legal.  
Importantly, you’ll need a niche like legal, that has so many niches within it. Take 
another highly competitive industry to rank in: web design. The keyword difficulty 
to rank for web design keywords in Citadel Cities is usually pretty high, but there 
isn’t the breadth of services to rank for like there is in legal.  
Less services and keywords to rank for means less keywords to rank for between 
all of the competitors in the niche. 
Look at the solicitor GMB we rank in the same map pack as Slater and Gordon. 


 
 
With over 300 phone calls directed to a legal firm in that area, this GMB has been 
pretty successful… Especially considering how few hours went into creating it. 
The website barely ranks in organic (was just created to bolster the GMB) and 
most of the calls from the GMB have come from a small handful of niche specific 
SL keywords. 
As we can see from the SERPs for this main keyword my GMB calls come 
through, the highest ranking site actually has the lowest DR in the niche.  


 
Under further investigation, this site is really locally relevant. The company only 
operates in this city, but they do it comprehensively. They have 6 offices just 
within the city! All of these offices have their own location pages. Meanwhile, 
most of the competitors barely have an office in Manchester, let alone 6. 
This will give the company endless localized optimizations they can make. While 
the competitors simply throw up a /service-solicitors-location page, this company 
has: 


- Multiple local GMBs 
- Multiple local directory citations per office 
- An entire site focused on this one city  
 
You probably wouldn’t normally need as much hyper-relevance as this business 
has, but it would certainly see you dominate the SERPs if your competitors don’t 
have much local relevancy.  
It’s also worth noting - this company isn’t even specific to this particular niche 
because they offer most of the other core legal services. However, they still 
ranked here through sheer local relevance, not even including channelling loads 
of niche relevant authority. 
In knowing we can beat competitors through channelling location and or niche 
relevance, it opens up opportunities for us to compete in subsections of 
competitive Citadel cities. Being in highly competitive and highly profitable niches 
without having to create a vastly powerful DR from acquiring loads of links is a 
great tool for our arsenal.  
Overall, it’s not as difficult as affiliate SEO, nor has nearly as many high-level 
SEOs competing in these citadel niches… Normally just Junior SEOs at your 
local agency doing most of the heavy lifting.

---
